Tell me, my daughters—since we now strip ourselves of power, lands, and the cares of the crown—which of you shall we say loves us the most? We wish to deal our greatest generosity where natural affection and merit align. Isabella, our firstborn, speak first.
ISABELLA. Sire, I love you beyond what mere words can measure; more dearly than my own sight, the open air, or my freedom. Beyond all treasures, however rare or costly; as much as life itself, matched with health, grace, and honor. As deeply as any child has ever loved, or any father has ever been loved. A devotion that renders my breath short and my tongue tied; beyond all limits of comparison, I love you.
DIANA. [Aside.] What shall Diana say? Love, and keep silent.
LEONTES. Of all these borders, from this river to this valley, rich with dark woods and fertile plains, with abundant streams and sprawling pastures, we make you sovereign. To your and Milan's heirs, let this be everlasting. What says our second daughter, our beloved Beatrice, wife of Venice? Speak.
BEATRICE. Sire, I am forged of the exact same steel as my sister, and value myself at her equal. In my honest heart, I find she has spoken the very shape of my love; only she falls short, in that I declare myself a sworn enemy to every other earthly joy that the senses can perceive, and find my only true happiness in your royal affection.
DIANA. [Aside.] Then poor Diana. And yet, not truly poor, for my love is far heavier than my hollow words.
LEONTES. To you and your heirs forever we bequeath this wealthy third of our grand realm; no less in scope, revenue, and beauty than what was granted to Isabella. Now, our final joy, though the youngest and smallest; for whose tender affections the vineyards of Aragon and the gold of Naples bitterly compete. What can you say to claim a third more magnificent than your sisters'? Speak.
DIANA. Nothing, my lord.
LEONTES. Nothing?
DIANA. Nothing.
LEONTES. Nothing will be born of nothing. Speak again.
DIANA. Wretched as I am, I cannot drag my heart into my mouth. I love your majesty according to my duty; no more, and no less.
LEONTES. How now, Diana? Fix your speech a little, lest you ruin your own fortune.
DIANA. My good liege, you fathered me, raised me, and loved me. I return those obligations exactly as is proper: I obey you, I love you, and I honor you above all. Why do my sisters have husbands if they claim they love only you? Perhaps, when I take a husband, that lord who accepts my vows shall take half my love with him, and half my care and duty. I am certain I shall never wed like my sisters, to give my father all my love.
LEONTES. But does your heart truly agree with this?
DIANA. It does, my good lord.
LEONTES. So young, and so hard?
DIANA. So young, my lord, and truthful.

VALERIUS: Do you smell the scandal?
CASSIUS: Can't say I'd wish it undone. The result is just too handsome.
VALERIUS: The funny thing is, I have a legitimate boy. Older by a year. But I don't love him any more than this one. Sure, the rogue crashed the party before the priest gave his blessing, but his mother was a beauty. We had a magnificent time making him. The bastard has to be acknowledged. Have you met the young man? Marcus!
MARCUS: No, my lord. I haven't had the pleasure.
VALERIUS: Cassius, consider him a good friend from now on.
MARCUS: An honor, sir.
CASSIUS: The pleasure is mine. I hope we get to know each other.
MARCUS: I'll do my best to earn it.
VALERIUS: He’s been away nine years. He’ll be leaving again soon. Shh—the King.
(Trumpets sound. King Leontes enters with the Dukes of Milan and Venice, his daughters Isabella, Beatrice, and Diana, and various attendants.)
LEONTES: Valerius. Go fetch the ambassadors from Aragon and Naples.
VALERIUS: At once, your grace. (Valerius and Marcus exit.)
LEONTES: In the meantime, let's lay out our darker intentions. Bring the map over here. Right. We’ve carved the empire into three. It's our firm plan to strip the heavy lifting off our shoulders and drop it onto younger backs. Let us crawl toward the grave without the baggage. Milan... Venice... today we're making our daughters' dowries public, hoping to kill any future squabbles before they start.
Aragon and Naples have been hovering around the court for months, fighting over our youngest. They want an answer today. But first: daughters. Since I am officially stripping myself of the crown, the land, and the headaches of state—which of you loves me the most? Let's see where natural affection meets actual merit. Isabella. You’re the eldest. Go.
ISABELLA: Father. I love you more than words can actually measure. More than my own eyesight. More than the open air. Beyond any treasure you could name—as much as life itself. I love you with a devotion that makes my breath catch and my words fail. Beyond any limit, I love you.
DIANA: (Aside) What am I supposed to say to that? Just love him, and keep my mouth shut.
LEONTES: Take a look. From this river to the valley—the dark woods, the plains, the pastures. You are sovereign here. To you and Milan’s heirs, forever. Now. What about our second daughter? Beatrice. Speak.
BEATRICE: I’m cut from the exact same cloth as my sister, sire. In my heart, she just spoke my own truth. Except she didn't go far enough. I consider myself a sworn enemy to every other earthly joy. I find my only actual happiness in your love.
DIANA: (Aside) Poor Diana. No, not poor. My love is just heavier than my words.
LEONTES: To you and your bloodline, we leave this wealthy third of the realm. Equal in scope and revenue to Isabella’s. And now. Our final joy. The youngest, but certainly not the least. The prize Aragon and Naples are currently ripping each other apart over. What can you say to claim a slice richer than your sisters'? Go on.
DIANA: Nothing, my lord.
LEONTES: Nothing?
DIANA: Nothing.
LEONTES: Nothing comes from nothing. Try again.
DIANA: I'm sorry, I just can't drag my heart into my mouth. I love you exactly as a daughter should. No more, no less.
LEONTES: Watch yourself, Diana. Fix your tone before you ruin your own life.
DIANA: You fathered me. You raised me, and you loved me. I'm returning that exact debt. I obey you, and I honor you. Why do my sisters even have husbands if they claim they love only you? When I marry, the man who takes my vows is going to take half my love with him. Half my care. I will never marry like my sisters—keeping all my love for my father.
LEONTES: Is this really where your heart is?
DIANA: It is.
LEONTES: So young, and so cold?
DIANA: Young, my lord. And honest.

She commands Prudence to be ever on the watch to discover how Pleasure may rule, and be safe. Justice she orders to grant what benefits she can, in order to secure those friendships which are necessary for bodily pleasure; to do wrong to no one, lest, on account of the breaking of the laws, Pleasure be not able to live in security. Fortitude she orders to keep her mistress, that is, Pleasure, bravely in her mind, if any affliction befall her body which does not occasion death, in order that by remembrance of former delights she may mitigate the poignancy of present pain. Temperance she commands to take only a certain quantity even of the most favourite food, lest, through immoderate use, anything prove hurtful by disturbing the health of the body, and thus Pleasure, which the Epicureans make to consist chiefly in the health of the body, be grievously offended. Thus the virtues, with the whole dignity of their glory, will be the slaves of Pleasure, as of some imperious and disreputable woman. There is nothing, say our philosophers, more disgraceful and monstrous than this picture, and which the eyes of good men can less endure. And they say the truth. But I do not think that the picture would be sufficiently becoming, even if it were made so that the virtues should be represented as the slaves of human glory; for, though that glory be not a luxurious woman, it is nevertheless puffed up, and has much vanity in it. Wherefore it is unworthy of the solidity and firmness of the virtues to represent them as serving this glory, so that Prudence shall provide nothing, Justice distribute nothing, Temperance moderate nothing, except to the end that men may be pleased and vainglory served. Nor will they be able to defend themselves from the charge of such baseness, whilst they, by way of being despisers of glory, disregard the judgment of other men, seem to themselves wise, and please themselves. For their virtue,--if, indeed, it is virtue at all,--is only in another way subjected to human praise; for he who seeks to please himself seeks still to please man. But he who, with true piety towards God, whom he loves, believes, and hopes in, fixes his attention more on those things in which he displeases himself, than on those things, if there are any such, which please himself, or rather, not himself, but the truth, does not attribute that by which he can now please the truth to anything but to the mercy of Him whom he has feared to displease, giving thanks for what in him is healed, and pouring out prayers for the healing of that which is yet unhealed. 21. _That the Roman dominion was granted by Him from whom is all power, and by whose providence all things are ruled._  These things being so, we do not attribute the power of giving kingdoms and empires to any save to the true God, who gives happiness in the kingdom of heaven to the pious alone, but gives kingly power on earth both to the pious and the impious, as it may please Him, whose good pleasure is always just. For though we have said something about the principles which guide His administration, in so far as it has seemed good to Him to explain it, nevertheless it is too much for us, and far surpasses our strength, to discuss the hidden things of men's hearts, and by a clear examination to determine the merits of various kingdoms. He, therefore, who is the one true God, who never leaves the human race without just judgment and help, gave a kingdom to the Romans when He would, and as great as He would, as He did also to the Assyrians, and even the Persians, by whom, as their own books testify, only two gods are worshipped, the one good and the other evil,--to say nothing concerning the Hebrew people, of whom I have already spoken as much as seemed necessary, who, as long as they were a kingdom, worshipped none save the true God. The same, therefore, who gave to the Persians harvests, though they did not worship the goddess Segetia, who gave the other blessings of the earth, though they did not worship the many gods which the Romans supposed to preside, each one over some particular thing, or even many of them over each several thing,--He, I say, gave the Persians dominion, though they worshipped none of those gods to whom the Romans believed themselves indebted for the empire.

She commands Prudence to be constantly vigilant in calculating future yields, ensuring that Wealth may increase and remain secure. Justice she directs to settle all debts and observe contracts strictly, not from a love of fairness, but to avoid the penalties of the magistrate and to secure the commercial alliances necessary for acquiring more gold. Fortitude she orders to endure the bitterest colds and the most exhausting labors, keeping the mind firmly fixed on the promise of future profits, so that the pain of present toil is swallowed up by the anticipation of a full treasury. Temperance she restricts to a frugal diet and modest apparel, lest excessive spending diminish the hoard, and thus Wealth, which the avaricious elevate as the highest good, be tragically diminished. Thus the noble virtues, stripped of their inherent majesty, are reduced to the grovelling servants of Wealth, acting as the slaves of a cruel and barren master. There is nothing, our scholars argue, more degraded and perverse than this arrangement, nor anything that right-minded men should find more repulsive. And they are entirely correct. But I do not believe this hierarchy would be any more acceptable even if the virtues were made subservient to Intellectual Prestige; for although that prestige is not a crass material obsession, it is nevertheless bloated with arrogance and overflowing with vanity. Therefore, it is beneath the eternal dignity of the virtues to be yoked to academic pride, wherein Prudence learns nothing, Justice balances nothing, and Temperance restrains nothing, except to ensure that a man is lauded by his peers and his own ego is inflated. Nor can such men acquit themselves of hypocrisy when they, claiming to despise the applause of the crowd, retreat inward to admire their own intellects, feeling entirely satisfied with themselves. For this supposed virtue—if it can be called virtue at all—is merely another form of human flattery; he who seeks to impress himself is still bowing to a man. Conversely, the man of genuine piety, whose faith and love are anchored in God, focuses his scrutiny on his own spiritual poverty, finding displeasure in himself so that he might find pleasure in the truth. He attributes whatever goodness he possesses not to his own intellect, but to the mercy of Him whom he reverences, offering gratitude for the afflictions that have been healed, and ceaselessly praying for the purification of those that remain unhealed.
	•	That worldly prosperity is distributed by Him who holds all authority, and by whose divine plan the universe is governed.
This being the case, we do not ascribe the accumulation of national wealth and the flourishing of empires to anyone other than the true God. He reserves the eternal treasures of heaven exclusively for the righteous, yet He distributes earthly riches to both the righteous and the wicked, according to His own will, which is always perfectly just. For although we may apprehend fragments of the design by which He governs the world, whenever He chooses to reveal them, it is ultimately beyond our finite capacity to dissect the secret motives of human hearts and perfectly measure why one kingdom prospers while another falls into ruin. Therefore, the one true God, who never abandons humanity to absolute chaos but provides just governance, granted immense riches and maritime supremacy to the Carthaginians when He saw fit, just as He did to the Greeks, and even to the Egyptians, who, as their own monuments demonstrate, worshipped beasts and rivers—to say nothing of the Hebrew people, whom I have already discussed, who worshipped only the true God during their time of sovereignty. The same God, therefore, who gave the Egyptians overflowing harvests, though they did not offer sacrifices to the goddess Ceres, and who bestowed the treasures of the earth, though they did not venerate the many spirits which the Romans believed to preside over the soil—He, I affirm, granted them economic dominion, despite their absolute ignorance of the very deities to whom other nations foolishly attributed their prosperity.

Picture Avarice giving orders to the virtues. She tells Prudence to stay awake at night, obsessively calculating market shifts just to keep the vault secure. She tells Justice to pay debts and honor contracts—not out of any real sense of fairness, but simply to dodge the magistrate and keep the trade routes open. Then there is Fortitude. Avarice orders Fortitude to suffer through freezing winters and exhausting labor, using the sheer promise of future profits to numb the pain of the present work. And Temperance? She is commanded to eat scraps and wear threadbare coats, terrified that a single lavish meal might shrink the hoard.
In this grotesque scenario, the virtues are stripped of their majesty. They become nothing more than accountants and bodyguards for Wealth. It’s a degrading picture. Scholars rightly call it perverse, something any decent person should find repulsive.
But honestly, it isn't much better if we swap out Wealth for Intellectual Prestige. Sure, academic pride isn't a crass material obsession, but it’s still bloated with arrogance. It is entirely beneath the dignity of the virtues to serve as handmaids to vanity. Why should Prudence study, or Temperance practice restraint, if the only goal is to win an argument or stroke one's own ego? Even the introverted scholars who claim to despise public applause aren't immune. They retreat to their studies to admire their own minds. But flattering yourself is still flattering a human being.
True piety looks entirely different. A person anchored in God looks inward and sees their own poverty. They aren't trying to please themselves; they want to align with the truth. Whatever goodness they find in their character, they credit to God's grace, not their own brilliance. They give thanks for the parts of their soul that have been healed, and they pray over the broken parts that remain.
Worldly prosperity comes only from the true authority that governs the universe.
Because of this, we cannot credit the rise of wealthy empires to anyone but the true God. He saves the actual treasures of heaven for the righteous, but He hands out earthly money and power to both the good and the wicked. He does this exactly as He pleases, and His reasons are always just, even when they baffle us. We might catch glimpses of His grand design here and there. But overall? It is completely beyond our mental capacity to untangle the hidden motives in human hearts and figure out exactly why one nation gets rich while another goes bankrupt.
The one true God never abandons the world to chaos. He gave immense wealth and maritime dominance to the Carthaginians when He felt like it. He did the same for the Greeks and the Egyptians, even though the Egyptians worshipped crocodiles and the Nile. (This leaves aside the Israelites, who actually worshipped the true God while they had their kingdom). God gave the Egyptians bumper crops, even though they never bothered to pray to Ceres. He gave them gold from the earth, even though they didn't worship the spirits of the soil. He handed them absolute economic dominance, regardless of the fact that they completely ignored the false gods other nations insisted were responsible for such blessings.

and so she asked the two Harringtons to come: but Harriet was ill, and so Pen was forced to come by herself; and then, what do you think we did? We dressed up Chamberlayne in woman’s clothes, on purpose to pass for a lady,--only think what fun! Not a soul knew of it, but Colonel and Mrs. Forster, and Kitty and me, except my aunt, for we were forced to borrow one of her gowns; and you cannot imagine how well he looked! When Denny, and Wickham, and Pratt, and two or three more of the men came in, they did not know him in the least. Lord! how I laughed! and so did Mrs. Forster. I thought I should have died. And _that_ made the men suspect something, and then they soon found out what was the matter.”  With such kind of histories of their parties and good jokes did Lydia, assisted by Kitty’s hints and additions, endeavour to amuse her companions all the way to Longbourn. Elizabeth listened as little as she could, but there was no escaping the frequent mention of Wickham’s name. Their reception at home was most kind. Mrs. Bennet rejoiced to see Jane in undiminished beauty; and more than once during dinner did Mr. Bennet say voluntarily to Elizabeth,----  “I am glad you are come back, Lizzy.”  Their party in the dining-room was large, for almost all the Lucases came to meet Maria and hear the news; and various were the subjects which occupied them: Lady Lucas was inquiring of Maria, across the table, after the welfare and poultry of her eldest daughter; Mrs. Bennet was doubly engaged, on one hand collecting an account of the present fashions from Jane, who sat some way below her, and on the other, retailing them all to the younger Miss Lucases; and Lydia, in a voice rather louder than any other person’s, was enumerating the various pleasures of the morning to anybody who would hear her. “Oh, Mary,” said she, “I wish you had gone with us, for we had such fun! as we went along Kitty and me drew up all the blinds, and pretended there was nobody in the coach; and I should have gone so all the way, if Kitty had not been sick; and when we got to the George, I do think we behaved very handsomely, for we treated the other three with the nicest cold luncheon in the world, and if you would have gone, we would have treated you too. And then when we came away it was such fun! I thought we never should have got into the coach. I was ready to die of laughter. And then we were so merry all the way home! we talked and laughed so loud, that anybody might have heard us ten miles off!”  To this, Mary very gravely replied, “Far be it from me, my dear sister, to depreciate such pleasures. They would doubtless be congenial with the generality of female minds. But I confess they would have no charms for _me_. I should infinitely prefer a book.”  But of this answer Lydia heard not a word. She seldom listened to anybody for more than half a minute, and never attended to Mary at all. In the afternoon Lydia was urgent with the rest of the girls to walk to Meryton, and see how everybody went on; but Elizabeth steadily opposed the scheme. It should not be said, that the Miss Bennets could not be at home half a day before they were in pursuit of the officers. There was another reason, too, for her opposition. She dreaded seeing Wickham again, and was resolved to avoid it as long as possible. The comfort to _her_, of the regiment’s approaching removal, was indeed beyond expression. In a fortnight they were to go, and once gone, she hoped there could be nothing more to plague her on his account. She had not been many hours at home, before she found that the Brighton scheme, of which Lydia had given them a hint at the inn, was under frequent discussion between her parents. Elizabeth saw directly that her father had not the smallest intention of yielding; but his answers were at the same time so vague and equivocal, that her mother, though often disheartened, had never yet despaired of succeeding at last. [Illustration]  Elizabeth’s impatience to acquaint Jane with what had happened could no longer be overcome; and at length resolving to suppress every particular in which her sister was concerned, and preparing her to be surprised, she related to her the next morning the chief of the scene between Mr. Darcy and herself.

“…and so she invited the two Woodwards to attend: but Amelia was unwell, and so Charlotte was compelled to arrive alone; and then, what do you suppose we contrived? We adorned young Mr. Townsend in a velvet bonnet and silk pelisse, expressly to present him as a visiting dowager,—only imagine the amusement! Not a living soul was privy to the jest but Captain and Mrs. Lennox, and Florence and myself, except my aunt, for we were obliged to utilize her wardrobe; and you cannot conceive how elegant he appeared! When Lieutenant Davies, and Carter, and Smith, and a few others walked in, they were entirely deceived. Heavens! How I giggled! And so did Mrs. Lennox. I was certain I should expire from laughing. And that caused the gentlemen to suspect a trick, and they presently unraveled the mystery.”
With such accounts of their lively gatherings and pleasantries did Florence, aided by the prompting of her younger sister, occupy her companions all the way to Willowbourne. Eleanor tuned them out as best she could, but there was no escaping the frequent utterance of Carter’s name. Their welcome at the manor was exceptionally warm. Mrs. Crawford rejoiced to see Jane looking as radiant as ever; and more than once during the meal did Mr. Crawford remark to Eleanor,——
“I am exceedingly glad you have returned, Eleanor.”
Their gathering in the dining-room was considerable, for nearly all the neighboring Winslows came to greet the travelers and hear the tidings; and various were the topics that engaged them: Lady Winslow was inquiring of Jane, across the table, regarding the health and gardens of her married sister; Mrs. Crawford was doubly occupied, on one side gathering a report of the latest metropolitan fashions from Jane, and on the other, relaying them to the younger Miss Winslows; and Florence, in a tone far more piercing than anyone else’s, was detailing the morning's exploits to whomever would listen.
“Oh, Beatrice,” said she, “I do wish you had accompanied us, for it was such sport! As we rode along we drew all the curtains tight, and pretended the carriage was entirely vacant; and I would have kept it so the entire journey, if my sister had not felt faint; and when we stopped at the Red Lion, I believe we acted very generously, for we treated the others to the most delightful pastries imaginable, and had you been there, we should have treated you as well. And leaving the inn was just as riotous! I thought we should never manage to climb back into the carriage. I was overcome with merriment. And then we were so boisterous the whole way home! We chattered and laughed with such volume that the whole county might have heard us!”
To this, Beatrice very solemnly answered, “Far be it from me, my dear sister, to disparage such amusements. They are undoubtedly suited to the general disposition of youthful minds. But I must admit they hold no attraction for me. I should vastly prefer the quiet study of a volume.”
But Florence did not register a single word of this rebuttal. She rarely paid attention to anyone for more than a moment, and never heeded Beatrice under any circumstance. In the afternoon Florence was insistent that the young ladies should stroll down to the village, and observe what the townsfolk were about; but Eleanor firmly resisted the proposal. It must not be rumored that the Crawford sisters could not abide at home for half a day before seeking out the militia officers. There was an additional motive, too, for her refusal. She deeply dreaded crossing paths with Carter again, and was determined to evade him for as long as she could. The relief she felt at the regiment’s impending departure was beyond description. In less than a month they would march away, and once they were gone, she prayed there would be no further distress on his account. She had not been situated at home for very long before she perceived that the seaside scheme, which Florence had alluded to at the inn, was the subject of constant debate between her parents. Eleanor recognized instantly that her father had no intention of conceding; but his responses were simultaneously so evasive and ambiguous, that her mother, though frequently frustrated, never abandoned the hope of ultimate triumph.
CHAPTER XX.
Eleanor’s desperation to inform Jane of the remarkable events could no longer be contained; and finally deciding to omit every detail in which her sister was implicated, and warning her to expect a shock, she recounted the following morning the bulk of the encounter between Mr. Sterling and herself.

"So we invited the Woodward girls, but Amelia was sick, which meant Charlotte had to come by herself. And then—oh, you'll never guess what we did! We dressed young Townsend up in a velvet bonnet and a silk pelisse, just to see if we could pass him off as a visiting dowager. It was the funniest thing you can imagine. Nobody knew a thing about it except Captain Lennox, his wife, me, and Florence. We had to borrow my aunt’s clothes, you see, but he looked surprisingly elegant! When Carter and the other lieutenants finally walked in, they were totally fooled. I thought I was going to die laughing. Mrs. Lennox was giggling so hard she gave the whole thing away, and the men finally figured it out."
Chattering about these pranks—with Florence occasionally chiming in to exaggerate a detail—kept the carriage noisy all the way back to Willowbourne. Eleanor tried her best to tune them out. Unfortunately, it was impossible to ignore how often Carter’s name came up.
At least the welcome home was warm. Mrs. Crawford loudly admired Jane’s radiant complexion, while Mr. Crawford quietly caught Eleanor’s eye during dinner and muttered, "Glad you're back, Eleanor."
The dining room was packed. The Winslow family had trooped over to hear all the gossip, creating a chaotic web of overlapping conversations. Across the table, Lady Winslow interrogated Jane about her married sister’s vegetable gardens. At the head of the table, Mrs. Crawford was frantically multitasking: pumping Jane for details on the latest city fashions while simultaneously broadcasting those same details to the younger Winslow girls.
Above it all rose Florence’s piercing voice.
"You really should have come, Beatrice," Florence practically shouted. "We had the best time! We pulled all the carriage blinds down on the way there and pretended the coach was empty. I would've kept them down the whole trip if Charlotte hadn't started feeling car-sick. Oh, and when we stopped at the Red Lion, we were so generous! We bought everyone the most amazing pastries. If you'd been there, we would have bought you one too. By the time we left, I was laughing so hard I could barely climb back into the carriage. We were so loud on the way home, half the county must have heard us!"
Beatrice looked up from her plate with intense solemnity. "I wouldn't dream of criticizing your hobbies, Florence. I am sure they are perfectly suited to the average mind. But they hold absolutely no appeal for me. I would rather read a book."
Florence didn't hear a word of it. She rarely listened to anyone for more than ten seconds, and she completely ignored Beatrice as a rule.
Later that afternoon, Florence begged the girls to walk down to the village to see what the soldiers were doing. Eleanor shut the idea down immediately. She wasn't about to let the neighbors say the Crawford girls couldn't be home for six hours before chasing after the militia. Plus, she was terrified of running into Carter. She planned to avoid him entirely. The thought of his regiment leaving next month was the only thing keeping her sane; once they marched out of town, she hoped she would never have to worry about him again.
Before the evening was out, Eleanor realized her mother’s grandiose plan to vacation at the seaside—which Florence had blabbed about earlier—was currently the main battlefield between her parents. It was obvious her father wasn't going to spend the money, but he was dodging the question so skillfully that Mrs. Crawford still thought she might win.
CHAPTER XX.
By the next morning, Eleanor couldn't keep her secret a second longer. She decided to filter out anything that might hurt Jane’s feelings, warned her sister to brace herself, and finally spilled the entire story of her explosive confrontation with Mr. Sterling.

As well might those tablets stand in the cave of Elephanta as here. In what census of living creatures, the dead of mankind are included; why it is that a universal proverb says of them, that they tell no tales, though containing more secrets than the Goodwin Sands; how it is that to his name who yesterday departed for the other world, we prefix so significant and infidel a word, and yet do not thus entitle him, if he but embarks for the remotest Indies of this living earth; why the Life Insurance Companies pay death-forfeitures upon immortals; in what eternal, unstirring paralysis, and deadly, hopeless trance, yet lies antique Adam who died sixty round centuries ago; how it is that we still refuse to be comforted for those who we nevertheless maintain are dwelling in unspeakable bliss; why all the living so strive to hush all the dead; wherefore but the rumor of a knocking in a tomb will terrify a whole city. All these things are not without their meanings. But Faith, like a jackal, feeds among the tombs, and even from these dead doubts she gathers her most vital hope. It needs scarcely to be told, with what feelings, on the eve of a Nantucket voyage, I regarded those marble tablets, and by the murky light of that darkened, doleful day read the fate of the whalemen who had gone before me. Yes, Ishmael, the same fate may be thine. But somehow I grew merry again. Delightful inducements to embark, fine chance for promotion, it seems—aye, a stove boat will make me an immortal by brevet. Yes, there is death in this business of whaling—a speechlessly quick chaotic bundling of a man into Eternity. But what then? Methinks we have hugely mistaken this matter of Life and Death. Methinks that what they call my shadow here on earth is my true substance. Methinks that in looking at things spiritual, we are too much like oysters observing the sun through the water, and thinking that thick water the thinnest of air. Methinks my body is but the lees of my better being. In fact take my body who will, take it I say, it is not me. And therefore three cheers for Nantucket; and come a stove boat and stove body when they will, for stave my soul, Jove himself cannot. CHAPTER 8. The Pulpit. I had not been seated very long ere a man of a certain venerable robustness entered; immediately as the storm-pelted door flew back upon admitting him, a quick regardful eyeing of him by all the congregation, sufficiently attested that this fine old man was the chaplain. Yes, it was the famous Father Mapple, so called by the whalemen, among whom he was a very great favourite. He had been a sailor and a harpooneer in his youth, but for many years past had dedicated his life to the ministry. At the time I now write of, Father Mapple was in the hardy winter of a healthy old age; that sort of old age which seems merging into a second flowering youth, for among all the fissures of his wrinkles, there shone certain mild gleams of a newly developing bloom—the spring verdure peeping forth even beneath February’s snow. No one having previously heard his history, could for the first time behold Father Mapple without the utmost interest, because there were certain engrafted clerical peculiarities about him, imputable to that adventurous maritime life he had led. When he entered I observed that he carried no umbrella, and certainly had not come in his carriage, for his tarpaulin hat ran down with melting sleet, and his great pilot cloth jacket seemed almost to drag him to the floor with the weight of the water it had absorbed. However, hat and coat and overshoes were one by one removed, and hung up in a little space in an adjacent corner; when, arrayed in a decent suit, he quietly approached the pulpit. Like most old fashioned pulpits, it was a very lofty one, and since a regular stairs to such a height would, by its long angle with the floor, seriously contract the already small area of the chapel, the architect, it seemed, had acted upon the hint of Father Mapple, and finished the pulpit without a stairs, substituting a perpendicular side ladder, like those used in mounting a ship from a boat at sea. The wife of a whaling captain had provided the chapel with a handsome pair of red worsted man-ropes for this ladder, which, being itself nicely headed, and stained with a mahogany colour, the whole contrivance, considering what manner of chapel it was, seemed by no means in bad taste.

As fittingly might these stone markers rest in the catacombs of Rome as here. In what register of creation the lost sailors of the world are counted; why it is that a common adage warns that the sea keeps her secrets, though she holds more treasures than the vaults of emperors; how it is that we don the darkest weeds of mourning for a friend who departed but yesterday, yet wear bright colors if he merely sets sail for the distant orient; why we weep with such bitter despair for those whom we vehemently declare are walking the golden streets of paradise; wherefore a sudden shadow in the water will strike terror into the stoutest heart. All these contradictions carry their own weight. Yet Belief, like a scavenger bird, circles the wrecks, and even from these drowned despairs she pulls her most sustaining nourishment. It requires little imagination to guess with what sentiments, on the night before my departure for the Arctic fisheries, I viewed those granite monuments, and by the dim glow of that weeping, dismal afternoon read the doom of the mariners who had ventured before me. Yes, Elias, the same doom may await thee. But somehow my spirit rallied. Marvelous incentives to weigh anchor, excellent prospects for glory, indeed—why, a crushed hull will make me an angel by default. Yes, there is a freezing demise in this trade of the ice—a breathtakingly sudden plunge of a man into the Abyss. But what of it? It seems to me we have entirely misunderstood this business of flesh and spirit. It seems to me that what they call my physical form here on earth is merely my shadow. It seems to me that in viewing eternity, we are too much like barnacles staring at the moon through the brine, and supposing that thick brine to be the clearest ether. It seems to me my body is but the heavy diving-bell of my true intellect. In truth, let the sea take my body if she will, take it I say, it is not my essence. And therefore hurrah for the frozen North; and let the crushing ice and the broken timbers come when they please, for crush my soul, Neptune himself cannot.
CHAPTER 9. The Chancel.
I had not been settled very long before a man of a certain weathered dignity arrived; instantly as the wind-blown door slammed shut behind him, a rapid respectful gaze by all the gathered townsfolk sufficiently proved that this rugged elder was the minister. Yes, it was the renowned Reverend Silas, so named by the sealers, among whom he was held in the highest esteem. He had been a navigator and a harpooner in his youth, but for several decades past had devoted his days to the church. At the time I now speak of, Reverend Silas was in the frosty winter of a vigorous old age; that sort of age which seems to harbor a second burning youth, for among all the deep crevices of his weathered face, there glowed certain warm embers of a renewed vitality—the summer sun burning bright even amidst December’s frost. No one knowing his past could for the first time look upon Reverend Silas without profound curiosity, for there were certain ingrained nautical habits about him, born of that perilous oceanic existence he had survived. When he stepped inside I noted that he bore no parasol, and surely had not ridden in a carriage, for his canvas sou’wester dripped with freezing rain, and his massive wool coat appeared to drag him downward with the sheer weight of the ocean it had drunk. Nevertheless, hat and coat and heavy boots were one by one shed, and hung up in a small alcove by the vestibule; then, dressed in a solemn black suit, he steadily advanced toward the chancel. Like most antique chancels, it was elevated considerably, and since a standard set of steps to such a peak would, by its gradual incline across the floor, severely restrict the already cramped seating of the chapel, the builder, it appeared, had taken a suggestion from Reverend Silas, and completed the chancel without stairs, substituting a slanted gangway plank, much like those used in boarding a frigate from the docks. The widow of a shipwright had furnished the chapel with a beautiful set of braided hemp handrails for this gangway, which, being itself varnished and polished to a rich oak hue, the whole arrangement, considering the character of the church, seemed in no way inappropriate.

These stone markers might as well be sitting in the Roman catacombs. It's a strange thing, how we categorize the dead. A common adage says the sea keeps her secrets—and she certainly holds more than any emperor's vault—but why do we dress in pitch-black for a friend who died yesterday, yet wear our brightest clothes if he merely sets sail for the Orient? We weep bitterly for people we claim are walking the streets of heaven. A sudden dark shape in the water is enough to terrify the bravest sailor. Contradictions, all of them, but they carry a certain weight. Belief is a scavenger. It circles the wreckage, pulling its most vital nourishment straight from our deepest despairs.
You can probably guess how I felt. It was the night before I was supposed to leave for the Arctic fisheries, and I was standing in the dim, weeping light of that chapel, reading the doom of the mariners who had gone before me. Yes, Elias, I told myself. That same doom might be waiting for you.
But somehow, my spirit rallied. What marvelous incentives to weigh anchor! Excellent prospects for glory. A crushed hull would make me an angel by default. There is a freezing kind of demise in the ice trade—a breathtakingly sudden plunge into the abyss—but what does it actually matter? I think we’ve completely misunderstood flesh and spirit. The physical form walking this earth is just a shadow. When we try to look at eternity, we're like barnacles staring at the moon through the brine, convinced the saltwater is the clearest sky. My body is just a heavy diving-bell for the mind. Let the sea take it. It isn't me.
Hurrah for the North. Let the crushing ice and the broken timbers come whenever they please. Neptune himself couldn't crush my soul.
CHAPTER 9. The Chancel.
I hadn't been sitting long when the wind blew the heavy chapel door wide open. A man of weathered dignity stepped through. The door slammed shut behind him, and the entire congregation turned to look, their quiet respect making it instantly clear: this was the minister.
It was Reverend Silas. The sealers all knew him, and they held him in the highest regard. He’d spent his youth as a navigator and harpooner before giving his life to the church. He was in the frosty winter of a vigorous old age, the kind of age that seems to hide a second youth burning just beneath the surface. Deep in the crevices of his weathered face, you could see the warm embers of renewed vitality. A summer sun burning right through the December frost.
Even if you didn't know his history, you couldn't look at Silas without being curious. He carried himself with the ingrained habits of a man who had survived a perilous life at sea. He certainly hadn't arrived in a carriage. His canvas sou’wester dripped with freezing rain, and his massive wool coat was so soaked it looked like it was dragging him to the floor.
One by one, he stripped off the hat, the coat, and his heavy boots, hanging them in a small alcove by the vestibule. Underneath, he wore a solemn black suit.
He walked steadily toward the chancel. It was an antique structure, elevated high above the floor. A standard set of steps would have eaten up too much of the cramped seating area, so the builder had apparently taken a suggestion from the Reverend himself. There were no stairs. Instead, a slanted gangway plank led up to the peak, just like the ones used to board a frigate from the docks. A shipwright's widow had even braided a set of hemp handrails for it. The wood was polished to a rich oak hue, and honestly, considering the kind of church this was, the whole setup felt perfectly right.

For elsewhere he says, "Charge them that are rich in this world, that they be not high-minded, nor trust in uncertain riches, but in the living God, who giveth us richly all things to enjoy; that they do good, that they be rich in good works, ready to distribute, willing to communicate; laying up in store for themselves a good foundation against the time to come, that they may lay hold on eternal life. "[52] They who were making such a use of their property have been consoled for light losses by great gains, and have had more pleasure in those possessions which they have securely laid past, by freely giving them away, than grief in those which they entirely lost by an anxious and selfish hoarding of them. For nothing could perish on earth save what they would be ashamed to carry away from earth. Our Lord's injunction runs, "Lay not up for yourselves treasures upon earth, where moth and rust doth corrupt, and where thieves break through and steal; but lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where neither moth nor rust doth corrupt, and where thieves do not break through nor steal: for where your treasure is, there will your heart be also. "[53] And they who have listened to this injunction have proved in the time of tribulation how well they were advised in not despising this most trustworthy teacher, and most faithful and mighty guardian of their treasure. For if many were glad that their treasure was stored in places which the enemy chanced not to light upon, how much better founded was the joy of those who, by the counsel of their God, had fled with their treasure to a citadel which no enemy can possibly reach! Thus our Paulinus, bishop of Nola,[54] who voluntarily abandoned vast wealth and became quite poor, though abundantly rich in holiness, when the barbarians sacked Nola, and took him prisoner, used silently to pray, as he afterwards told me, "O Lord, let me not be troubled for gold and silver, for where all my treasure is Thou knowest." For all his treasure was where he had been taught to hide and store it by Him who had also foretold that these calamities would happen in the world. Consequently those persons who obeyed their Lord when He warned them where and how to lay up treasure, did not lose even their earthly possessions in the invasion of the barbarians; while those who are now repenting that they did not obey Him have learnt the right use of earthly goods, if not by the wisdom which would have prevented their loss, at least by the experience which follows it. But some good and Christian men have been put to the torture, that they might be forced to deliver up their goods to the enemy. They could indeed neither deliver nor lose that good which made themselves good. If, however, they preferred torture to the surrender of the mammon of iniquity, then I say they were not good men. Rather they should have been reminded that, if they suffered so severely for the sake of money, they should endure all torment, if need be, for Christ's sake; that they might be taught to love Him rather who enriches with eternal felicity all who suffer for Him, and not silver and gold, for which it was pitiable to suffer, whether they preserved it by telling a lie, or lost it by telling the truth. For under these tortures no one lost Christ by confessing Him, no one preserved wealth save by denying its existence. So that possibly the torture which taught them that they should set their affections on a possession they could not lose, was more useful than those possessions which, without any useful fruit at all, disquieted and tormented their anxious owners. But then we are reminded that some were tortured who had no wealth to surrender, but who were not believed when they said so. These too, however, had perhaps some craving for wealth, and were not willingly poor with a holy resignation; and to such it had to be made plain, that not the actual possession alone, but also the desire of wealth, deserved such excruciating pains. And even if they were destitute of any hidden stores of gold and silver, because they were living in hopes of a better life,--I know not indeed if any such person was tortured on the supposition that he had wealth; but if so, then certainly in confessing, when put to the question, a holy poverty, he confessed Christ.

For as the apostle writes, "Set your minds on things that are above, not on things that are on earth; for you have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God." They who have directed their ambitions in such a manner have found comfort during sudden political ruins and exiles, feeling more joy in the celestial honors they have secured by humbling themselves, than sorrow in the worldly titles they forfeited by a desperate and prideful clinging to them. For nothing truly perishes in the collapse of kingdoms save that which is bound to the dust of the earth. Our Lord's command is clear, "Do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul; rather fear him who can destroy both soul and body in hell." And those who have heeded this warning have discovered in the hour of persecution how wise they were to trust this supreme physician, and most invincible defender of their true life. If many rejoiced that their physical bodies were sheltered in fortresses the marauders failed to breach, how much more profound was the peace of those who, guided by their Creator, had anchored their existence in a sanctuary no human blade can pierce! Thus our venerable Martin, leader of his flock, who willingly relinquished the prestige of the magistrate's chair and embraced the lowly state of a servant, though immensely exalted in grace, when the usurpers stormed the province and bound him in chains, would quietly pray, as he later recounted to us, "O Lord, let me not tremble for my station or my flesh, for my true dignity is held entirely by Thee." For all his honor was deposited where he had been instructed to entrust it by Him who warned that thrones would topple and nations rage. Consequently, those individuals who heeded their Master when He taught them where to seek glory, did not lose their essential nobility even in the degradation of the prisons; while those who now lament their failure to obey Him have discovered the proper estimation of human praise, if not by the humility that would have spared their pride, at least by the experience of the humiliation that shattered it. But some devout and faithful men have been cast into dungeons, that they might be compelled to renounce their allegiances or confess to treasons. They could not, in truth, forfeit that inward loyalty which made them faithful. If, however, they preferred the agonizing rack to the surrender of their political ambitions, then I maintain they were not truly devout. Rather, they should have been instructed that, if they endured such extreme torment for the sake of a fleeting crown, they ought to bear all suffering, if necessary, for the King of Heaven; so they might learn to adore Him who crowns with everlasting glory all who bleed for Him, and not the favor of monarchs, for which it was tragic to suffer, whether they retained it by a treacherous oath, or lost it by an honest word. For under the whip, no one lost Christ by proclaiming Him, no one preserved earthly power except by abandoning their soul. So that possibly the imprisonment which demonstrated that they must fix their hopes on a kingdom they could not lose, was far more beneficial than those high offices which, lacking any eternal merit, only burdened and endangered their anxious occupants. But we are told that some were imprisoned who possessed no political influence to surrender, yet their captors refused to believe them when they said so. These individuals, however, perhaps harbored a secret lust for power, and did not accept their humble station with a sanctified peace; and to them it had to be revealed that not only the actual possession, but the mere craving for worldly authority, merited such bitter correction. And even if they were completely devoid of any hidden thirst for rank, because they lived entirely for the kingdom to come—I cannot say for certain if any such soul was tormented under the false assumption that they held political sway; but if they were, then surely in proclaiming, when put to the question, a holy obscurity, they confessed Christ.

The apostle offers a clear directive: "Set your minds on things that are above, not on things that are on earth; for you have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God." Those who actually live by this principle find a strange comfort when political ruin or sudden exile strikes. They realize that the spiritual honors they’ve secured through humility bring them far more joy than any grief caused by the loss of their worldly titles. After all, when a kingdom collapses, the only things that truly perish are the things tied to the dust in the first place.
Consider Christ's warning not to fear those who can only kill the body, but rather to fear the one who can destroy both body and soul. People who take this seriously find out, in times of severe persecution, just how wise it is to trust the ultimate defender of their lives. It's one thing to feel relieved because your physical body is hiding behind fortress walls the enemy hasn't breached yet. It’s a far deeper peace to know you’ve anchored your existence in a sanctuary no human weapon can ever touch.
I think of Martin. He willingly gave up the prestige of a magistrate's seat to live as a humble servant, becoming immensely rich in grace instead. When usurpers finally overran the province and threw him in chains, he didn't panic. He later told me his silent prayer during captivity: "Lord, let me not tremble for my status or my flesh. You alone hold my true dignity." He had deposited his honor exactly where he was taught to put it, trusting the same God who warned us that earthly thrones would eventually fall.
Because of this, those who listened to the Master didn't lose their essential nobility, even when they were degraded in prison cells. As for the others—those who now bitterly regret ignoring His advice—they are finally learning the true value of human praise. If they couldn't learn it through a humility that would have protected their pride, they are learning it through the humiliation that shattered it.
Of course, the objection is raised that some genuinely devout people were thrown into dungeons and tortured to extract political confessions. But let's look closer. They couldn't actually forfeit the inward loyalty that made them good in the first place. If they chose to endure the rack simply to protect their earthly political ambitions, then they weren't truly devout. Someone should have pointed out the irony: if they were willing to suffer that much for a temporary crown, they should have been willing to endure it for the King of Heaven. Suffering for the favor of a mortal monarch is a tragedy, whether you keep that favor by lying or lose it by telling the truth.
Under the whip, no one loses their salvation by confessing it, and no one keeps their earthly power without compromising their soul. In the end, the torture that forces someone to value an eternal kingdom over a temporary office is far more useful than the office itself, which only ever brought its owner anxiety.
But what about those who were tortured because they were suspected of having political influence, even when they actually had none? Their captors simply refused to believe them. Even here, it’s entirely possible they harbored a secret, unsanctified lust for power. If so, the pain served to show them that merely craving worldly authority is just as toxic as possessing it. And what if they were entirely free of that ambition, living only for the life to come? I can't say for sure if anyone completely innocent of political ambition was tortured on the mere suspicion of it. But if they were, then by confessing their holy obscurity under the torturer's hands, they were ultimately just confessing their allegiance to Christ.

“Hast thou seen the White Whale?” demanded Ahab, when the boat drifted back. “Think, think of thy whale-boat, stoven and sunk! Beware of the horrible tail!”  “I tell thee again, Gabriel, that—” But again the boat tore ahead as if dragged by fiends. Nothing was said for some moments, while a succession of riotous waves rolled by, which by one of those occasional caprices of the seas were tumbling, not heaving it. Meantime, the hoisted sperm whale’s head jogged about very violently, and Gabriel was seen eyeing it with rather more apprehensiveness than his archangel nature seemed to warrant. When this interlude was over, Captain Mayhew began a dark story concerning Moby Dick; not, however, without frequent interruptions from Gabriel, whenever his name was mentioned, and the crazy sea that seemed leagued with him. It seemed that the Jeroboam had not long left home, when upon speaking a whale-ship, her people were reliably apprised of the existence of Moby Dick, and the havoc he had made. Greedily sucking in this intelligence, Gabriel solemnly warned the captain against attacking the White Whale, in case the monster should be seen; in his gibbering insanity, pronouncing the White Whale to be no less a being than the Shaker God incarnated; the Shakers receiving the Bible. But when, some year or two afterwards, Moby Dick was fairly sighted from the mast-heads, Macey, the chief mate, burned with ardour to encounter him; and the captain himself being not unwilling to let him have the opportunity, despite all the archangel’s denunciations and forewarnings, Macey succeeded in persuading five men to man his boat. With them he pushed off; and, after much weary pulling, and many perilous, unsuccessful onsets, he at last succeeded in getting one iron fast. Meantime, Gabriel, ascending to the main-royal mast-head, was tossing one arm in frantic gestures, and hurling forth prophecies of speedy doom to the sacrilegious assailants of his divinity. Now, while Macey, the mate, was standing up in his boat’s bow, and with all the reckless energy of his tribe was venting his wild exclamations upon the whale, and essaying to get a fair chance for his poised lance, lo! a broad white shadow rose from the sea; by its quick, fanning motion, temporarily taking the breath out of the bodies of the oarsmen. Next instant, the luckless mate, so full of furious life, was smitten bodily into the air, and making a long arc in his descent, fell into the sea at the distance of about fifty yards. Not a chip of the boat was harmed, nor a hair of any oarsman’s head; but the mate for ever sank. It is well to parenthesize here, that of the fatal accidents in the Sperm-Whale Fishery, this kind is perhaps almost as frequent as any. Sometimes, nothing is injured but the man who is thus annihilated; oftener the boat’s bow is knocked off, or the thigh-board, in which the headsman stands, is torn from its place and accompanies the body. But strangest of all is the circumstance, that in more instances than one, when the body has been recovered, not a single mark of violence is discernible; the man being stark dead. The whole calamity, with the falling form of Macey, was plainly descried from the ship. Raising a piercing shriek—“The vial! the vial!” Gabriel called off the terror-stricken crew from the further hunting of the whale. This terrible event clothed the archangel with added influence; because his credulous disciples believed that he had specifically fore-announced it, instead of only making a general prophecy, which any one might have done, and so have chanced to hit one of many marks in the wide margin allowed. He became a nameless terror to the ship. Mayhew having concluded his narration, Ahab put such questions to him, that the stranger captain could not forbear inquiring whether he intended to hunt the White Whale, if opportunity should offer. To which Ahab answered—“Aye.” Straightway, then, Gabriel once more started to his feet, glaring upon the old man, and vehemently exclaimed, with downward pointed finger—“Think, think of the blasphemer—dead, and down there!—beware of the blasphemer’s end!”  Ahab stolidly turned aside; then said to Mayhew, “Captain, I have just bethought me of my letter-bag; there is a letter for one of thy officers, if I mistake not. Starbuck, look over the bag.”  Every whale-ship takes out a goodly number of letters for various ships, whose delivery to the persons to whom they may be addressed, depends upon the mere chance of encountering them in the four oceans.

"Hast thou tracked the Shadow Leviathan?" demanded Elias, when the skiff pitched closer.
"Reflect, reflect upon thy shattered timbers and drowned men! Beware the sweeping fin!"
"I declare to thee again, Malachi, that—" But once more the small boat was propelled forward as if by unseen specters. Nothing was said for several moments, while a chaotic series of rogue waves surged past, which by some strange humor of the ocean were churning, not swelling. Meanwhile, the suspended carcass of the pilot whale pitched about furiously, and Malachi was observed glaring at it with a dread that scarcely fit his self-proclaimed holy mantle. When this violent motion subsided, Captain Thorne commenced a grim recounting of the Shadow Leviathan; not, however, without constant interjections from Malachi, whenever the creature's name was uttered, and the turbulent sea that appeared to do its bidding.
It emerged that the Providence had only recently passed the Cape, when upon hailing a merchantman, her crew was reliably informed of the Shadow Leviathan's existence, and the ruin it had wrought. Eagerly drinking in this dark news, Malachi fiercely cautioned the captain against pursuing the beast, should it appear; in his fevered delusions, proclaiming the Leviathan to be the Wrath of the Almighty made flesh; the very instrument of divine judgment. But when, some months later, the Leviathan was distinctly spied from the rigging, Silas, the first mate, burned with ambition to strike it; and the captain being somewhat inclined to grant him the chance, despite all the prophet’s condemnations and dire omens, Silas succeeded in rallying six men to lower his boat. With them he launched forth; and, after an exhausting pursuit, and several dangerous, failed approaches, he at last managed to secure one line fast.
Meanwhile, Malachi, climbing to the highest cross-trees, was waving his arms in frenzied motions, and screaming predictions of imminent ruin upon the wicked attackers of his idol. Now, while Silas, the mate, stood tall in the prow, and with all the reckless vigor of his kind was shouting his fierce cries at the beast, and maneuvering to strike with his raised lance, behold! a massive dark shape breached from the depths; by its sudden, sweeping displacement, temporarily pulling the breath from the lungs of the rowers. In the next instant, the doomed mate, so brimming with violent energy, was launched bodily into the sky, and tracing a high curve in his fall, plunged into the waves some forty yards away. Not a plank of the craft was splintered, nor a hair on any rower's head harmed; but the mate sank forever.
It is well to note here, that of the lethal accidents in the deep-sea fisheries, this specific tragedy is perhaps almost as frequent as any. Sometimes, nothing is damaged save the man who is thus extinguished; oftener the boat’s stem is shattered, or the bracing-plank, against which the harpooneer leans, is wrenched away and falls with the corpse. But most peculiar of all is the circumstance, that in more instances than one, when the body has been retrieved, not a single bruise or mark of violence is visible; the man being entirely lifeless.
The entire disaster, with the plummeting figure of Silas, was clearly witnessed from the deck. Raising a piercing shriek—"The Seventh Seal! The Seal is broken!" Malachi commanded the terrified crew to abandon the further hunting of the beast. This horrific event cloaked the prophet with increased authority; because his superstitious followers believed that he had exactly fore-announced it, instead of merely shouting a broad prophecy, which any madman might have done, and so happened to strike one of many marks in the vast unpredictability of the sea. He became a nameless terror aboard the ship.
Thorne having concluded his narration, Elias put such inquiries to him, that the visiting captain could not forbear asking whether he intended to chase the Shadow Leviathan, should opportunity offer.
To which Elias answered—"Aye."
Straightway, then, Malachi leapt up once more, glaring fiercely at the scarred captain, and passionately cried, with a downward pointed finger—"Think, think of the doomed mate—dead, and rotting below!—beware the sinner's grave!"
Elias impassively turned his head; then said to Thorne, "Captain, I am just reminded of my dispatch-box; there is a parcel for one of thy crew, if my memory serves. Mr. Vance, fetch the mail."
Every voyaging vessel takes out a goodly number of letters for various ships, whose delivery to the intended hands relies upon the mere chance of encountering them in the four oceans.

"Have you tracked the Shadow Leviathan?" Elias called out as the skiff pitched closer to the hull.
"Reflect on your shattered timbers! Think of your drowned men! Beware the sweeping fin!"
"I declare to you again, Malachi, that—"
Captain Thorne's words were cut off. The small boat jerked forward, shoved by some unseen force beneath the water. Nobody spoke for a long minute. A chaotic run of rogue waves surged past them, churning rather than swelling, driven by a strange oceanic humor. Tied alongside the ship, the carcass of a pilot whale pitched violently in the froth. Malachi stared down at it. For a self-proclaimed prophet, he looked terrified.
Once the sea settled, Thorne finally told the story of the Shadow Leviathan. He couldn't get through it without Malachi interrupting every time the beast's name was spoken.
It turned out the Providence had barely rounded the Cape when they hailed a passing merchantman and first heard about the Leviathan's path of ruin. Malachi had soaked up every dark detail. He began threatening the captain, warning him never to pursue it. In his fevered mind, the Leviathan was the Wrath of the Almighty made flesh—a divine instrument of judgment.
But months later, the lookout spotted the beast from the rigging. Silas, the first mate, was practically burning with the need to kill it. Despite the prophet’s screaming omens, Thorne gave Silas the go-ahead. The mate grabbed six men and lowered his boat.
They rowed for hours. After several near-misses, Silas finally managed to sink a harpoon into the creature's flank.
High above them on the ship, Malachi had climbed to the cross-trees. He was thrashing his arms, screaming that doom was coming for the wicked men attacking his false idol.
Down in the boat, Silas stood tall in the prow. He was shouting, full of reckless adrenaline, maneuvering to strike again with his raised lance.
Then a massive dark shape breached. The sheer force of the displacement sucked the air right out of the rowers' lungs. A heartbeat later, the mate was launched bodily into the sky. He traced a high, long arc and plunged into the waves forty yards away. The boat wasn't scratched. None of the rowers lost a single drop of blood.
But Silas never came back up.
Oddly enough, this kind of accident is shockingly common in the deep-sea fisheries. Sometimes a man is just extinguished, leaving the boat completely intact. Other times the stem is shattered, or the bracing-plank is ripped out and falls into the sea with the harpooneer. The strangest part is when the body is actually recovered. Often, there isn't a single bruise or broken bone to be found. The man is just dead.
The crew watched the entire disaster from the deck of the Providence.
"The Seventh Seal is broken!" Malachi shrieked, commanding the terrified crew to abandon the hunt.
The tragedy gave the madman an iron grip over the crew. They actually believed he had predicted Silas's death with divine accuracy, rather than just shouting a broad curse that happened to come true in a highly dangerous profession. From that day on, he became a walking terror aboard the ship.
When Thorne finished the story, Elias asked a few pointed questions. Finally, the visiting captain had to ask if Elias actually intended to chase the Leviathan if he got the chance.
"Aye," Elias said.
Malachi sprang to his feet. He glared at the scarred captain and pointed a trembling finger at the water. "Think of the dead mate! Rotting in the dark! Beware the sinner's grave!"
Elias barely even blinked. He turned away and looked down at Thorne. "Captain, I just remembered my dispatch box. I believe I have a parcel for one of your men. Mr. Vance, fetch the mail."
It was a common enough practice. Voyaging vessels always carried letters for other ships, trusting the sheer coincidence of crossing paths in the trackless oceans to get the mail where it belonged.

What advantage can it be to you to offend Mr. Darcy? You will never recommend yourself to his friend by so doing.”  Nothing that she could say, however, had any influence. Her mother would talk of her views in the same intelligible tone. Elizabeth blushed and blushed again with shame and vexation. She could not help frequently glancing her eye at Mr. Darcy, though every glance convinced her of what she dreaded; for though he was not always looking at her mother, she was convinced that his attention was invariably fixed by her. The expression of his face changed gradually from indignant contempt to a composed and steady gravity. At length, however, Mrs. Bennet had no more to say; and Lady Lucas, who had been long yawning at the repetition of delights which she saw no likelihood of sharing, was left to the comforts of cold ham and chicken. Elizabeth now began to revive. But not long was the interval of tranquillity; for when supper was over, singing was talked of, and she had the mortification of seeing Mary, after very little entreaty, preparing to oblige the company. By many significant looks and silent entreaties did she endeavour to prevent such a proof of complaisance,--but in vain; Mary would not understand them; such an opportunity of exhibiting was delightful to her, and she began her song. Elizabeth’s eyes were fixed on her, with most painful sensations; and she watched her progress through the several stanzas with an impatience which was very ill rewarded at their close; for Mary, on receiving amongst the thanks of the table the hint of a hope that she might be prevailed on to favour them again, after the pause of half a minute began another. Mary’s powers were by no means fitted for such a display; her voice was weak, and her manner affected. Elizabeth was in agonies. She looked at Jane to see how she bore it; but Jane was very composedly talking to Bingley. She looked at his two sisters, and saw them making signs of derision at each other, and at Darcy, who continued, however, impenetrably grave. She looked at her father to entreat his interference, lest Mary should be singing all night. He took the hint, and, when Mary had finished her second song, said aloud,--  “That will do extremely well, child. You have delighted us long enough. Let the other young ladies have time to exhibit.”  Mary, though pretending not to hear, was somewhat disconcerted; and Elizabeth, sorry for her, and sorry for her father’s speech, was afraid her anxiety had done no good. Others of the party were now applied to. “If I,” said Mr. Collins, “were so fortunate as to be able to sing, I should have great pleasure, I am sure, in obliging the company with an air; for I consider music as a very innocent diversion, and perfectly compatible with the profession of a clergyman. I do not mean, however, to assert that we can be justified in devoting too much of our time to music, for there are certainly other things to be attended to. The rector of a parish has much to do. In the first place, he must make such an agreement for tithes as may be beneficial to himself and not offensive to his patron. He must write his own sermons; and the time that remains will not be too much for his parish duties, and the care and improvement of his dwelling, which he cannot be excused from making as comfortable as possible. And I do not think it of light importance that he should have attentive and conciliatory manners towards everybody, especially towards those to whom he owes his preferment. I cannot acquit him of that duty; nor could I think well of the man who should omit an occasion of testifying his respect towards anybody connected with the family.” And with a bow to Mr. Darcy, he concluded his speech, which had been spoken so loud as to be heard by half the room. Many stared--many smiled; but no one looked more amused than Mr. Bennet himself, while his wife seriously commended Mr. Collins for having spoken so sensibly, and observed, in a half-whisper to Lady Lucas, that he was a remarkably clever, good kind of young man. To Elizabeth it appeared, that had her family made an agreement to expose themselves as much as they could during the evening, it would have been impossible for them to play their parts with more spirit, or finer success; and happy did she think it for Bingley and her sister that some of the exhibition had escaped his notice, and that his feelings were not of a sort to be much distressed by the folly which he must have witnessed.

"What possible benefit can there be in alienating Mr. Hawthorne? You will certainly not elevate yourself in his friend's esteem by such a public display."
Her quiet admonitions, however, carried no weight. Her mother continued to broadcast her ambitions in the same penetrating volume. Eleanor felt her cheeks burn with successive waves of humiliation. She could not resist casting anxious glances toward Mr. Hawthorne, though every look confirmed her worst fears; for while he did not constantly stare at her mother, Eleanor was certain his focus was entirely captured by the spectacle. The disposition of his features shifted slowly from disdainful shock to a mask of absolute, chilling stoicism. Eventually, however, Mrs. Stanton had no more breath to expend; and Lady Bertram, who had spent the last hour stifling yawns over the recital of triumphs she saw no likelihood of ever sharing, was abandoned to the consolations of roast pheasant and wine. Eleanor began to revive.
But her reprieve was remarkably short; for the moment supper concluded, musical entertainment was proposed, and she suffered the mortification of watching Beatrice, needing barely a whisper of encouragement, preparing to command the room. Through desperate glares and silent pleas did Eleanor attempt to halt this display of eager compliance—but without success; Beatrice would not interpret them; the chance to demonstrate her accomplishments was too intoxicating, and she commenced her ballad. Eleanor’s eyes were locked on her, consumed by acute distress; she tracked the progression of the verses with an impatience that was bitterly rewarded at their conclusion; for Beatrice, interpreting the polite thanks of the table as a hope that she might be prevailed upon to sing again, paused only for half a minute before launching into a second melody. Beatrice’s abilities were completely inadequate for such an exhibition; her pitch was wavering, and her delivery hopelessly affected. Eleanor was in agony. She glanced at Charlotte to gauge how she bore it; but Charlotte was peacefully absorbed in conversation with Mr. Alden. She looked at his two sisters, and saw them exchanging smirks of derision, and at Hawthorne, who maintained his impenetrable granite facade. She looked at her father to entreat his interference, lest Beatrice should be screeching all night. He comprehended the hint, and, the instant Beatrice concluded her second effort, he announced aloud,—
"That will do perfectly well, my dear. You have entertained us long enough. Allow the other young women an opportunity to exhibit."
Beatrice, though attempting to feign deafness to the slight, was visibly flustered; and Eleanor, pitying her sister, and regretting her father's tactless intervention, feared her anxiety had only worsened the situation. Others in the party were now addressed.
"If Providence," declared Mr. Whitfield, "had blessed me with a singing voice, I should be exceedingly glad, I assure you, to favor the company with a hymn; for I regard musical exertion as a perfectly innocent pastime, and entirely suitable for a man of the cloth. I do not suggest, naturally, that we are justified in dedicating excessive hours to the pursuit, for there are certainly graver responsibilities to attend to. The vicar of a parish carries a heavy burden. Primarily, he must negotiate his tithes in a manner profitable to himself and not offensive to his patron. He must compose his own sermons; and the hours that remain will not be too much for his pastoral duties, and the improvement of his parsonage, which he cannot be excused from making as comfortable as possible. And I do not consider it of light importance that he exhibit an attentive and subservient demeanor to everyone, most particularly to those to whom he owes his preferment. I cannot acquit him of that duty; nor could I respect a man who should neglect an occasion to broadcast his reverence toward anyone connected to the patron's noble line." And with a sweeping bow toward Mr. Hawthorne, he concluded his speech, which had been delivered so loudly as to be heard by half the parlor.
Many gaped—many smiled; but nobody looked more entertained than Mr. Stanton himself, while his wife earnestly praised Mr. Whitfield for having spoken so sensibly, and observed, in a half-whisper to Lady Bertram, that he was a staggeringly clever, good kind of young gentleman. To Eleanor, it appeared that had her relatives made a formal pact to expose themselves to the greatest possible degree during the evening, they could not have played their parts with more spirit, or finer success; and she considered it a profound mercy for Mr. Alden and her sister that much of the exhibition had escaped his notice, and that his mild feelings were not of a sort to be deeply distressed by the folly he was forced to witness.

"What possible good does it do to alienate Mr. Hawthorne?" Eleanor whispered frantically. "You certainly aren't going to win over his best friend by acting like this."
It was no use. Her mother ignored the warning entirely, continuing to broadcast her grand social schemes at the exact same piercing volume. Eleanor felt her cheeks burning. Wave after wave of humiliation washed over her. She couldn't stop herself from casting nervous glances across the room at Mr. Hawthorne, even though every look only confirmed her worst fears. He wasn't staring directly at her mother the whole time, but Eleanor knew he was listening to every single word. She watched the expression on his face harden, slowly shifting from shocked disdain to a mask of freezing, impenetrable stoicism.
Eventually, though, Mrs. Stanton ran out of breath. Lady Bertram—who had spent the better part of an hour stifling yawns while listening to social triumphs she would never share—was finally left in peace with a plate of cold roast pheasant.
Eleanor exhaled.
The relief didn't last. The moment the supper plates were cleared, someone proposed musical entertainment. To Eleanor's absolute horror, Beatrice needed barely a whisper of encouragement before marching over to the pianoforte. Eleanor tried everything—desperate glares, silent pleading motions—to stop her sister's eager exhibition. Beatrice was blind to it all. The chance to show off was simply too intoxicating, and she launched into a ballad.
Eleanor sat in agony. She tracked the painfully slow progression of the verses, praying for the song to end. But her patience was bitterly rewarded. Beatrice took the polite, tepid applause as a desperate demand for an encore. She paused just long enough to draw a breath, then plunged straight into a second melody.
She simply didn't have the talent for this kind of display. Her pitch wobbled terribly, and her delivery was hopelessly, embarrassingly melodramatic. Eleanor looked around the room in a panic. She checked Charlotte to see how she was bearing the torture, but Charlotte was perfectly happy, completely absorbed in her conversation with Mr. Alden. Hawthorne’s haughty sisters, however, were openly exchanging smirks. Hawthorne himself sat like a granite statue.
Desperate, Eleanor shot a pleading look at her father. Do something, she prayed, terrified Beatrice was going to screech until dawn.
