Mr. Stanton took the hint. The very second Beatrice finished her second song, he spoke up from his armchair. "That will do perfectly well, my dear. You've entertained us quite enough for one evening. Let the other young ladies have a turn."
Beatrice pretended not to hear the slight, but she flushed bright pink, clearly rattled. Eleanor’s stomach dropped. She felt awful for her sister and hated her father's tactless delivery, instantly regretting that she had begged him to intervene at all.
By then, the rest of the room was moving on.
"If the Almighty had blessed me with a singing voice," Mr. Whitfield suddenly announced to the room at large, "I assure you, I would be exceedingly glad to favor you all with a hymn. I regard musical exertion as a perfectly wholesome pastime—highly suitable for a clergyman! I don't mean to suggest one should dedicate excessive hours to it, naturally. A vicar has much heavier burdens."
He paused, ensuring he had an audience. "Primarily, he must negotiate his tithes so they are profitable to him, yet entirely agreeable to his noble patron. He must compose sermons! The hours leftover are barely enough for pastoral visits and the upkeep of the parsonage. And, of course, I consider it a matter of supreme importance that a clergyman exhibit a flattering, subservient demeanor to everyone—especially the aristocratic hands that feed him. I could never respect a man who failed to seize every public occasion to broadcast his deep reverence for anyone connected to his patron's noble line."
To punctuate this bizarre, unprompted speech, Whitfield executed a deep, sweeping bow directly at Mr. Hawthorne. He had spoken so loudly that every single person in the parlor heard him.
People stared. A few hid their smiles behind their hands. Mr. Stanton looked absolutely delighted by the sheer absurdity of it all, while his wife earnestly whispered to Lady Bertram about what a staggeringly brilliant young man Mr. Whitfield was.
Eleanor closed her eyes. If her relatives had signed a formal, blood-sworn pact to thoroughly disgrace themselves before midnight, they couldn't have executed it with more devastating perfection. Her only comfort was knowing that Mr. Alden was so wrapped up in Charlotte that he had missed most of the circus, and that his nature was far too sweet to be repelled by the profound idiocy he was forced to witness.

When the clock struck three, Elizabeth felt that she must go, and very unwillingly said so. Miss Bingley offered her the carriage, and she only wanted a little pressing to accept it, when Jane testified such concern at parting with her that Miss Bingley was obliged to convert the offer of the chaise into an invitation to remain at Netherfield for the present. Elizabeth most thankfully consented, and a servant was despatched to Longbourn, to acquaint the family with her stay, and bring back a supply of clothes. [Illustration:  “The Apothecary came” ]     [Illustration:  “covering a screen” ]     CHAPTER VIII. [Illustration]  At five o’clock the two ladies retired to dress, and at half-past six Elizabeth was summoned to dinner. To the civil inquiries which then poured in, and amongst which she had the pleasure of distinguishing the much superior solicitude of Mr. Bingley, she could not make a very favourable answer. Jane was by no means better. The sisters, on hearing this, repeated three or four times how much they were grieved, how shocking it was to have a bad cold, and how excessively they disliked being ill themselves; and then thought no more of the matter: and their indifference towards Jane, when not immediately before them, restored Elizabeth to the enjoyment of all her original dislike. Their brother, indeed, was the only one of the party whom she could regard with any complacency. His anxiety for Jane was evident, and his attentions to herself most pleasing; and they prevented her feeling herself so much an intruder as she believed she was considered by the others. She had very little notice from any but him. Miss Bingley was engrossed by Mr. Darcy, her sister scarcely less so; and as for Mr. Hurst, by whom Elizabeth sat, he was an indolent man, who lived only to eat, drink, and play at cards, who, when he found her prefer a plain dish to a ragout, had nothing to say to her. When dinner was over, she returned directly to Jane, and Miss Bingley began abusing her as soon as she was out of the room. Her manners were pronounced to be very bad indeed,--a mixture of pride and impertinence: she had no conversation, no style, no taste, no beauty. Mrs. Hurst thought the same, and added,--  “She has nothing, in short, to recommend her, but being an excellent walker. I shall never forget her appearance this morning. She really looked almost wild.”  “She did indeed, Louisa. I could hardly keep my countenance. Very nonsensical to come at all! Why must _she_ be scampering about the country, because her sister had a cold? Her hair so untidy, so blowzy!”  “Yes, and her petticoat; I hope you saw her petticoat, six inches deep in mud, I am absolutely certain, and the gown which had been let down to hide it not doing its office.”  “Your picture may be very exact, Louisa,” said Bingley; “but this was all lost upon me. I thought Miss Elizabeth Bennet looked remarkably well when she came into the room this morning. Her dirty petticoat quite escaped my notice.”  “_You_ observed it, Mr. Darcy, I am sure,” said Miss Bingley; “and I am inclined to think that you would not wish to see _your sister_ make such an exhibition.”  “Certainly not.”  “To walk three miles, or four miles, or five miles, or whatever it is, above her ancles in dirt, and alone, quite alone! what could she mean by it? It seems to me to show an abominable sort of conceited independence, a most country-town indifference to decorum.”  “It shows an affection for her sister that is very pleasing,” said Bingley. “I am afraid, Mr. Darcy,” observed Miss Bingley, in a half whisper, “that this adventure has rather affected your admiration of her fine eyes.”  “Not at all,” he replied: “they were brightened by the exercise.” A short pause followed this speech, and Mrs. Hurst began again,--  “I have an excessive regard for Jane Bennet,--she is really a very sweet girl,--and I wish with all my heart she were well settled. But with such a father and mother, and such low connections, I am afraid there is no chance of it.”  “I think I have heard you say that their uncle is an attorney in Meryton?”  “Yes; and they have another, who lives somewhere near Cheapside.”  “That is capital,” added her sister; and they both laughed heartily.

When the grandfather clock chimed four, Eleanor recognized that her visit must conclude, and expressed her need to depart with obvious reluctance. Miss Harrington proposed the use of the carriage, and Eleanor required only a fraction of encouragement to accept it, when Margaret exhibited such distress at the prospect of separation that Miss Harrington was compelled to transform the offer of transport into a formal invitation to remain at Rosewood for the time being. Eleanor accepted with profound gratitude, and a footman was immediately dispatched to Willowbourne, to inform her parents of the arrangement and to procure a trunk of necessary garments.
CHAPTER IX.
At half-past five the ladies withdrew to change their attire, and shortly after seven Eleanor was summoned to the dining room. To the polite inquiries which immediately surrounded her, among which she could easily distinguish the far more genuine concern of Mr. Charles Harrington, she was unable to provide a comforting reply. Margaret was in no way improved. The two sisters, upon receiving this news, declared repeatedly how devastated they were, how dreadful a severe fever could be, and how vehemently they detested falling ill themselves; and having said this, they dismissed the subject from their minds: and their complete apathy toward Margaret, when she was not directly in their sight, revived all of Eleanor's initial antipathy toward them. Their brother, indeed, was the singular member of the household whom she could view with any charity. His distress for Margaret was unmistakable, and his polite efforts toward Eleanor were entirely soothing; they served to mitigate her persistent feeling that she was an unwanted interloper among the rest of the company. She received little attention from anyone but him. Miss Harrington was entirely absorbed by Mr. Sterling, and her sister Augusta scarcely less devoted to the same pursuit; and as for Augusta’s husband, Mr. Vance, beside whom Eleanor was seated, he was a sluggish gentleman whose sole passions were roasted meats and billiards, who, upon observing that she preferred a simple broth to the rich ragout, found her entirely unworthy of conversation.
Once the meal was concluded, she hastened immediately back to Margaret's chamber, and Miss Harrington commenced a thorough critique of her the moment the door was shut. Her deportment was judged to be exceedingly coarse—a combination of arrogance and lack of polish: she possessed no wit, no elegance, no refinement, no charm. Augusta was of the exact same mind, and supplemented the attack,—
“She has absolutely nothing to recommend her, save a capacity for vigorous hiking. I shall not soon recover from her arrival this afternoon. She looked practically feral.”
“She truly did, Augusta. I was forced to look away to hide my shock. What utter nonsense to march over here at all! Why must she be tramping across the fields, merely because her sister has caught a chill? Her hair was so dishevelled, so utterly chaotic!”
“Yes, and her pelisse; I pray you noticed her pelisse, splattered with mire up to the calves, I am perfectly certain, and the cloak she wore attempting to conceal it entirely failing in its duty.”
“Your observation may be precise, Augusta,” remarked Charles; “but I confess I saw none of it. I thought Miss Eleanor looked exceptionally vibrant when she entered the parlor today. The mud on her hem was entirely lost on me.”
“You must have noted it, Mr. Sterling, I am convinced,” stated Miss Harrington; “and I am quite certain you would never desire your sister to make such a public spectacle.”
“Undoubtedly not.”
“To march two or three miles, or whatever the distance might be, up to her shins in the muck, and without a chaperone, completely unaccompanied! What on earth could be her justification? To me, it demonstrates a dreadful sort of arrogant rebellion, a most vulgar, provincial disregard for propriety.”
“It demonstrates a devotion to her sister that is highly commendable,” Charles countered.
“I deeply fear, Mr. Sterling,” murmured Miss Harrington in a lowered voice, “that this wild escapade has somewhat lessened your appreciation for her brilliant eyes.”
“Not in the slightest,” he answered: “they were only made brighter by the brisk air.”
A brief silence succeeded this remark, before Augusta resumed the assault,—
“I harbor a profound fondness for Margaret,—she is genuinely a delightful creature,—and I desire nothing more than to see her make a respectable match. But with such parents, and such appallingly low relations, I fear there is zero probability of it.”
“Did I not hear you mention that their uncle is a mere clerk in the village?”
“Indeed; and there is another who trades in textiles somewhere near Cheapside.”
“How marvelous,” added her sister; and the two women shared a long and hearty laugh.

The clock chimed four. Eleanor knew it was time to leave, though she hated the thought of going. Miss Harrington politely offered the carriage. Eleanor was just about to accept—needing only the slightest bit of prodding—when Margaret became so distressed at the idea of being left behind that Miss Harrington had no choice. She was forced to turn her offer of a ride into an invitation to stay.
Eleanor accepted with massive relief. A footman was quickly sent off to Willowbourne to let her parents know and to pack a trunk of clothes.
CHAPTER IX.
By half-past five, the ladies went upstairs to change, and a little after seven, Eleanor was called down to dinner.
A chorus of polite inquiries greeted her, though only Charles Harrington’s concern felt remotely genuine. Unfortunately, Eleanor had no good news to share; Margaret wasn't doing any better. Hearing this, the two Harrington sisters immediately launched into a theatrical display of sympathy. They repeated over and over how devastating fevers were, how dreadful the whole situation was, and how much they absolutely hated being sick themselves.
And then, having checked that social box, they dropped the subject entirely.
Watching them completely forget Margaret's existence the moment she was out of sight brought all of Eleanor’s original dislike rushing right back. Charles really was the only person in the room she could stand. He was obviously worried about Margaret, and he went out of his way to make Eleanor feel comfortable, which made her feel a little less like an unwanted intruder.
Certainly, no one else was paying her any attention. Miss Harrington was completely occupied with fawning over Mr. Sterling, and Augusta was doing exactly the same. As for Augusta’s husband, Mr. Vance? He was a sluggish man whose only real interests were eating heavy meats and playing billiards. He happened to be sitting next to Eleanor, but the moment he realized she preferred a simple broth to the heavy, rich sauces, he wrote her off as entirely unworthy of his time.
The second the meal ended, Eleanor escaped back to Margaret's room.
The door had barely clicked shut before Miss Harrington started tearing her apart. Her manners were supposedly atrocious—a terrible mix of arrogance and awkwardness. She had no wit, no elegance, no charm whatsoever.
Augusta eagerly agreed. "She has absolutely nothing going for her, except maybe the fact that she's a very vigorous hiker. I don't think I'll ever recover from seeing her walk in this afternoon. She looked practically feral."
"She really did, Augusta. I actually had to look away! I mean, what utter nonsense to march all the way over here. Why does she have to go tramping across the fields just because her sister caught a chill? Her hair was a total disaster."
"And her coat! Tell me you saw the hem of her coat. She was splashed with mud up to her calves, I'm sure of it. That cloak she wore to cover it up didn't hide a thing."
"You might be right, Augusta," Charles cut in, "but I confess I didn't notice any of that. I thought Miss Eleanor looked exceptionally vibrant when she walked in today. I certainly didn't see any mud."
Miss Harrington turned to the dark-haired guest. "You must have noticed it, Mr. Sterling. I’m quite sure you would never want to see your sister make such a public spectacle of herself."
"Undoubtedly not."
"To march two or three miles—or whatever it is—up to her shins in muck! And completely alone! What on earth was she thinking? To me, it just shows this dreadful, arrogant rebelliousness. A totally provincial disregard for basic propriety."
"It shows she's deeply devoted to her sister," Charles argued. "I think it's highly commendable."
Miss Harrington leaned in, lowering her voice to a theatrical whisper. "I do fear, Mr. Sterling, that this wild little escapade might have lessened your appreciation for her brilliant eyes."
"Not in the slightest," Sterling replied mildly. "The brisk air only made them brighter."
A brief, uncomfortable silence settled over the room. Augusta quickly pivoted to a new angle of attack.
"I really am terribly fond of Margaret. She's a sweet girl, and I'd love to see her make a respectable match. But with parents like hers? And those appallingly low relations? There is zero probability of it."
"Didn't you say their uncle is a clerk down in the village?" Miss Harrington asked.
"Yes! And they have another one who trades in textiles somewhere near Cheapside."
"How marvelous," Miss Harrington said drily.
The two sisters looked at each other and burst into loud, ringing laughter.

I prithee tell me who is it quickly, and speak apace. I would thou couldst stammer, that thou mightst pour this concealed man out of thy mouth, as wine comes out of narrow-mouthed bottle—either too much at once or none at all. I prithee take the cork out of thy mouth that I may drink thy tidings. CELIA. So you may put a man in your belly. ROSALIND. Is he of God’s making? What manner of man? Is his head worth a hat, or his chin worth a beard? CELIA. Nay, he hath but a little beard. ROSALIND. Why, God will send more if the man will be thankful. Let me stay the growth of his beard, if thou delay me not the knowledge of his chin. CELIA. It is young Orlando, that tripped up the wrestler’s heels and your heart both in an instant. ROSALIND. Nay, but the devil take mocking! Speak sad brow and true maid. CELIA. I’ faith, coz, ’tis he. ROSALIND. Orlando? CELIA. Orlando. ROSALIND. Alas the day, what shall I do with my doublet and hose? What did he when thou saw’st him? What said he? How looked he? Wherein went he? What makes he here? Did he ask for me? Where remains he? How parted he with thee? And when shalt thou see him again? Answer me in one word. CELIA. You must borrow me Gargantua’s mouth first. ’Tis a word too great for any mouth of this age’s size. To say ay and no to these particulars is more than to answer in a catechism. ROSALIND. But doth he know that I am in this forest and in man’s apparel? Looks he as freshly as he did the day he wrestled? CELIA. It is as easy to count atomies as to resolve the propositions of a lover. But take a taste of my finding him, and relish it with good observance. I found him under a tree, like a dropped acorn. ROSALIND. It may well be called Jove’s tree when it drops forth such fruit. CELIA. Give me audience, good madam. ROSALIND. Proceed. CELIA. There lay he, stretched along like a wounded knight. ROSALIND. Though it be pity to see such a sight, it well becomes the ground. CELIA. Cry “holla!” to thy tongue, I prithee. It curvets unseasonably. He was furnished like a hunter. ROSALIND. O, ominous! He comes to kill my heart. CELIA. I would sing my song without a burden. Thou bring’st me out of tune. ROSALIND. Do you not know I am a woman? When I think, I must speak. Sweet, say on. Enter Orlando and Jaques. CELIA. You bring me out. Soft, comes he not here? ROSALIND. ’Tis he! Slink by, and note him. [_Rosalind and Celia step aside._]  JAQUES. I thank you for your company but, good faith, I had as lief have been myself alone. ORLANDO. And so had I, but yet, for fashion sake, I thank you too for your society. JAQUES. God be wi’ you, let’s meet as little as we can. ORLANDO. I do desire we may be better strangers. JAQUES. I pray you, mar no more trees with writing love songs in their barks. ORLANDO. I pray you, mar no more of my verses with reading them ill-favouredly. JAQUES. Rosalind is your love’s name? ORLANDO. Yes, just. JAQUES. I do not like her name. ORLANDO. There was no thought of pleasing you when she was christened. JAQUES. What stature is she of? ORLANDO. Just as high as my heart. JAQUES. You are full of pretty answers. Have you not been acquainted with goldsmiths’ wives, and conned them out of rings? ORLANDO. Not so; but I answer you right painted cloth, from whence you have studied your questions. JAQUES. You have a nimble wit. I think ’twas made of Atalanta’s heels. Will you sit down with me? And we two will rail against our mistress the world and all our misery. ORLANDO. I will chide no breather in the world but myself, against whom I know most faults. JAQUES. The worst fault you have is to be in love. ORLANDO. ’Tis a fault I will not change for your best virtue. I am weary of you. JAQUES. By my troth, I was seeking for a fool when I found you. ORLANDO. He is drowned in the brook. Look but in, and you shall see him. JAQUES. There I shall see mine own figure. ORLANDO. Which I take to be either a fool or a cipher.

LEONORA. I beg you, tell me who it is at once, and speak swiftly. I wish you suffered from a stutter, so that you might force this secret man out of your mouth the way water escapes a narrow jug—either a great flood all at once, or nothing at all. I beg you, unseal your lips so I may drink down your news.
JULIA. Careful, or you might swallow a man whole.
LEONORA. Is he a proper creature? What kind of man is he? Is his head fit for a crown, or his chin fit for a shave?
JULIA. Well, he has but a shadow of a beard.
LEONORA. Why, time will grant him more if he is patient. I will forgive his lack of a beard if you do not delay telling me the owner of the chin.
JULIA. It is young Sebastian, who knocked the champion from his horse and stole your heart in the very same afternoon.
LEONORA. Stop, may the devil take your teasing! Speak to me with a serious face and a true heart.
JULIA. Upon my honor, cousin, it is he.
LEONORA. Sebastian?
JULIA. Sebastian.
LEONORA. Heavens above, what am I to do dressed in these stable-boy clothes? What was he doing when you spied him? What did he say? How did he appear? What was he wearing? Why is he in this province? Did he inquire after me? Where is he staying? How did you take your leave of him? And when will you cross his path again? Answer me in a single breath.
JULIA. You must find me a dragon’s mouth first. The answer is too massive for any ordinary mortal's jaw. To simply say yes or no to your demands would be longer than reciting a holy text.
LEONORA. But does he realize I am hiding in this valley, wearing a man’s coat? Does he look as glorious as he did the day of the joust?
JULIA. It is easier to count the grains of sand on the shore than to satisfy the curiosity of a woman in love. But take a small sample of my discovery, and listen closely. I found him resting beneath a willow, like a fallen branch.
LEONORA. It must be a sacred tree to drop such magnificent timber.
JULIA. Give me the floor, good madam.
LEONORA. Proceed.
JULIA. There he lay, stretched out across the grass like a weary soldier.
LEONORA. A pitiful sight, perhaps, but one that surely blessed the earth beneath him.
JULIA. Call a halt to your tongue, I beg you. It runs wildly off the path. He was dressed like a traveling merchant.
LEONORA. Oh, tragic! He means to purchase my soul.
JULIA. I would like to finish my tale without a chorus. You throw me entirely off my rhythm.
LEONORA. Do you forget I am a woman? When a thought strikes me, I must voice it. Sweet cousin, continue.
Enter Sebastian and Cassius.
JULIA. Now you have silenced me. Look softly, is that not him approaching?
LEONORA. It is! Step into the shadows, and let us watch.
[Leonora and Julia step aside.]
CASSIUS. I thank you for walking with me, but truthfully, I would have much preferred my own solitude.
SEBASTIAN. I feel exactly the same, but for the sake of politeness, I will thank you for your companionship.
CASSIUS. Heaven keep you; let us arrange to meet as infrequently as possible.
SEBASTIAN. I sincerely hope we might become absolute strangers.
CASSIUS. I ask that you stop ruining the tavern walls by carving your tragic love poems into the wood.
SEBASTIAN. I ask that you stop ruining my poems by reading them with such a sour voice.
CASSIUS. Leonora is the name of your beloved?
SEBASTIAN. Yes, exactly.
CASSIUS. I find it a deeply unpleasant name.
SEBASTIAN. Nobody was thinking of your preferences on the day she was baptized.
CASSIUS. What height is she?
SEBASTIAN. Exactly as high as the heavens.
CASSIUS. You are full of rehearsed retorts. Have you been spending time with street performers, memorizing their cheap jokes?
SEBASTIAN. Hardly; but I answer you with the same painted-on wit that you use to ask your tiresome questions.
CASSIUS. You have a quick tongue. I imagine it was forged from quicksilver. Will you sit by the river with me? We can complain together about the wretchedness of the world and our own miserable lives.
SEBASTIAN. I refuse to criticize any living, breathing soul in this world except for myself, since I know my own flaws best.
CASSIUS. The most glaring flaw you possess is being in love.
SEBASTIAN. It is a flaw I would never exchange for your greatest virtue. I am thoroughly tired of you.
CASSIUS. I swear to you, I was wandering about looking for a fool when I bumped into you.
SEBASTIAN. He drowned in the river. Just look into the water, and you will spot him.
CASSIUS. If I look in the water, I will only see my own reflection.
SEBASTIAN. Which I consider to be either a fool or a total zero.

LEONORA: Tell me. Now. I wish you had a stutter so you’d just choke the secret out, like water glugging out of a bottleneck—either pouring out in a flood or stopping completely. Just pull the cork out and let me drink the news!
JULIA: Careful, you'll drown on it.
LEONORA: Is he handsome? What kind of man are we talking about? Does he have a noble head? A decent jawline?
JULIA: He has the beginnings of a beard, at least.
LEONORA: Well, time will fix that if he’s patient. I don't care about the beard, just tell me whose face it’s on!
JULIA: It’s Sebastian. The one who unhorsed the champion yesterday and stole your heart in the exact same afternoon.
LEONORA: Stop. Don't mess with me. Look me in the eye and tell me the truth.
JULIA: I swear on my life, cousin. It’s him.
LEONORA: Sebastian?
JULIA: Sebastian.
LEONORA: Oh, God. What am I supposed to do in these ridiculous stable-boy clothes? What was he doing when you saw him? What did he say? How did he look? What was he wearing? Why is he even here? Did he ask about me? Where is he staying? How did you leave things with him, and when are you going to see him again? Answer me in one word.
JULIA: I'd need the mouth of a sea monster. You're asking for the impossible. Just saying "yes" or "no" to half of that would take me an hour.
LEONORA: But does he know I'm hiding in this province? Does he know I'm dressed like a boy? Does he look as incredible as he did at the joust?
JULIA: It would be easier to count the atoms in the air than to answer a woman in love. But listen. Here's how I found him. He was lying under a massive willow tree, curled up like a dropped branch.
LEONORA: A sacred tree, to drop something so gorgeous.
JULIA: Will you let me speak?
LEONORA: Go on.
JULIA: He was stretched out in the grass, looking like an exhausted soldier.
LEONORA: It hurts to think of him suffering, but I bet the grass was thrilled.
JULIA: Rein it in, Leonora, seriously. Your tongue is running away without you. Anyway, he was dressed like a traveling merchant.
LEONORA: Oh, God. He's here to buy my soul.
JULIA: I'd really love to finish this story without the background commentary. You're ruining my flow.
LEONORA: I can't help it! When I have a thought, it has to come out. Go on, sweetie, keep going.
(Enter Sebastian and Cassius)
JULIA: Well, you distracted me. Look—isn't that him?
LEONORA: It is! Hide, hide. Let's listen.
(Leonora and Julia duck behind a hedge.)
CASSIUS: Thanks for the walk. Honestly, though? I'd rather be alone.
SEBASTIAN: The feeling is mutual. But I'm trying to be polite, so thanks for the company.
CASSIUS: Take care of yourself. Let's try to never do this again.
SEBASTIAN: I'd love it if we could be perfect strangers.
CASSIUS: Do me a favor and stop vandalizing the tavern walls by carving your terrible love poems into the wood.
SEBASTIAN: Do me a favor and stop ruining my poems by reading them out loud in that grating voice.
CASSIUS: Your girl's name is Leonora, right?
SEBASTIAN: It is.
CASSIUS: Hate the name.
SEBASTIAN: I don't think anyone was consulting you at her christening.
CASSIUS: How tall is she?
SEBASTIAN: Tall enough to reach my heart.
CASSIUS: Very slick. Did you memorize that off a cheap greeting card?
SEBASTIAN: No, but I'm matching the cheap, painted-on sarcasm you're using to ask these questions.
CASSIUS: You're quick, I'll give you that. Want to sit by the river with me? We can complain about how awful the world is and how miserable we are.
SEBASTIAN: I don't criticize anyone in this world but myself. I'm the only one whose flaws I actually know.
CASSIUS: Your worst flaw is being in love.
SEBASTIAN: A flaw I wouldn't trade for your best virtue. I'm so bored of you.
CASSIUS: I swear, I was wandering around looking for an idiot when I ran into you.
SEBASTIAN: The idiot drowned in the river. Go look in the water, you'll see him.
CASSIUS: If I look in the water, I'm just going to see my own reflection.
SEBASTIAN: Which is either an idiot or a total zero. Take your pick.

That’s meat and drink to me now. I have seen Sackerson loose twenty times, and have taken him by the chain. But, I warrant you, the women have so cried and shrieked at it that it passed. But women, indeed, cannot abide ’em; they are very ill-favoured rough things. Enter Page. PAGE Come, gentle Master Slender, come. We stay for you. SLENDER. I’ll eat nothing, I thank you, sir. PAGE. By cock and pie, you shall not choose, sir! Come, come. SLENDER. Nay, pray you lead the way. PAGE. Come on, sir. SLENDER. Mistress Anne, yourself shall go first. ANNE. Not I, sir; pray you keep on. SLENDER. Truly, I will not go first; truly, la! I will not do you that wrong. ANNE. I pray you, sir. SLENDER. I’ll rather be unmannerly than troublesome. You do yourself wrong, indeed, la! [_Exeunt._]  SCENE II. The same  Enter Sir Hugh Evans and Simple. EVANS. Go your ways, and ask of Doctor Caius’ house which is the way. And there dwells one Mistress Quickly, which is in the manner of his nurse, or his dry nurse, or his cook, or his laundry, his washer and his wringer. SIMPLE. Well, sir. EVANS. Nay, it is petter yet. Give her this letter. For it is a ’oman that altogether’s acquaintance with Mistress Anne Page; and the letter is to desire and require her to solicit your master’s desires to Mistress Anne Page. I pray you be gone. I will make an end of my dinner; there’s pippins and cheese to come. [_Exeunt._]  SCENE III. A room in the Garter Inn  Enter Falstaff, Host, Bardolph, Nym, Pistol and Robin. FALSTAFF. Mine host of the Garter! HOST. What says my bully rook? Speak scholarly and wisely. FALSTAFF. Truly, mine host, I must turn away some of my followers. HOST. Discard, bully Hercules; cashier. Let them wag; trot, trot. FALSTAFF. I sit at ten pounds a week. HOST. Thou’rt an emperor—Caesar, Keiser, and Pheazar. I will entertain Bardolph. He shall draw, he shall tap. Said I well, bully Hector? FALSTAFF. Do so, good mine host. HOST. I have spoke, let him follow.—Let me see thee froth and lime. I am at a word, follow. [_Exit Host._]  FALSTAFF. Bardolph, follow him. A tapster is a good trade. An old cloak makes a new jerkin; a withered servingman a fresh tapster. Go, adieu. BARDOLPH. It is a life that I have desired. I will thrive. PISTOL. O base Hungarian wight, wilt thou the spigot wield? [_Exit Bardolph._]  NYM He was gotten in drink. Is not the humour conceited? FALSTAFF. I am glad I am so acquit of this tinderbox. His thefts were too open. His filching was like an unskilful singer, he kept not time. NYM. The good humour is to steal at a minute’s rest. PISTOL. “Convey,” the wise it call. “Steal?” Foh! A _fico_ for the phrase! FALSTAFF. Well, sirs, I am almost out at heels. PISTOL. Why, then, let kibes ensue. FALSTAFF. There is no remedy, I must cony-catch, I must shift. PISTOL. Young ravens must have food. FALSTAFF. Which of you know Ford of this town? PISTOL. I ken the wight, he is of substance good. FALSTAFF. My honest lads, I will tell you what I am about. PISTOL. Two yards, and more. FALSTAFF. No quips now, Pistol. Indeed, I am in the waist two yards about, but I am now about no waste; I am about thrift. Briefly, I do mean to make love to Ford’s wife. I spy entertainment in her. She discourses, she carves, she gives the leer of invitation. I can construe the action of her familiar style; and the hardest voice of her behaviour, to be Englished rightly, is “I am Sir John Falstaff’s.”  PISTOL. He hath studied her will and translated her will—out of honesty into English. NYM. The anchor is deep. Will that humour pass? FALSTAFF. Now, the report goes she has all the rule of her husband’s purse. He hath a legion of angels. PISTOL. As many devils entertain, and “To her, boy,” say I.  NYM. The humour rises; it is good. Humour me the angels. FALSTAFF. I have writ me here a letter to her; and here another to Page’s wife, who even now gave me good eyes too, examined my parts with most judicious oeillades. Sometimes the beam of her view gilded my foot, sometimes my portly belly. PISTOL. Then did the sun on dunghill shine. NYM.

FALLOW. That is bread and butter to me now. I have seen the great mastiff unleashed a dozen times, and held him by the leather collar. But I swear, the ladies wept and shrieked so loudly it was deafening. Truly, women cannot stomach such beasts; they are rough, ugly brutes.
Enter Sterling.
STERLING. Come along, good Master Fallow, come. We wait upon your presence.
FALLOW. I have no appetite, I thank you, sir.
STERLING. By the saints, you shall not refuse us, sir! Come along.
FALLOW. Nay, I pray you, walk ahead.
STERLING. Come on, sir.
FALLOW. Mistress Clara, you must proceed first.
CLARA. Not I, sir; I pray you, continue.
FALLOW. Truly, I will not walk before you; truly, no! I will not offer you such an insult.
CLARA. I insist, sir.
FALLOW. I would rather be considered rude than a nuisance. You do yourself an injustice, truly, no!
[Exeunt.]
SCENE II. The same
Enter Father Clement and Tobias.
CLEMENT. Go your way, and ask at the apothecary’s shop for the proper path. And there resides one Dame Prattle, who serves as his housekeeper, or his nursemaid, or his baker, or his laundress, folding and scrubbing.
TOBIAS. Very well, sir.
CLEMENT. Nay, there is more. Deliver her this parchment. For she is a woman holding great sway with Mistress Clara Sterling; and the letter is to command and beg her to advance your master’s suit to Mistress Clara. I pray you, hurry. I must finish my supper; there are roasted apples and wine yet to come.
[Exeunt.]
SCENE III. A room in the Boar's Head Tavern
Enter Captain Stone, Innkeeper, Tankard, Slicer, and Flint.
STONE. My good host of the Boar's Head!
INNKEEPER. What says my stout general? Speak with wit and wisdom.
STONE. Truly, mine host, I must dismiss some of my retinue.
INNKEEPER. Cast them off, brave Samson; discharge them. Let them wander; away, away.
STONE. My lodgings cost me five pounds a week.
INNKEEPER. Thou art a king—a sultan, a czar, a ruler. I will employ Tankard. He shall pour the ale, he shall draw the taps. Do I speak well, mighty Hector?
STONE. Do so, my excellent host.
INNKEEPER. The word is given, let him follow.—Let me see you pour and skim. I am a man of my word, follow.
[Exit Innkeeper.]
STONE. Tankard, go with him. A barkeep is a noble calling. An old coat makes a fine tavern-rag; a stale rogue makes a fresh pourer. Go, farewell.
TANKARD. It is the very life I have dreamed of. I shall prosper.
FLINT. Oh, miserable vagabond, wilt thou stoop to a wooden spout?
[Exit Tankard.]
SLICER. He was conceived in a wine barrel. Is the jest not amusing?
STONE. I am relieved to be rid of that clumsy thief. His pickpocketing was too obvious. His thievery was like a lame dancer, entirely out of step.
SLICER. The proper jest is to swipe the coin on the downbeat.
FLINT. "Acquire," the learned men say. "Steal?" Bah! A curse upon the word!
STONE. Well, gentlemen, my purse is completely empty.
FLINT. Why, then, let hunger follow.
STONE. There is no other way, I must cheat, I must scheme.
FLINT. Hungry wolves must feed.
STONE. Which of you is acquainted with Mercer of this village?
FLINT. I know the fellow; his vaults are heavy with gold.
STONE. My loyal knaves, I shall reveal my plot to you.
FLINT. A mile wide, and more.
STONE. No jests now, Flint. Truly, I am a mile wide in the belly, but I have no time for belly-laughs; I must find coin. In short, I intend to woo Mercer’s wife. I see an opening there. She chats, she smiles, she casts a welcoming eye. I can translate the language of her bold manners; and the loudest decree of her conduct, translated plainly, is "I belong to Captain Stone."
FLINT. He has read her mind and decoded her intent—from virtue into plain speech.
SLICER. The plot deepens. Will the trick succeed?
STONE. Now, rumor holds that she controls every penny of her husband’s wealth. He possesses a mountain of gold coins.
FLINT. A mountain of demons awaits, and "Attack her, brave soldier," say I.
SLICER. The scheme ripens; it is excellent. Fetch me those coins.
STONE. I have drafted a letter to her here; and yet another to Sterling’s wife, who just today gave me a favorable look as well, surveying my figure with the most calculating glances. At one moment her gaze illuminated my sturdy boot, and at the next my majestic stomach.
FLINT. Thus the golden sun beams upon a heap of manure.
SLICER.

FALLOW: That’s the meat and potatoes of it for me. I’ve watched the big mastiff get turned loose a dozen times, and I’m the one who holds him by the leather collar! Course, the ladies always shrieked and cried until it was deafening. Women just can't stomach it. Too rough and ugly for 'em.
(Sterling enters.)
STERLING: Come along, Fallow. We’re all waiting on you.
FALLOW: Oh, I couldn't eat a thing. Thank you, though.
STERLING: Nonsense, I won't take no for an answer! Come on.
FALLOW: No, please, you go ahead.
STERLING: Come on, man.
FALLOW: Mistress Clara? You first.
CLARA: Oh, no. Please, lead the way.
FALLOW: I wouldn't dream of it. Truly! That would be a terrible insult.
CLARA: I insist.
FALLOW: I’d rather be rude than annoying. You do yourself a disservice, truly!
(They exit.)
SCENE II
(Father Clement and Tobias.)
CLEMENT: Go on, then. Stop by the apothecary’s and ask for directions. You're looking for a Dame Prattle. She’s his housekeeper. Or maybe his nurse, or baker, or laundress—basically, she washes the sheets and bakes the bread.
TOBIAS: Got it.
CLEMENT: Wait, there's more. Give her this letter. She's got a lot of influence with Clara Sterling. The letter tells her—well, begs her, really—to push your master’s suit with the girl. Hurry up, now. I need to finish my supper. I think there are roasted apples coming.
(They exit.)
SCENE III. The Boar's Head Tavern.
(Captain Stone, the Innkeeper, Tankard, Slicer, and Flint.)
STONE: Host! Mine excellent host of the Boar's Head!
INNKEEPER: What's the word, my stout general? Lay some wisdom on me.
STONE: Honestly? I need to lay off some of my men.
INNKEEPER: Cut 'em loose, Samson! Send 'em packing. Let them hit the road.
STONE: I'm paying five pounds a week just for my rooms.
INNKEEPER: You're a king, a sultan, an absolute czar! Tell you what. I’ll hire Tankard. He can draw the taps and pour the ale. How’s that sound, Hector?
STONE: You're a lifesaver.
INNKEEPER: Done. Let's see you pour a pint, Tankard. Come on.
(The Innkeeper exits.)
STONE: Follow him, Tankard. Barkeep is a noble job. An old coat makes a great bar rag, and a washed-up thief makes a decent pourer. Farewell.
TANKARD: It’s everything I ever wanted. I'm going to be rich.
FLINT: You miserable wretch. Are you really going to stoop to pouring cheap ale?
(Tankard exits.)
SLICER: He was probably conceived in a wine barrel anyway. Funny, right?
STONE: Good riddance. The man was a completely incompetent thief. His pickpocketing was painfully obvious—like a dancer who can't find the beat.
SLICER: The real trick is swiping the purse on the downbeat.
FLINT: "Acquire," gentlemen. The learned call it "acquiring." "Steal" is such an ugly word!
STONE: Regardless, my purse is totally empty.
FLINT: Guess we starve, then.
STONE: No. I'll have to cheat. I need a scheme.
FLINT: Wolves gotta eat.
STONE: Do any of you know Mercer? The local fellow?
FLINT: I know him. Guy's loaded.
STONE: Well, boys, let me tell you what I'm planning.
FLINT: A massive dinner?
STONE: Quiet, Flint. Yes, I have a massive gut, but I don't have time for belly-laughs. I need cash. Long story short, I’m going to seduce Mercer’s wife. I see an opening. She chats, she smiles, she lingers just a little too long. I can read between the lines of her bold behavior, and the absolute loudest thing she's broadcasting is, "I belong to Captain Stone."
FLINT: He's decoded her virtue and translated it into plain English.
SLICER: The plot thickens. Think it'll work?
STONE: Word on the street is she controls every single penny of her husband’s money. And he has a mountain of gold.
FLINT: Go get 'em, soldier.
SLICER: I like where this is going. Go get us that gold.
STONE: I’ve already written her a letter. And I wrote another one to Sterling’s wife, who actually gave me the eye today, too. She gave my physique a very thorough look. One second she's admiring my boots, and the next, she's eyeing my majestic stomach.
FLINT: Like the golden sun beaming down on a pile of manure.
SLICER:

ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. You would all this time have proved there is no time for all things. DROMIO OF SYRACUSE. Marry, and did, sir; namely, e’en no time to recover hair lost by nature. ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. But your reason was not substantial why there is no time to recover. DROMIO OF SYRACUSE. Thus I mend it: Time himself is bald, and therefore, to the world’s end will have bald followers. ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. I knew ’twould be a bald conclusion. But soft! who wafts us yonder? Enter Adriana and Luciana. ADRIANA. Ay, ay, Antipholus, look strange and frown, Some other mistress hath thy sweet aspects. I am not Adriana, nor thy wife. The time was once when thou unurg’d wouldst vow That never words were music to thine ear, That never object pleasing in thine eye, That never touch well welcome to thy hand, That never meat sweet-savour’d in thy taste, Unless I spake, or look’d, or touch’d, or carv’d to thee. How comes it now, my husband, O, how comes it, That thou art then estranged from thyself? Thyself I call it, being strange to me, That, undividable, incorporate, Am better than thy dear self’s better part. Ah, do not tear away thyself from me; For know, my love, as easy mayst thou fall A drop of water in the breaking gulf, And take unmingled thence that drop again Without addition or diminishing, As take from me thyself, and not me too. How dearly would it touch thee to the quick, Should’st thou but hear I were licentious? And that this body, consecrate to thee, By ruffian lust should be contaminate? Wouldst thou not spit at me, and spurn at me, And hurl the name of husband in my face, And tear the stain’d skin off my harlot brow, And from my false hand cut the wedding-ring, And break it with a deep-divorcing vow? I know thou canst; and therefore, see thou do it. I am possess’d with an adulterate blot; My blood is mingled with the crime of lust; For if we two be one, and thou play false, I do digest the poison of thy flesh, Being strumpeted by thy contagion. Keep then fair league and truce with thy true bed, I live distain’d, thou undishonoured. ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. Plead you to me, fair dame? I know you not. In Ephesus I am but two hours old, As strange unto your town as to your talk, Who, every word by all my wit being scann’d, Wants wit in all one word to understand. LUCIANA. Fie, brother, how the world is chang’d with you. When were you wont to use my sister thus? She sent for you by Dromio home to dinner. ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. By Dromio? DROMIO OF SYRACUSE. By me? ADRIANA. By thee; and this thou didst return from him, That he did buffet thee, and in his blows Denied my house for his, me for his wife. ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. Did you converse, sir, with this gentlewoman? What is the course and drift of your compact? DROMIO OF SYRACUSE. I, sir? I never saw her till this time. ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. Villain, thou liest, for even her very words Didst thou deliver to me on the mart. DROMIO OF SYRACUSE. I never spake with her in all my life. ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. How can she thus, then, call us by our names? Unless it be by inspiration. ADRIANA. How ill agrees it with your gravity To counterfeit thus grossly with your slave, Abetting him to thwart me in my mood; Be it my wrong, you are from me exempt, But wrong not that wrong with a more contempt. Come, I will fasten on this sleeve of thine. Thou art an elm, my husband, I a vine, Whose weakness, married to thy stronger state, Makes me with thy strength to communicate: If aught possess thee from me, it is dross, Usurping ivy, brier, or idle moss, Who all, for want of pruning, with intrusion Infect thy sap, and live on thy confusion. ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. To me she speaks; she moves me for her theme. What, was I married to her in my dream? Or sleep I now, and think I hear all this? What error drives our eyes and ears amiss? Until I know this sure uncertainty I’ll entertain the offer’d fallacy. LUCIANA. Dromio, go bid the servants spread for dinner. DROMIO OF SYRACUSE. O, for my beads! I cross me for a sinner.

VALENTINE. So you would argue that a fool and his gold are never truly parted?
TRANIO. Verily, sir; for the gold only leaves his purse to buy a mirror, wherein he finds a greater fool.
VALENTINE. That is a hollow reason for a heavy purse's emptying.
TRANIO. Let me patch it, then: the mirror shatters, the gold is spent, and the fool is left with nothing but his own reflections, which are worthless to the world's end.
VALENTINE. A shattered conclusion, indeed. But soft, who comes toward us with such purpose?
Enter Isabella and Beatrice.
ISABELLA. Yes, Valentine, cast your eyes away and play the stranger.
Some newer affection claims your gentle looks.
I am no longer Isabella, nor your lawful bride.
There was a time when you would freely swear
That no music was so sweet as my voice,
No sight so fair as my approaching shadow,
No touch so warm as my hand in yours,
Unless I spoke, or smiled, or walked beside you.
How is it now, my lord, oh, how can it be,
That you are so divided from your own heart?
I call it your heart, though it is frozen to me,
Because we two, united in holy bonds,
Make up one single, indivisible soul.
Do not rend yourself away from my side;
For know this, my love: you might as well cast
A cup of wine into the roaring sea,
And try to scoop that selfsame vintage back
Without a drop of brine corrupting it,
As try to separate your life from mine.
How deeply would it wound your noble pride,
If you should hear that I had played you false?
That this chaste bed, kept wholly for your sake,
By some vile stranger's touch was made impure?
Would you not curse my name, and cast me out,
And hurl the title 'wife' back in my teeth,
And strip the silken garments from my back,
And shatter our gold ring upon the stones?
I know you would; therefore, execute your justice.
For I am tainted by a borrowed sin;
My honor is darkened by your lustful crimes;
For if we two are one, and you stray far,
I drink the bitter venom of your deeds,
Made foul by your contagious treachery.
Keep, then, your vows sacred to our true home,
Lest I live blackened, while you walk unblamed.
VALENTINE. Do you address this plea to me, fair lady?
I know you not. In Mantua I am but newly arrived,
As foreign to your streets as to your speech,
Which, though I weigh each syllable with care,
Defies my wits to grasp its true intent.
BEATRICE. Shame on you, brother, to alter so drastically.
When did you ever mock my sister thus?
She sent your man Tranio to bring you to the feast.
VALENTINE. By Tranio?
TRANIO. By me?
ISABELLA. By you; and you brought back this cruel report:
That he did strike you, and amidst his blows,
Swore he knew not my house, nor me as wife.
VALENTINE. Did you parley, sir, with this noblewoman?
What is the meaning of this secret pact?
TRANIO. I, sir? I never laid eyes on her till this moment.
VALENTINE. Rogue, you lie, for her exact complaints
You shouted at me in the market square.
TRANIO. I never spoke a word to her in all my days.
VALENTINE. How can she, then, address us by our names?
Unless she reads it by some dark enchantment.
ISABELLA. How poorly it suits your dignity and age
To plot so basely with your serving-man,
Encouraging his lies to vex my patience.
Though you do me wrong, I cannot cast you off,
But do not mock my grief with added scorn.
Come, I will bind my arm unto your sleeve.
You are the sturdy oak, my lord, I the clinging vine,
Whose fragile nature, bound to your great trunk,
Draws strength and life from your foundation:
If any pestilence takes you from my hold,
It is but worthless rot, or choking weeds,
Which, lacking the gardener's blade, infect
Your noble roots, and thrive on your decay.
VALENTINE. She speaks to me; I am the center of her tale.
Was I wedded to this woman in my sleep?
Or am I slumbering now, and dreaming this?
What madness plays upon our mortal senses?
Until I find the truth beneath this fog,
I will accept the illusion she presents.
BEATRICE. Tranio, run ahead and tell them to pour the wine.
TRANIO. O, fetch a priest! I cross myself against the devil.

VALENTINE: So you're telling me a fool and his money are never truly parted?
TRANIO: Exactly, sir. The gold merely leaves his pocket to buy a looking-glass—where he immediately discovers an even bigger fool.
VALENTINE: That's a terribly flimsy excuse for going broke.
TRANIO: Let me fix it, then. The glass breaks, the money is gone, and the fool is left with absolutely nothing but his own reflections. Which, frankly, will be worthless until the end of time.
VALENTINE: A shattered conclusion if I ever heard one. Wait—hold on. Who is this coming toward us? She looks furious.
(Enter Isabella and Beatrice)
ISABELLA: Yes, go ahead, Valentine. Look away. Act like you don't know me. I suppose some new mistress has caught your eye today? I am not Isabella anymore. I'm certainly not your wife.
There was a time when you used to swear—without my having to ask!—that no music sounded as sweet as my voice. That no sight was as lovely as my face, and no touch as warm as mine. Unless I spoke, or smiled, or sat beside you, the world was nothing to you.
How is it now, my husband? How can it be that you are so completely alienated from yourself? And I do call it yourself, even though you treat me like a stranger. Because we are indivisible. We are one. I am the better part of you.
Don't tear yourself away from me. Listen to me: you might as well drop a cup of wine into the ocean and try to scoop that exact vintage back out—without a single drop of saltwater in it—as try to separate your life from mine.
Think about it. How deeply would it cut you if you heard I was being unfaithful? If this body, which belongs entirely to you, had been contaminated by some stranger's lust? Wouldn't you spit on me? Wouldn't you throw the title of 'husband' back in my face, strip the clothes off my back, and smash our wedding ring against the stones?
I know you would. So do it.
Because I am tainted. I'm stained by your sins. If we are one, and you play false, then I am the one digesting the poison of your affairs. Your contagion makes me a whore. Keep your vows. Come back to your true bed. Otherwise, I live stained, and you live dishonored.
VALENTINE: Are you... talking to me, lady? I don't know you. I've been in Mantua for exactly two hours. I'm as unfamiliar with your city as I am with your conversation. I'm listening to every word you say, and I don't understand a single one of them.
BEATRICE: Shame on you, brother. How can you change so quickly? When did you ever treat my sister like this? She sent Tranio to fetch you home for the feast.
VALENTINE: Tranio?
TRANIO: Me?
ISABELLA: Yes, you. And you came back with a miserable report. You claimed he beat you, and between the blows, he swore this wasn't his house and I wasn't his wife.
VALENTINE: Did you speak with this woman, sir? What kind of scam are you running here?
TRANIO: Me? Sir, I have never seen her in my life!
VALENTINE: You liar. You shouted those exact words at me in the market square not ten minutes ago!
TRANIO: I've never spoken to her!
VALENTINE: Then how does she know our names? Unless it's witchcraft.
ISABELLA: It is so beneath you to play these games with your servant. Encouraging him to lie just to get under my skin. Even if you're wronging me, I can't leave you—so don't add insult to injury.
Come on. I'm holding onto your sleeve and I'm not letting go. You are the oak, my husband, and I am the vine. I draw my strength and life from your roots. If anything distracts you from me, it's just rot. It's choking ivy that needs to be pruned before it infects your sap and destroys you.
VALENTINE: (Aside) She's looking right at me. I'm the center of this entire story. Did I marry this woman in my sleep? Am I asleep right now? What is wrong with my eyes and ears? ...You know what? Until I figure out what's going on, I'll just play along with the illusion.
BEATRICE: Tranio, run ahead and tell them to pour the wine.
TRANIO: Someone get a priest! I'm crossing myself, this place is full of witches!

Much as I respect them, I believe I thought only of _you_.”  Elizabeth was too much embarrassed to say a word. After a short pause, her companion added, “You are too generous to trifle with me. If your feelings are still what they were last April, tell me so at once. _My_ affections and wishes are unchanged; but one word from you will silence me on this subject for ever.”  Elizabeth, feeling all the more than common awkwardness and anxiety of his situation, now forced herself to speak; and immediately, though not very fluently, gave him to understand that her sentiments had undergone so material a change since the period to which he alluded, as to make her receive with gratitude and pleasure his present assurances. The happiness which this reply produced was such as he had probably never felt before; and he expressed himself on the occasion as sensibly and as warmly as a man violently in love can be supposed to do. Had Elizabeth been able to encounter his eyes, she might have seen how well the expression of heartfelt delight diffused over his face became him: but though she could not look she could listen; and he told her of feelings which, in proving of what importance she was to him, made his affection every moment more valuable. They walked on without knowing in what direction. There was too much to be thought, and felt, and said, for attention to any other objects. She soon learnt that they were indebted for their present good understanding to the efforts of his aunt, who _did_ call on him in her return through London, and there relate her journey to Longbourn, its motive, and the substance of her conversation with Elizabeth; dwelling emphatically on every expression of the latter, which, in her Ladyship’s apprehension, peculiarly denoted her perverseness and assurance, in the belief that such a relation must assist her endeavours to obtain that promise from her nephew which _she_ had refused to give. But, unluckily for her Ladyship, its effect had been exactly contrariwise. “It taught me to hope,” said he, “as I had scarcely ever allowed myself to hope before. I knew enough of your disposition to be certain, that had you been absolutely, irrevocably decided against me, you would have acknowledged it to Lady Catherine frankly and openly.”  Elizabeth coloured and laughed as she replied, “Yes, you know enough of my _frankness_ to believe me capable of _that_. After abusing you so abominably to your face, I could have no scruple in abusing you to all your relations.”  “What did you say of me that I did not deserve? For though your accusations were ill-founded, formed on mistaken premises, my behaviour to you at the time had merited the severest reproof. It was unpardonable. I cannot think of it without abhorrence.”  “We will not quarrel for the greater share of blame annexed to that evening,” said Elizabeth. “The conduct of neither, if strictly examined, will be irreproachable; but since then we have both, I hope, improved in civility.”  “I cannot be so easily reconciled to myself. The recollection of what I then said, of my conduct, my manners, my expressions during the whole of it, is now, and has been many months, inexpressibly painful to me. Your reproof, so well applied, I shall never forget: ‘Had you behaved in a more gentlemanlike manner.’ Those were your words. You know not, you can scarcely conceive, how they have tortured me; though it was some time, I confess, before I was reasonable enough to allow their justice.”  “I was certainly very far from expecting them to make so strong an impression. I had not the smallest idea of their being ever felt in such a way.”  “I can easily believe it. You thought me then devoid of every proper feeling, I am sure you did. The turn of your countenance I shall never forget, as you said that I could not have addressed you in any possible way that would induce you to accept me.”  “Oh, do not repeat what I then said. These recollections will not do at all. I assure you that I have long been most heartily ashamed of it.”  Darcy mentioned his letter. “Did it,” said he,--“did it _soon_ make you think better of me? Did you, on reading it, give any credit to its contents?”  She explained what its effects on her had been, and how gradually all her former prejudices had been removed.

“However highly I esteem them, I assure you my thoughts were entirely occupied by you.”
Beatrice found herself too overwhelmed by emotion to utter a single syllable. After a brief hesitation, her companion continued, “You possess too much goodness to keep me in suspense. If your disposition remains unchanged from last November, I beg you to declare it. My devotion and intentions are entirely resolute; but a single word of refusal from you will conclude this matter forever.”
Beatrice, acutely aware of the extraordinary tension and vulnerability of his position, compelled herself to reply; and swiftly, though with a faltering voice, conveyed to him that her feelings had undergone so profound a transformation since the time he referenced, that she accepted his present declarations with the utmost gratitude and joy. The sheer elation this answer inspired in him was unlike anything he had ever experienced; and he articulated his happiness with all the eloquence and fervor that a gentleman deeply in love might muster. Had Beatrice possessed the courage to meet his gaze, she would have witnessed how handsomely the glow of pure joy illuminated his features: but though she dared not look, she could attend to his words; and he spoke of an affection that, by demonstrating her absolute importance to his happiness, rendered his devotion more precious with every passing moment.
They continued their stroll without the slightest awareness of their destination. Their minds were too crowded with reflections, emotions, and confessions to spare a glance for their surroundings. She soon discovered that their present felicity was entirely due to the interference of his overbearing aunt, Lady Westbourne, who had visited him upon her return to town, detailing her journey to the country, its purpose, and the precise nature of her confrontation with Beatrice. The lady had emphasized every word of Beatrice’s responses, which, in her Ladyship’s mind, perfectly illustrated her insolence and audacity, fully expecting that this report would persuade her nephew to abandon his pursuit. But, fortunately for his Ladyship’s intended victim, the result was entirely the reverse.
“It gave me reason to hope,” he explained, “as I had scarcely dared to hope for months. I understood your character well enough to know that, had you been absolutely and irrevocably set against me, you would have confessed it to Lady Westbourne without the slightest hesitation.”
Beatrice blushed and smiled as she answered, “Indeed, you are well acquainted with my frankness to suppose me capable of that. After rebuking you so terribly to your face, I could hardly shrink from offending your relations.”
“But what did you say of me that I had not fully earned? For although your charges were drawn from false impressions, my demeanor toward you at that time warranted the sharpest condemnation. It was inexcusable. I shudder to recall it.”
“Let us not argue over who bears the greater fault for that dreadful evening,” said Beatrice. “Neither of our actions, if subjected to scrutiny, would be found faultless; but since then, I trust we have both improved in our manners.”
“I cannot forgive myself so readily. The memory of my words, my arrogance, and my posture during that entire exchange has been a source of unutterable torment to me. Your reprimand, so perfectly deserved, will never leave me: ‘Had you conducted yourself as a true gentleman.’ Those were your exact words. You cannot possibly fathom how they have haunted me; though it required some time, I must admit, before my pride would acknowledge their truth.”
“I was certainly not anticipating that they would leave such a lasting mark. I never imagined you would feel them so acutely.”
“I can well believe it. You believed me entirely devoid of any honorable sentiment, I am certain. I shall never forget the expression on your face when you declared that no form of address could have ever persuaded you to accept my hand.”
“Oh, I beg you, do not repeat my past foolishness. Those memories are too painful to bear. I promise you I have long repented of that speech.”
Mr. Carlisle then brought up the subject of his letter. “Did it,” he asked, “did it quickly alter your opinion of my character? Did you grant any truth to its claims upon your first reading?”
She proceeded to describe the profound effect it had upon her, and how, by slow degrees, all her former resentments had been washed away.

"As much as I esteem your family, my thoughts were entirely consumed by you."
Beatrice was too overwhelmed to speak.
He paused, waiting, before finally adding, "You're too kind to toy with me. If your feelings haven't changed since November, just say so. My own affections are exactly the same. But one word from you, and I will never speak of this again."
The sheer vulnerability in his voice finally pushed Beatrice to answer. Though her words came out a bit tangled, she managed to make him understand that her sentiments had completely shifted. The man she had once rejected was now the only one she wanted; she accepted his offer with absolute gratitude.
The happiness that flooded his face was something he had likely never felt before. He spoke with all the warmth and desperate relief of a man violently in love. Beatrice couldn't quite bring herself to meet his eyes, but she listened. He poured out his heart, making her realize just how thoroughly she had consumed his life, and his affection felt more precious with every step they took.
They kept walking, completely blind to where they were going. There was simply too much to say, too much to feel, to pay attention to the path.
Before long, Beatrice learned the hilarious truth: they owed this entire reunion to his overbearing aunt. Lady Westbourne had marched straight to him upon returning to London, bitterly complaining about Beatrice’s insolence. She recounted their entire argument, emphasizing Beatrice’s stubborn refusal to promise she would never marry him. The old woman had fully expected this to cure him of his infatuation.
Instead, it had done the exact opposite.
"It gave me hope," he admitted, his voice softening. "I barely dared to believe it at first. But I knew your character, Beatrice. I knew that if you were still completely and irrevocably disgusted by me, you would have had no problem telling my aunt exactly that."
Beatrice blushed, though a laugh escaped her. "Well, you certainly know my frankness well enough. After insulting you so horribly to your face, why would I hold back with your aunt?"
"Because you didn't say anything I didn't deserve. Your assumptions might have been wrong, but my behavior that night was inexcusable. I still shudder when I think of it."
"Let's not fight over who was more awful that evening," Beatrice replied gently. "Neither of us behaved perfectly. But I like to think we've both improved since then."
"I can't forgive myself that easily. The way I spoke to you, my arrogance... it's tormented me for months. You told me I hadn't behaved 'as a true gentleman.' I'll never forget that. You can't imagine how much those words stung, even if it took my pride a while to admit you were right."
"I never expected them to leave such a mark. I didn't think you'd care."
"Of course you didn't. You thought I was completely devoid of human feeling. The look on your face when you told me that nothing could ever persuade you to marry me..."
"Oh, please, stop," Beatrice groaned, burying her face in her hands. "Don't repeat it. I've been ashamed of that speech for months."
Carlisle smiled, then quietly brought up the letter he had written her the next day. "Did it help?" he asked. "Did you actually believe anything I wrote when you first read it?"
She finally looked up at him, explaining exactly how his words had struck her, and how, bit by bit, all her angry prejudices had quietly melted away.

But it was all impudent and careless.”  “If they had had facts--I mean, real facts--or at least grounds for suspicion, then they would certainly have tried to hide their game, in the hope of getting more (they would have made a search long ago besides). But they have no facts, not one. It is all mirage--all ambiguous. Simply a floating idea. So they try to throw me out by impudence. And perhaps, he was irritated at having no facts, and blurted it out in his vexation--or perhaps he has some plan... he seems an intelligent man. Perhaps he wanted to frighten me by pretending to know. They have a psychology of their own, brother. But it is loathsome explaining it all. Stop!”  “And it’s insulting, insulting! I understand you. But... since we have spoken openly now (and it is an excellent thing that we have at last--I am glad) I will own now frankly that I noticed it in them long ago, this idea. Of course the merest hint only--an insinuation--but why an insinuation even? How dare they? What foundation have they? If only you knew how furious I have been. Think only! Simply because a poor student, unhinged by poverty and hypochondria, on the eve of a severe delirious illness (note that), suspicious, vain, proud, who has not seen a soul to speak to for six months, in rags and in boots without soles, has to face some wretched policemen and put up with their insolence; and the unexpected debt thrust under his nose, the I.O.U. presented by Tchebarov, the new paint, thirty degrees Reaumur and a stifling atmosphere, a crowd of people, the talk about the murder of a person where he had been just before, and all that on an empty stomach--he might well have a fainting fit! And that, that is what they found it all on! Damn them! I understand how annoying it is, but in your place, Rodya, I would laugh at them, or better still, spit in their ugly faces, and spit a dozen times in all directions. I’d hit out in all directions, neatly too, and so I’d put an end to it. Damn them! Don’t be downhearted. It’s a shame!”  “He really has put it well, though,” Raskolnikov thought. “Damn them? But the cross-examination again, to-morrow?” he said with bitterness. “Must I really enter into explanations with them? I feel vexed as it is, that I condescended to speak to Zametov yesterday in the restaurant....”  “Damn it! I will go myself to Porfiry. I will squeeze it out of him, as one of the family: he must let me know the ins and outs of it all! And as for Zametov...”  “At last he sees through him!” thought Raskolnikov. “Stay!” cried Razumihin, seizing him by the shoulder again. “Stay! you were wrong. I have thought it out. You are wrong! How was that a trap? You say that the question about the workmen was a trap. But if you had done _that_, could you have said you had seen them painting the flat... and the workmen? On the contrary, you would have seen nothing, even if you had seen it. Who would own it against himself?”  “If I had done _that thing_, I should certainly have said that I had seen the workmen and the flat,” Raskolnikov answered, with reluctance and obvious disgust. “But why speak against yourself?”  “Because only peasants, or the most inexperienced novices deny everything flatly at examinations. If a man is ever so little developed and experienced, he will certainly try to admit all the external facts that can’t be avoided, but will seek other explanations of them, will introduce some special, unexpected turn, that will give them another significance and put them in another light. Porfiry might well reckon that I should be sure to answer so, and say I had seen them to give an air of truth, and then make some explanation.”  “But he would have told you at once that the workmen could not have been there two days before, and that therefore you must have been there on the day of the murder at eight o’clock. And so he would have caught you over a detail.”  “Yes, that is what he was reckoning on, that I should not have time to reflect, and should be in a hurry to make the most likely answer, and so would forget that the workmen could not have been there two days before.”  “But how could you forget it?”  “Nothing easier.

"But their entire approach was brazen and sloppy."
"If they possessed hard evidence—I mean, indisputable proof—or even a solid foundation for their suspicions, they would have masked their intentions to lure you in further (they would have obtained a warrant and searched your room by now). But they have nothing, not a single thread. It is all smoke—all conjecture. Just a hovering theory. So they attempt to rattle you with their brazenness. And perhaps, he was frustrated by his lack of evidence, and let it slip in his anger—or perhaps he is executing a larger strategy... he appears to be a cunning fellow. Perhaps he intended to unnerve you by feigning omnipotence. They operate on a distinct psychological plane, my friend. But it is nauseating to dissect it. Enough!"
"And it is degrading, utterly degrading! I hear you. But... since we are laying our cards on the table (and it is a relief that we finally are—I am truly glad), I will confess now that I perceived this undercurrent in them days ago. Merely a whisper, of course—an implication—but why even an implication? How possess they the audacity? What grounds do they stand upon? If you only knew the rage that has consumed me. Just imagine! Simply because an impoverished clerk, driven mad by hunger and nervous exhaustion, standing on the precipice of a violent fever (mark that well), sensitive, proud, isolated, who has not exchanged a word with a living soul for a quarter of a year, wearing a threadbare coat and tattered shoes, is forced to endure the mockery of petty officials; and then a sudden demand for rent shoved in his face, the landlord's threats, the smell of fresh varnish, the stifling heat of the precinct, a mob of strangers, the sudden chatter about the robbery of a merchant he had visited that very morning, and all this on an empty stomach—he might naturally succumb to a swoon! And that, that single moment of weakness is the entirety of their case! Curse them! I comprehend how maddening it is, but if I were you, Leonid, I would mock them, or better yet, spit right in their smug faces, and spit a hundred times over. I would strike back at them, swiftly and decisively, and put a stop to it. Curse them! Do not let them break your spirit. It is an outrage!"
"He actually frames it quite convincingly," Leonid thought. "Curse them? But the summons for tomorrow?" he muttered with dark resentment. "Am I truly forced to justify myself to them again? I am already disgusted that I lowered myself to exchange pleasantries with that rat Sokolov yesterday in the tavern...."
"To hell with it! I shall go to Lebedev myself. I will wring the truth out of him, as an old acquaintance: he must lay out his entire hand! And as for Sokolov..."
"He finally sees him for what he is!" thought Leonid.
"Wait!" cried Viktor, grabbing him by the arm. "Wait! You miscalculated. I have worked it out. You are completely wrong! How could that be a snare? You claim the question about the shattered mirror was a snare. But if you had committed the act, would you have admitted to seeing the broken glass... and the blood? On the contrary, you would have claimed ignorance, even if you had stared right at it. Who would deliberately implicate himself?"
"If I had committed the act, I would undoubtedly have stated that I had seen the glass and the room," Leonid replied, with a heavy, visible revulsion. "But why testify against your own interests?"
"Because only simpletons, or the most frightened amateurs, deny every detail outright during an interrogation. If a man possesses even a fraction of intellect and worldly experience, he will inevitably admit to all the physical realities that cannot be disproven, but he will invent alternative justifications for them, he will introduce some clever, unexpected angle, that will alter their meaning and cast them in an innocent light. Lebedev would naturally calculate that I should respond exactly so, and claim I had seen the mirror to project an aura of honesty, and then weave some excuse."
"But he would have informed you immediately that the mirror was not broken until Tuesday evening, and that therefore you must have been present at the exact hour of the crime. And in that manner, he would have trapped you on a technicality."
"Precisely, that is what he was anticipating, that I would lack the time to deliberate, and would rush to offer the most plausible-sounding defense, and thus forget that the mirror had been perfectly whole until Tuesday."
"But how could a man forget such a thing?"
"It is the easiest thing in the world."

"But the whole thing was so careless. So brazen."
Viktor paced the narrow room, his hands gesturing wildly. "If they actually had proof—real, undeniable proof—or even a decent reason to suspect you, they’d have played it close to the chest. They would have searched this rat hole days ago. But they’ve got nothing. Not a single thread. It’s all smoke and mirrors. A floating theory. So they’re trying to rattle you with pure insolence. Maybe Lebedev is just frustrated because he has no evidence, and he let it slip out of anger. Or maybe he’s playing a longer game... he’s a smart bastard, I’ll give him that. Maybe he just wanted to scare you into thinking he knows everything. Cops have their own twisted psychology, my friend. But it’s sickening just thinking about it. Enough!"
He stopped and threw his hands up. "It’s degrading! I get it. But... look, since we’re finally laying our cards on the table (and thank God we are, it’s a relief), I’ll admit I noticed this undercurrent with them days ago. Just a whisper, of course. An implication. But how do they even have the nerve? What are they basing it on? God, you have no idea how furious I’ve been."
Viktor leaned in, his voice rising. "Just think about it! A starving clerk, half-mad from nervous exhaustion, practically delirious with a fever. A guy who is sensitive, proud, totally isolated—who hasn't had a real conversation in three months. He walks into a precinct wearing shoes with no soles and a coat falling apart at the seams. He has to deal with the sneers of petty officials. Then, boom—a demand for back rent shoved in his face. The landlord screaming. The stink of fresh varnish, the suffocating heat of the station, a crowd pressing in, and suddenly they’re gossiping about the violent robbery of a merchant he’d just visited that morning. All on an empty stomach! Of course he fainted! And that—that one moment of physical weakness—is their entire case! Damn them. If I were you, Leonid, I’d laugh in their faces. I’d spit at them. I’d strike back so fast it would put an end to this whole circus. It's an outrage."
He actually makes a decent argument, Leonid thought.
"Damn them?" Leonid muttered, his voice dripping with bitterness. "What about the summons for tomorrow? Do I really have to go back and justify myself to them again? I’m already sick to my stomach that I lowered myself to drink with that rat Sokolov at the tavern yesterday."
"To hell with that! I’ll go to Lebedev myself. I know the man. I’ll wring the truth right out of him and make him lay his cards on the table! And as for Sokolov..."
Finally, he sees him for what he is, Leonid thought.
"Wait!" Viktor grabbed Leonid’s arm, his eyes widening with sudden realization. "Hold on. You miscalculated. I just figured it out—you're completely wrong! How could that be a trap? You said the question about the shattered mirror was a trap. But if you had done it, would you have ever admitted to seeing the broken glass and the blood? Of course not. You would have played dumb, even if you’d stared right at it. Nobody willingly implicates themselves."
Leonid grimaced. Visible disgust washed over his face. "If I had done it, I absolutely would have said I saw the glass and the room."
"Why? Why testify against yourself?"
"Because only idiots and frightened amateurs deny everything. If a man has even half a brain, he admits to the physical facts that can’t be disproven. But he invents a different excuse for them. He spins the context, introduces some unexpected detail that changes the whole meaning and makes him look innocent. Lebedev would count on me doing exactly that. He’d expect me to claim I saw the mirror just to look honest, and then weave a lie around it."
"But he’d just snap back that the mirror wasn't broken until Tuesday night! That would prove you were there at the exact time of the robbery. He’d catch you on a technicality."
"Exactly," Leonid said softly. "That’s what he was banking on. That I’d be too rushed to think it through. That I'd panic, give the most plausible-sounding lie, and completely forget that the mirror had been whole until Tuesday."
"But how could a man forget something like that?"
"Nothing is easier."

Now and then I was happy: once in a week perhaps. At first, I expected there would be sad work persuading you to let me keep my word to Linton: for I had engaged to call again next day, when we quitted him; but, as you stayed upstairs on the morrow, I escaped that trouble. While Michael was refastening the lock of the park door in the afternoon, I got possession of the key, and told him how my cousin wished me to visit him, because he was sick, and couldn’t come to the Grange; and how papa would object to my going: and then I negotiated with him about the pony. He is fond of reading, and he thinks of leaving soon to get married; so he offered, if I would lend him books out of the library, to do what I wished: but I preferred giving him my own, and that satisfied him better. “On my second visit Linton seemed in lively spirits; and Zillah (that is their housekeeper) made us a clean room and a good fire, and told us that, as Joseph was out at a prayer-meeting and Hareton Earnshaw was off with his dogs—robbing our woods of pheasants, as I heard afterwards—we might do what we liked. She brought me some warm wine and gingerbread, and appeared exceedingly good-natured; and Linton sat in the arm-chair, and I in the little rocking chair on the hearth-stone, and we laughed and talked so merrily, and found so much to say: we planned where we would go, and what we would do in summer. I needn’t repeat that, because you would call it silly. “One time, however, we were near quarrelling. He said the pleasantest manner of spending a hot July day was lying from morning till evening on a bank of heath in the middle of the moors, with the bees humming dreamily about among the bloom, and the larks singing high up overhead, and the blue sky and bright sun shining steadily and cloudlessly.
