I feel less pride than discomfort.
I have only done what duty demanded.
Any man who wished as earnestly for victory
Has earned as much credit as I.
GENERAL. That is too severe a judgment.
The realm ought to know who secured this triumph.
To conceal worthy deeds serves no one.
Let me speak of them before the army,
Not as payment, but as simple truth.
VALERIUS. My wounds complain loudly enough.
They have no need of public remembrance.
GENERAL. Then perhaps they should complain of neglect instead.
Among all the wealth captured today—
horses, coin, and spoils of war—
you may choose a tenth share before any division is made.
VALERIUS. I am grateful, but I cannot accept.
The service of a soldier should not be bought afterward.
I ask only for the portion granted to every man who fought.
The soldiers erupt in cheers.
SOLDIERS. Valerius! Valerius!
VALERIUS. Enough.
You honour me beyond reason.
I happened to stand where danger was greatest;
that is all.
Many others showed equal courage and receive no such noise.
GENERAL. You are harder on yourself than any critic could be.
Still, whether you like it or not,
your actions deserve acknowledgment.
Before all present,
I give you my own warhorse,
known throughout the army.
And for your victory at Blackstone Gate,
let that place become part of your name.
Henceforth, be called Valerius Blackstone.
Trumpets sound.
ALL. Valerius Blackstone!
BLACKSTONE. Then I shall try to make the title worthy of itself.
GENERAL. Come. Reports must be sent before nightfall.
CAPTAIN. The messengers are ready.
BLACKSTONE. It seems I cannot escape owing a favour after all.
There is one thing I would ask.
GENERAL. Name it.
BLACKSTONE. During the campaign a poor townsman gave me shelter.
I saw him captured in the confusion of battle.
I ask that he be released.
GENERAL. Easily done.
For your sake, I would grant it without hesitation.
CAPTAIN. What is the man's name?
BLACKSTONE. I... cannot recall it.
War has left me more tired than I knew.
GENERAL. Then it is time you rested.
Come. The blood has dried upon your face.
They depart.
SCENE X. Outside the Fallen Fortress
Enter ARDEN with several battered soldiers.
ARDEN. The fortress has fallen.
SOLDIER. Negotiations are underway. Perhaps not all is lost.
ARDEN. Not all?
The walls are gone, the banners lowered.
I almost envy the men who conquered it.
Being among the defeated leaves little comfort.
They look toward the distant city as the scene ends.

But, step in, pray! Have you walked from Gimmerton this evening?”  “From the Grange,” I replied; “and while they make me lodging room there, I want to finish my business with your master; because I don’t think of having another opportunity in a hurry.”  “What business, sir?” said Nelly, conducting me into the house. “He’s gone out at present, and won’t return soon.”  “About the rent,” I answered. “Oh! then it is with Mrs. Heathcliff you must settle,” she observed; “or rather with me. She has not learnt to manage her affairs yet, and I act for her: there’s nobody else.”  I looked surprised. “Ah! you have not heard of Heathcliff’s death, I see,” she continued. “Heathcliff dead!” I exclaimed, astonished. “How long ago?”  “Three months since: but sit down, and let me take your hat, and I’ll tell you all about it. Stop, you have had nothing to eat, have you?”  “I want nothing: I have ordered supper at home. You sit down too. I never dreamt of his dying! Let me hear how it came to pass. You say you don’t expect them back for some time—the young people?”  “No—I have to scold them every evening for their late rambles: but they don’t care for me. At least, have a drink of our old ale; it will do you good: you seem weary.”  She hastened to fetch it before I could refuse, and I heard Joseph asking whether “it warn’t a crying scandal that she should have followers at her time of life? And then, to get them jocks out o’ t’ maister’s cellar! He fair shaamed to ’bide still and see it.”  She did not stay to retaliate, but re-entered in a minute, bearing a reaming silver pint, whose contents I lauded with becoming earnestness. And afterwards she furnished me with the sequel of Heathcliff’s history. He had a “queer” end, as she expressed it. * * * * *   I was summoned to Wuthering Heights, within a fortnight of your leaving us, she said; and I obeyed joyfully, for Catherine’s sake. My first interview with her grieved and shocked me: she had altered so much since our separation. Mr. Heathcliff did not explain his reasons for taking a new mind about my coming here; he only told me he wanted me, and he was tired of seeing Catherine: I must make the little parlour my sitting-room, and keep her with me. It was enough if he were obliged to see her once or twice a day. She seemed pleased at this arrangement; and, by degrees, I smuggled over a great number of books, and other articles, that had formed her amusement at the Grange; and flattered myself we should get on in tolerable comfort. The delusion did not last long. Catherine, contented at first, in a brief space grew irritable and restless. For one thing, she was forbidden to move out of the garden, and it fretted her sadly to be confined to its narrow bounds as spring drew on; for another, in following the house, I was forced to quit her frequently, and she complained of loneliness: she preferred quarrelling with Joseph in the kitchen to sitting at peace in her solitude. I did not mind their skirmishes: but Hareton was often obliged to seek the kitchen also, when the master wanted to have the house to himself; and though in the beginning she either left it at his approach, or quietly joined in my occupations, and shunned remarking or addressing him—and though he was always as sullen and silent as possible—after a while, she changed her behaviour, and became incapable of letting him alone: talking at him; commenting on his stupidity and idleness; expressing her wonder how he could endure the life he lived—how he could sit a whole evening staring into the fire, and dozing. “He’s just like a dog, is he not, Ellen?” she once observed, “or a cart-horse? He does his work, eats his food, and sleeps eternally! What a blank, dreary mind he must have! Do you ever dream, Hareton? And, if you do, what is it about? But you can’t speak to me!”  Then she looked at him; but he would neither open his mouth nor look again. “He’s, perhaps, dreaming now,” she continued. “He twitched his shoulder as Juno twitches hers. Ask him, Ellen.”  “Mr. Hareton will ask the master to send you upstairs, if you don’t behave!” I said.

“Come inside, sir, do,” said Mrs. Barton, holding the door wider. “You’ve come a fair distance this evening, I’ll wager.”
“From Ashcombe Hall,” I replied. “And since they are preparing a room for me there, I thought I would settle one small matter before retiring. I may not pass this way again for many months.”
“With whom?” she asked, leading me into the parlour.
“With Mr. Grey,” said I.
She paused and looked at me curiously.
“Then nobody has told you?”
“Told me what?”
“Mr. Grey has been dead these four months.”
I stared at her.
“Dead? Impossible! I saw him less than a year ago.”
“Aye, and a different man he was then. Sit down. You shall hear the whole story. Have you supped?”
“No.”
“Then let me bring something. You look tired enough.”
Before I could object, she disappeared. From the kitchen I heard old Matthew grumbling that “folk nowadays had no respect for decent hours, nor for decent company either.” Mrs. Barton ignored him entirely and returned with a tray carrying bread, cheese, and a steaming cup.
“You must eat while I talk,” she said.
I complied, and she began her account.
Not long after you departed, she said, I was sent for at Willow House. The summons surprised me, but I went willingly, for Miss Clara’s sake. I scarcely knew her when I arrived. She had grown pale and thin, and though she greeted me warmly, there was a sadness in her face that had not been there before.
Mr. Grey explained little. He merely informed me that Clara was to spend most of her time under my care. I was given the morning room, and she was encouraged to remain there with me. At first she appeared content. We arranged books, embroidery, and various little amusements, and for a while I believed we might pass the season pleasantly.
Yet her spirits soon changed. She disliked confinement and grew restless as summer approached. The grounds around the house were extensive, but she was forbidden to leave them, and restrictions of any sort were intolerable to her nature. Whenever I was occupied elsewhere, she complained bitterly of solitude.
There was another resident of the house, young Edward Hale. In the beginning she scarcely acknowledged him. If he entered a room, she would leave it; if he spoke, she answered only when politeness required. Edward, for his part, seemed perfectly willing to maintain the distance. He spent his days reading, working, or staring into the fire, speaking little to anyone.
Over time, however, Clara altered her approach.
One evening she watched him sitting silently beside the hearth.
“Do you suppose he ever grows tired of thinking?” she asked me. “Or perhaps he never thinks at all.”
Edward made no reply.
“Look at him, Mrs. Barton. He sits there hour after hour. If I were condemned to such a life, I should lose my senses. Tell me, Mr. Hale, what occupies your mind? Do you dream of adventures? Of distant countries? Or nothing at all?”
Still he said nothing.
“He heard me,” she continued. “I know he did. He frowned. That proves he possesses some spark of life.”
“Miss Clara,” I interrupted, “if you continue provoking him, you’ll soon regret it.”
At that Edward glanced up from his book, though whether in annoyance or amusement I could not tell.

“Step in, sir,” Mrs. Barton said. “You must be chilled after such a walk.”
“I’ve come from Ashcombe Hall,” I answered. “They’re making ready a room for me there, but before the evening ends I hoped to conclude a piece of business with Mr. Grey. I doubt I’ll have another chance soon.”
She stopped midway through the passage.
“With Mr. Grey?”
“Yes.”
A strange expression crossed her face.
“Then you haven’t heard.”
“Heard what?”
“That he died four months ago.”
For a moment I thought I had misunderstood her.
“Mr. Grey dead?”
She nodded quietly.
“Come and sit by the fire. I’ll tell you everything. You’ve not eaten, have you?”
I confessed that I had not. She disappeared into the kitchen, where an elderly servant could be heard muttering complaints about visitors, late hours, and the decline of manners. A few minutes later she returned with food and insisted I take it.
Only then did she begin.
I was called to Willow House shortly after your departure, she said. The request came unexpectedly, though I was glad enough to go because of Miss Clara. When I first saw her, the change startled me. She was thinner than before, less lively too, though she tried to conceal it.
Mr. Grey offered little explanation. He merely arranged matters so that Clara spent most of her time with me. At first it seemed a sensible plan. We filled the days with books and small occupations, and she appeared happier.
The improvement did not last.
As the weather warmed, she became increasingly impatient. Though she could walk through the gardens, she was not permitted beyond them, and the limitation weighed heavily on her. Worse still, I could not always keep her company. Whenever household duties drew me away, she complained of being abandoned.
There was also young Edward Hale. At first the two scarcely exchanged a word. He was quiet by nature and seemed content to remain unnoticed.
Gradually, though, Clara developed a habit of teasing him.
One evening she found him sitting by the fireplace as usual.
“Mrs. Barton,” she said, loud enough for him to hear, “do you think Mr. Hale ever dreams? Or does he simply sit and stare until bedtime?”
Edward continued reading.
“I cannot imagine such patience,” she went on. “If I spent my days that way, I should go mad. Tell me, sir—what do you think about all evening?”
Nothing.
“There,” she said triumphantly, “he refuses to answer because he has no answer.”
“Miss Clara,” I warned, “leave the poor gentleman alone.”
At that, Edward finally looked up. Whether he was irritated or secretly entertained by the exchange, I could never decide.

But who that was weak and unlearned could escape the deceits of both the princes of the state and the demons? 33. _That the times of all kings and kingdoms are ordained by the judgment and power of the true God._  Therefore that God, the author and giver of felicity, because He alone is the true God, Himself gives earthly kingdoms both to good and bad. Neither does He do this rashly, and, as it were, fortuitously,--because He is God, not fortune,--but according to the order of things and times, which is hidden from us, but thoroughly known to Himself; which same order of times, however, He does not serve as subject to it, but Himself rules as lord and appoints as governor. Felicity He gives only to the good. Whether a man be a subject or a king makes no difference: he may equally either possess or not possess it. And it shall be full in that life where kings and subjects exist no longer. And therefore earthly kingdoms are given by Him both to the good and the bad; lest His worshippers, still under the conduct of a very weak mind, should covet these gifts from Him as some great things. And this is the mystery of the Old Testament, in which the New was hidden, that there even earthly gifts are promised: those who were spiritual understanding even then, although not yet openly declaring, both the eternity which was symbolized by these earthly things, and in what gifts of God true felicity could be found. 34. _Concerning the kingdom of the Jews, which was founded by the one and true God, and preserved by Him as long as they remained in the true religion._  Therefore, that it might be known that these earthly good things, after which those pant who cannot imagine better things, remain in the power of the one God Himself, not of the many false gods whom the Romans have formerly believed worthy of worship, He multiplied His people in Egypt from being very few, and delivered them out of it by wonderful signs. Nor did their women invoke Lucina when their offspring was being incredibly multiplied; and that nation having increased incredibly, He Himself delivered, He Himself saved them from the hands of the Egyptians, who persecuted them, and wished to kill all their infants. Without the goddess Rumina they sucked; without Cunina they were cradled; without Educa and Potina they took food and drink; without all those puerile gods they were educated; without the nuptial gods they were married; without the worship of Priapus they had conjugal intercourse; without invocation of Neptune the divided sea opened up a way for them to pass over, and overwhelmed with its returning waves their enemies who pursued them. Neither did they consecrate any goddess Mannia when they received manna from heaven; nor, when the smitten rock poured forth water to them when they thirsted, did they worship Nymphs and Lymphs. Without the mad rites of Mars and Bellona they carried on war; and while, indeed, they did not conquer without victory, yet they did not hold it to be a goddess, but the gift of their God. Without Segetia they had harvests; without Bubona, oxen; honey without Mellona; apples without Pomona: and, in a word, everything for which the Romans thought they must supplicate so great a crowd of false gods, they received much more happily from the one true God. And if they had not sinned against Him with impious curiosity, which seduced them like magic arts, and drew them to strange gods and idols, and at last led them to kill Christ, their kingdom would have remained to them, and would have been, if not more spacious, yet more happy, than that of Rome. And now that they are dispersed through almost all lands and nations, it is through the providence of that one true God; that whereas the images, altars, groves, and temples of the false gods are everywhere overthrown, and their sacrifices prohibited, it may be shown from their books how this has been foretold by their prophets so long before; lest, perhaps, when they should be read in ours, they might seem to be invented by us. But now, reserving what is to follow for the following book, we must here set a bound to the prolixity of this one. FOOTNOTES:  [155] In Augustine's letter to Evodius (169), which was written towards the end of the year 415, he mentions that this fourth book and the following one were begun and finished during that same year.

27. That the rise and fall of nations are governed by divine providence rather than by chance or the favor of many gods.
If, therefore, kingdoms flourish or decline, not through the power of fortune but by the will of the supreme God, it follows that no earthly dominion exists apart from His judgment. Men frequently attribute prosperity to luck, to destiny, or to the favor of various deities; yet these names merely conceal their ignorance of the true cause. For God alone distributes power, establishes rulers, and determines the seasons in which nations grow strong or weak. These judgments are often hidden from human understanding, because our sight is confined to the present moment, whereas His wisdom comprehends the whole order of ages.
Nor should anyone suppose that worldly success is itself the highest good. Wealth, authority, and military strength are bestowed upon both the righteous and the wicked. Were they reserved only for the virtuous, the weak-minded might serve God merely in hope of temporal reward. By granting such things indiscriminately, He teaches that they are not the proper object of devotion. True blessedness belongs only to those who possess wisdom and justice, and this blessedness cannot be taken away by the loss of earthly possessions or political power.
28. Concerning a certain people who prospered under the worship of the one God and declined when they abandoned Him.
To make this truth more evident, let us consider a nation that was once small and insignificant, yet rose to great strength under the guidance of the one God. It was not through the aid of many divine patrons that they increased, nor by ceremonies directed toward separate gods of fertility, harvest, war, or navigation. Their fields yielded crops, their families multiplied, and their enemies were overcome without the multitude of rites that other nations considered necessary.
They crossed rivers without invoking river gods, harvested grain without offerings to guardians of the fields, and obtained victory without believing victory itself to be a goddess. The blessings they received came not from a divided host of celestial powers, but from the single source from which all good things proceed. What others sought through countless ceremonies, they obtained through obedience.
Yet prosperity did not preserve them from error. As often happens among men, abundance produced pride, and pride gave birth to curiosity concerning foreign customs and strange forms of worship. Gradually they turned away from the laws that had sustained them and trusted in inventions of their own making. Their decline followed, not because the power that had raised them failed, but because they abandoned the wisdom by which they had been governed.
Even their later misfortunes served a purpose. Scattered among many lands, they became witnesses to ancient prophecies and preserved records that testified to events long foretold. Thus what appeared to be punishment also became an instrument of providence, demonstrating that divine purposes often extend beyond the understanding of those through whom they are accomplished.
Having treated this matter sufficiently for the present discussion, we shall reserve the remaining questions for a later examination, lest the length of this discourse exceed proper measure.

AI-

27. On the government of kingdoms by the will of God rather than by fortune
When nations rise to greatness or sink into weakness, people commonly search for visible explanations. Some praise fortune, others blame fate, and many imagine that success comes through the favor of various divine powers. Yet such explanations mistake effects for causes. The true source of authority and dominion lies in the providence of God, who appoints rulers, measures the duration of kingdoms, and orders events according to a wisdom that human beings perceive only in part.
Because His purposes are often hidden, men are tempted to think events occur at random. But what seems confused to us is not confused to Him. The sequence of ages, the growth of empires, and the fall of dynasties belong to an order known fully only by the one who governs it.
For this reason earthly prosperity should never be mistaken for the highest blessing. Power, riches, and victory are possessed by good men and bad alike. If such advantages belonged exclusively to the righteous, many would pursue virtue for reward rather than for its own sake. God therefore allows temporal goods to be shared widely, while reserving true happiness for those whose hearts are rightly ordered. That happiness depends neither on wealth nor rank and cannot be destroyed by changes in worldly circumstances.
28. How one nation demonstrated the sufficiency of the one God
There once existed a people whose history plainly illustrates this principle. Their beginnings were modest, yet they increased in number and strength. Unlike neighboring nations, they did not divide the functions of life among a multitude of gods. They did not seek one deity for harvests, another for war, and another for safe journeys. Nevertheless they prospered.
Their crops grew, their households expanded, and they survived dangers that might easily have destroyed them. What others pursued through elaborate rituals, they received through dependence upon a single divine ruler. Their success served as a lesson that the source of blessing is not multiplied by multiplying objects of worship.
In time, however, success bred complacency. Familiar prosperity weakened gratitude. They became attracted to foreign practices and gradually abandoned the principles that had distinguished them. Their decline followed this departure. The change did not reveal weakness in the God who had guided them; rather, it exposed the instability of human loyalty.
Even so, their story did not end with their downfall. Though dispersed among many regions, they carried with them traditions and writings that preserved the memory of earlier promises and predictions. In this way their misfortune became part of a larger design. What appeared to contemporaries as mere ruin later served as evidence of a providence working through centuries rather than moments.
Here, however, it is fitting to conclude. The remaining matters belong more properly to another discussion and would carry this one beyond reasonable bounds.

OLD LADY. In faith, for little England You’d venture an emballing. I myself Would for Caernarfonshire, although there longed No more to th’ crown but that. Lo, who comes here? Enter Lord Chamberlain. CHAMBERLAIN. Good morrow, ladies. What were’t worth to know The secret of your conference? ANNE. My good lord, Not your demand; it values not your asking. Our mistress’ sorrows we were pitying. CHAMBERLAIN. It was a gentle business, and becoming The action of good women. There is hope All will be well. ANNE. Now, I pray God, amen! CHAMBERLAIN. You bear a gentle mind, and heavenly blessings Follow such creatures. That you may, fair lady, Perceive I speak sincerely, and high note’s Ta’en of your many virtues, the King’s Majesty Commends his good opinion of you, and Does purpose honour to you no less flowing Than Marchioness of Pembroke, to which title A thousand pound a year annual support Out of his grace he adds. ANNE. I do not know What kind of my obedience I should tender. More than my all is nothing; nor my prayers Are not words duly hallowed, nor my wishes More worth than empty vanities; yet prayers and wishes Are all I can return. Beseech your lordship, Vouchsafe to speak my thanks and my obedience, As from a blushing handmaid, to his Highness, Whose health and royalty I pray for. CHAMBERLAIN. Lady, I shall not fail t’ approve the fair conceit The King hath of you. [_Aside_.] I have perused her well. Beauty and honour in her are so mingled That they have caught the King; and who knows yet But from this lady may proceed a gem To lighten all this isle? I’ll to the King, And say I spoke with you. ANNE. My honoured lord. [_Exit Lord Chamberlain._]  OLD LADY. Why, this it is: see, see! I have been begging sixteen years in court, Am yet a courtier beggarly, nor could Come pat betwixt too early and too late For any suit of pounds; and you, O fate! A very fresh fish here—fie, fie, fie upon This compelled fortune!—have your mouth filled up Before you open it. ANNE. This is strange to me. OLD LADY. How tastes it? Is it bitter? Forty pence, no. There was a lady once—’tis an old story— That would not be a queen, that would she not, For all the mud in Egypt. Have you heard it? ANNE. Come, you are pleasant. OLD LADY. With your theme, I could O’ermount the lark. The Marchioness of Pembroke? A thousand pounds a year for pure respect? No other obligation? By my life, That promises more thousands; honour’s train Is longer than his foreskirt. By this time I know your back will bear a duchess. Say, Are you not stronger than you were? ANNE. Good lady, Make yourself mirth with your particular fancy, And leave me out on’t. Would I had no being If this salute my blood a jot. It faints me To think what follows. The Queen is comfortless, and we forgetful In our long absence. Pray do not deliver What here you’ve heard to her. OLD LADY. What do you think me? [_Exeunt._]  SCENE IV. A hall in Blackfriars. Trumpets, sennet, and cornets. Enter two Vergers, with short silver wands; next them, two Scribes, in the habit of doctors; after them, the Archbishop of Canterbury alone; after him, the Bishops of Lincoln, Ely, Rochester, and Saint Asaph; next them, with some small distance, follows a Gentleman bearing the purse with the great seal, and a cardinal’s hat; then two Priests, bearing each a silver cross; then a Gentleman Usher bare-headed, accompanied with a Sergeant-at-arms bearing a silver mace; then two Gentlemen, bearing two great silver pillars; after them, side by side, the two Cardinals; two Noblemen with the sword and mace. The King takes place under the cloth of state. The two Cardinals sit under him as judges. The Queen takes place some distance from the King. The Bishops place themselves on each side the court, in manner of consistory; below them the Scribes. The Lords sit next the Bishops. The rest of the Attendants stand in convenient order about the stage. WOLSEY. Whilst our commission from Rome is read, Let silence be commanded. KING. What’s the need? It hath already publicly been read, And on all sides th’ authority allowed; You may then spare that time. WOLSEY. Be’t so. Proceed. SCRIBE. Say, “Henry King of England, come into the court.”  CRIER.

LADY MARGARET. Indeed, for less than half the kingdom some women would hazard much. But look, who approaches?
Enter Lord Steward.
STEWARD. Good morrow, ladies. Have I interrupted matters of great secrecy?
ELIZABETH. Nothing, my lord, that would reward your curiosity. We were speaking of our mistress and lamenting her troubles.
STEWARD. A charitable occupation, and fitting for gentlewomen. Yet take comfort. Affairs may still turn toward a happier conclusion.
ELIZABETH. Heaven grant it.
STEWARD. Your compassion does you credit, lady. And since I find you in such virtuous disposition, I am charged to deliver a message. His Majesty has spoken favourably of you. In recognition of your merit, he intends to raise you to the title of Countess of Wexford, with revenues suitable to the dignity.
ELIZABETH. My lord, you leave me uncertain how to answer. Such honour exceeds anything I have deserved. I possess no gift equal to so great a kindness, except my gratitude and humble prayers for the King's prosperity.
STEWARD. I shall faithfully convey those sentiments.
Aside.
She carries herself with admirable grace. It is no wonder the King regards her so highly. Fortune has curious habits; today's attendant may become tomorrow's ornament of the realm.
Aloud.
Farewell, lady.
ELIZABETH. My thanks, my lord.
Exit Steward.
LADY MARGARET. There! Do you see how the world proceeds? I have spent twenty years pursuing favour at court and have worn out more shoes than I care to count. Yet fortune barely nods at me. Meanwhile you arrive, and honours are placed in your lap before you have learned how to refuse them.
ELIZABETH. It all seems unreal.
LADY MARGARET. Unreal? Does it taste unpleasant? A title, an income, the King's notice? I have never heard anyone choke on such things.
ELIZABETH. You make sport of me.
LADY MARGARET. Because the matter invites it. A countess today. Who shall say what comes tomorrow? If ambition were a ladder, I suspect you have already set foot upon the first rung.
ELIZABETH. I wish you would leave me out of these predictions. They bring me no comfort.
LADY MARGARET. Then they bring you more modesty than most courtiers possess.
ELIZABETH. The Queen suffers enough already. I would not add to her grief by becoming the subject of idle speculation. I beg you, speak of this to no one.
LADY MARGARET. Have no fear. My tongue can keep a secret when it chooses.
Exeunt.

SCENE IV. A Great Chamber of State
Trumpets sound.
Enter Officers, Clerks, Bishops, Lords, and Attendants in solemn procession. The KING sits beneath a canopy. Two CHANCELLORS sit below him as judges. The QUEEN is seated apart.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Since our authority has been confirmed by the council, let the proceedings begin in proper order.
KING. Must every formality be repeated? The commission has already been read before all concerned. Let us proceed to the matter itself.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. As Your Majesty wishes.
CLERK. Call the parties before the court.
CRIER. Let all who are summoned attend and answer.
SECOND CHANCELLOR. Let silence be observed throughout the hall.
The assembly grows quiet.
KING. We are present. What remains?
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Only that justice may be seen as well as done. The record shall show that all parties stand before this tribunal by lawful authority.
QUEEN. I trust, then, that fairness shall accompany authority.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Your Grace shall have every opportunity to speak.
KING. Let no man imagine the outcome settled before the hearing. We sit here to discover truth, not to pronounce it beforehand.
A murmur passes among the lords.
SECOND CHANCELLOR. Then let the examination begin.
The court prepares to proceed.

LADY MARGARET. Some women would risk far more for far less. Ah—but here comes company.
Enter Lord Steward.
STEWARD. Good morning, ladies. Have I stumbled into a secret council?
ELIZABETH. Nothing so important, my lord. We were only discussing our mistress and wishing her better fortune.
STEWARD. A generous subject. And perhaps not a hopeless one.
ELIZABETH. I pray you are right.
STEWARD. Since we speak of fortune, I carry a message that concerns you. His Majesty has taken particular notice of your conduct and desires to reward it. He intends to create you Countess of Wexford and provide an estate suitable to the title.
ELIZABETH. My lord, I scarcely know how to respond. Such generosity leaves me indebted beyond my ability to repay. All I can offer is my humble thanks and my continued prayers for the King's health.
STEWARD. They will be well received.
Aside.
The King is not alone in admiring her. Beauty and dignity seldom meet so comfortably in one person.
Aloud.
I take my leave.
ELIZABETH. You honour me, my lord.
Exit Steward.
LADY MARGARET. Well! There is justice for you. I have spent half my life chasing favour through corridors and antechambers, and what have I gained? A collection of disappointments. Yet you stand still, and honours come running toward you.
ELIZABETH. It feels very strange.
LADY MARGARET. Strange? Most people would call it delightful. A title, an income, royal approval—few recipes produce a bitter dish from those ingredients.
ELIZABETH. You are determined to laugh at me.
LADY MARGARET. Only because the situation insists upon it. Countess today; who knows what tomorrow may bring?
ELIZABETH. Please, enough. Such talk troubles me more than it pleases me.
LADY MARGARET. Then you possess a rare quality at court.
ELIZABETH. The Queen has troubles enough. I would not willingly become another source of pain for her. Promise me you will not repeat what was said.
LADY MARGARET. My discretion is occasionally better than my reputation suggests.
They depart.

SCENE IV. A Hall of Judgment
Trumpets announce the entrance of officials, clergy, and nobles. The KING takes his seat beneath a canopy, while the QUEEN sits at some distance.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Let order be kept while these proceedings begin.
KING. We need not rehearse what has already been heard. The commission is known to everyone present.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Very well.
CLERK. Summon those required before the court.
CRIER. Let all concerned attend and answer.
The hall falls silent.
SECOND CHANCELLOR. The court is assembled.
KING. Then let us not waste time.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. The record shall note that this hearing proceeds under lawful authority and with the consent of all required parties.
QUEEN. Authority is valuable. Justice is more valuable still.
FIRST CHANCELLOR. Both shall be observed, Your Grace.
KING. That is my desire as well. Let no one assume the matter settled before it is heard.
A few lords exchange glances.
SECOND CHANCELLOR. Then we may begin.
The hearing proceeds as the court watches in expectation.

I assure you it is very refreshing after sitting so long in one attitude.”  Elizabeth was surprised, but agreed to it immediately. Miss Bingley succeeded no less in the real object of her civility: Mr. Darcy looked up. He was as much awake to the novelty of attention in that quarter as Elizabeth herself could be, and unconsciously closed his book. He was directly invited to join their party, but he declined it, observing that he could imagine but two motives for their choosing to walk up and down the room together, with either of which motives his joining them would interfere. What could he mean? She was dying to know what could be his meaning--and asked Elizabeth whether she could at all understand him. “Not at all,” was her answer; “but, depend upon it, he means to be severe on us, and our surest way of disappointing him will be to ask nothing about it.”  Miss Bingley, however, was incapable of disappointing Mr. Darcy in anything, and persevered, therefore, in requiring an explanation of his two motives. “I have not the smallest objection to explaining them,” said he, as soon as she allowed him to speak. “You either choose this method of passing the evening because you are in each other’s confidence, and have secret affairs to discuss, or because you are conscious that your figures appear to the greatest advantage in walking: if the first, I should be completely in your way; and if the second, I can admire you much better as I sit by the fire.”  “Oh, shocking!” cried Miss Bingley. “I never heard anything so abominable. How shall we punish him for such a speech?”  “Nothing so easy, if you have but the inclination,” said Elizabeth. “We can all plague and punish one another. Tease him--laugh at him. Intimate as you are, you must know how it is to be done.”  “But upon my honour I do _not_. I do assure you that my intimacy has not yet taught me _that_. Tease calmness of temper and presence of mind! No, no; I feel he may defy us there. And as to laughter, we will not expose ourselves, if you please, by attempting to laugh without a subject. Mr. Darcy may hug himself.”  “Mr. Darcy is not to be laughed at!” cried Elizabeth. “That is an uncommon advantage, and uncommon I hope it will continue, for it would be a great loss to _me_ to have many such acquaintance. I dearly love a laugh.”  “Miss Bingley,” said he, “has given me credit for more than can be. The wisest and best of men,--nay, the wisest and best of their actions,--may be rendered ridiculous by a person whose first object in life is a joke.”  “Certainly,” replied Elizabeth, “there are such people, but I hope I am not one of _them_. I hope I never ridicule what is wise or good. Follies and nonsense, whims and inconsistencies, _do_ divert me, I own, and I laugh at them whenever I can. But these, I suppose, are precisely what you are without.”  “Perhaps that is not possible for anyone. But it has been the study of my life to avoid those weaknesses which often expose a strong understanding to ridicule.”  “Such as vanity and pride.”  “Yes, vanity is a weakness indeed. But pride--where there is a real superiority of mind--pride will be always under good regulation.”  Elizabeth turned away to hide a smile. “Your examination of Mr. Darcy is over, I presume,” said Miss Bingley; “and pray what is the result?”  “I am perfectly convinced by it that Mr. Darcy has no defect. He owns it himself without disguise.”  “No,” said Darcy, “I have made no such pretension. I have faults enough, but they are not, I hope, of understanding. My temper I dare not vouch for. It is, I believe, too little yielding; certainly too little for the convenience of the world. I cannot forget the follies and vices of others so soon as I ought, nor their offences against myself. My feelings are not puffed about with every attempt to move them. My temper would perhaps be called resentful. My good opinion once lost is lost for ever.”  “_That_ is a failing, indeed!” cried Elizabeth. “Implacable resentment _is_ a shade in a character. But you have chosen your fault well. I really cannot _laugh_ at it. You are safe from me.”  “There is, I believe, in every disposition a tendency to some particular evil, a natural defect, which not even the best education can overcome.”  “And _your_ defect is a propensity to hate everybody.”  “And yours,” he replied, with a smile, “is wilfully to misunderstand them.”  “Do let us have a little music,” cried Miss Bingley, tired of a conversation in which she had no share.

“It is a relief to move about after sitting still for so long,” said Miss Harrington.
Charlotte agreed readily, though she suspected there was more civility than necessity in the proposal. The true purpose, however, was not entirely unsuccessful. Mr. Ashford, who had been absorbed in a volume near the fire, raised his eyes from the page. The movement in his direction had not escaped him. After a moment he closed the book and listened.
“You must join us,” said Miss Harrington.
“I fear I cannot,” he replied. “Your walk appears to be founded on one of two intentions, and my presence would spoil either.”
The remark instantly awakened curiosity.
“What intentions?” cried Miss Harrington. “You must explain yourself.”
Charlotte smiled.
“Do not encourage him. He wishes to provoke us, and the surest punishment is to let him keep his secret.”
But Miss Harrington was not so easily discouraged.
“No, indeed. I insist upon an explanation.”
“With pleasure,” said Mr. Ashford. “Either you have chosen to pace the room because you have confidential matters to discuss, in which case I should be an intrusion; or you imagine that walking displays you to greater advantage than sitting, in which case I can admire the exhibition much better from where I am.”
“How intolerable!” exclaimed Miss Harrington. “What shall be done with him?”
“There are many forms of revenge,” said Charlotte. “Ridicule is among the easiest. Surely you know enough of his character to laugh at him effectively.”
“I assure you I do not,” replied Miss Harrington. “One cannot easily laugh at a person who refuses to provide material. Mr. Ashford is entirely too composed.”
“Then he enjoys a rare privilege,” said Charlotte. “Few people are safe from ridicule. I confess I value a laugh too highly not to employ it whenever I can.”
“You give ridicule too honourable a place,” said Mr. Ashford. “The wisest actions may be made absurd by someone determined to see nothing else.”
“True,” said Charlotte, “but I hope I am not one of those people. I do not laugh at wisdom. Folly, however, is another matter. Contradictions, vanities, little absurdities—those are fair game. And I imagine you possess none of them.”
“No person is entirely free from weakness.”
“Vanity, perhaps?”
“Certainly vanity.”
“And pride?”
“Pride, if governed by reason, is less dangerous than vanity.”
Charlotte turned aside to conceal a smile.
“Your investigation seems complete,” said Miss Harrington. “What judgment have you reached?”
“That Mr. Ashford considers himself remarkably free from defects.”
“Not at all,” said he. “I know several of mine. My temper, for example, is not especially forgiving. I do not easily forget injuries, nor do I readily alter an opinion once formed. Some would call me stubborn.”
“That is indeed a fault,” Charlotte answered. “An unwillingness to forgive is never attractive. Still, it is not a fault I find amusing, so you remain secure.”
“I suspect everyone has some natural weakness.”
“And yours is a tendency to think poorly of people.”
“And yours,” he replied with a faint smile, “is a determination to misinterpret them.”
Before Charlotte could answer, Miss Harrington rose abruptly.
“Someone must play the pianoforte,” she declared. “I am exhausted by conversations in which I contribute so little.”

“It feels impossible to sit still any longer,” said Miss Harrington. “Come, let us walk about the room.”
Charlotte joined her at once. The invitation had another purpose besides exercise, and that purpose soon succeeded. Mr. Ashford looked up from the book he had been reading. A moment later, he shut it altogether.
“You ought to join us,” Miss Harrington said.
“I think not.”
“And why?”
“Because your walk is prompted by one of two reasons, and I should interfere with both.”
Naturally, this produced immediate curiosity.
Charlotte laughed. “Pay no attention. He means to puzzle us.”
“That is not enough,” said Miss Harrington. “I demand an explanation.”
Mr. Ashford obliged.
“You are either exchanging secrets, which would make me an inconvenience, or you believe the room offers an excellent stage upon which to display yourselves. In that case, I prefer my current seat as a viewing position.”
Miss Harrington stared at him.
“Really, Mr. Ashford, you are impossible.”
“There is a simple remedy,” Charlotte said. “Laugh at him.”
“If only it were so easy. He never supplies the necessary material.”
“Then he possesses an enviable advantage,” Charlotte replied. “Most people are generous providers of nonsense. I enjoy discovering it.”
Mr. Ashford smiled slightly.
“That habit has ruined many fair judgments. A determined wit can find something ridiculous in almost anything.”
“Almost anything,” Charlotte agreed. “But not everything. I hope I know the difference between genuine merit and mere absurdity. It is the latter that entertains me.”
“And you suppose I have none?”
“I suspect you try very hard not to.”
“No one succeeds entirely.”
“Not even with vanity?”
“Certainly not.”
“Or pride?”
“At least pride may be controlled.”
That answer nearly made Charlotte laugh outright.
Miss Harrington, who had followed the exchange with growing impatience, interrupted.
“Well? What have you discovered about him?”
Charlotte considered.
“He appears to regard himself as nearly faultless.”
“I said nothing of the kind,” said Mr. Ashford. “I am perfectly aware of my defects. My opinions are slow to change. Injuries are slower still to leave my memory. Once I think poorly of someone, I rarely think well again.”
“A serious flaw,” Charlotte replied. “Though not a humorous one. I cannot mock you for it.”
“Every character has its weakness.”
“Then yours is distrusting people.”
He met her eyes.
“And yours is misunderstanding them on purpose.”
For a second neither spoke.
Miss Harrington broke the silence with visible relief.
“Enough philosophy. Let us have music. It is far more agreeable than being ignored in one's own drawing room.”

Let me speak proudly: tell the Constable We are but warriors for the working-day. Our gayness and our gilt are all besmirch’d With rainy marching in the painful field; There’s not a piece of feather in our host— Good argument, I hope, we will not fly— And time hath worn us into slovenry; But, by the mass, our hearts are in the trim; And my poor soldiers tell me, yet ere night They’ll be in fresher robes, or they will pluck The gay new coats o’er the French soldiers’ heads And turn them out of service. If they do this— As, if God please, they shall,—my ransom then Will soon be levied. Herald, save thou thy labour. Come thou no more for ransom, gentle herald. They shall have none, I swear, but these my joints; Which if they have as I will leave ’em them, Shall yield them little, tell the Constable. MONTJOY. I shall, King Harry. And so fare thee well; Thou never shalt hear herald any more. [_Exit._]  KING HENRY. I fear thou’lt once more come again for ransom. Enter York. YORK. My lord, most humbly on my knee I beg The leading of the vaward. KING HENRY. Take it, brave York. Now, soldiers, march away; And how thou pleasest, God, dispose the day! [_Exeunt._]  SCENE IV. The field of battle. Alarum. Excursions. Enter Pistol, French Soldier and Boy. PISTOL. Yield, cur! FRENCH SOLDIER. _Je pense que vous êtes le gentilhomme de bonne qualité._  PISTOL. _Qualité? Caleno custore me!_ Art thou a gentleman? What is thy name? Discuss. FRENCH SOLDIER. _O Seigneur Dieu!_  PISTOL. O, Signieur Dew should be a gentleman. Perpend my words, O Signieur Dew, and mark: O Signieur Dew, thou diest on point of fox, Except, O signieur, thou do give to me Egregious ransom. FRENCH SOLDIER. _O, prenez miséricorde! Ayez pitié de moi!_  PISTOL. Moy shall not serve; I will have forty moys, Or I will fetch thy rim out at thy throat In drops of crimson blood. FRENCH SOLDIER. _Est-il impossible d’échapper la force de ton bras?_  PISTOL. Brass, cur! Thou damned and luxurious mountain goat, Offer’st me brass? FRENCH SOLDIER. _O pardonnez-moi!_  PISTOL. Say’st thou me so? Is that a ton of moys? Come hither, boy; ask me this slave in French What is his name. BOY. _Écoutez. Comment êtes-vous appelé?_  FRENCH SOLDIER. _Monsieur le Fer._  BOY. He says his name is Master Fer. PISTOL. Master Fer! I’ll fer him, and firk him, and ferret him. Discuss the same in French unto him. BOY. I do not know the French for fer, and ferret, and firk. PISTOL. Bid him prepare; for I will cut his throat. FRENCH SOLDIER. _Que dit-il, monsieur?_  BOY. _Il me commande à vous dire que vous faites vous prêt, car ce soldat ici est disposé tout à cette heure de couper votre gorge._  PISTOL. Owy, cuppele gorge, permafoy, Peasant, unless thou give me crowns, brave crowns; Or mangled shalt thou be by this my sword. FRENCH SOLDIER. _O, je vous supplie, pour l’amour de Dieu, me pardonner! Je suis le gentilhomme de bonne maison; gardez ma vie, et je vous donnerai deux cents écus._  PISTOL. What are his words? BOY. He prays you to save his life. He is a gentleman of a good house; and for his ransom he will give you two hundred crowns. PISTOL. Tell him my fury shall abate, and I The crowns will take. FRENCH SOLDIER. _Petit monsieur, que dit-il?_  BOY. _Encore qu’il est contre son jurement de pardonner aucun prisonnier; néanmoins, pour les écus que vous lui avez promis, il est content à vous donner la liberté, le franchisement._  FRENCH SOLDIER. _Sur mes genoux je vous donne mille remerciements; et je m’estime heureux que je suis tombé entre les mains d’un chevalier, je pense, le plus brave, vaillant, et très distingué seigneur d’Angleterre._  PISTOL. Expound unto me, boy. BOY. He gives you upon his knees, a thousand thanks; and he esteems himself happy that he hath fallen into the hands of one, as he thinks, the most brave, valorous, and thrice-worthy _seigneur_ of England. PISTOL. As I suck blood, I will some mercy show. Follow me! BOY. _Suivez-vous le grand capitaine._  [_Exeunt Pistol and French Soldier._]  I did never know so full a voice issue from so empty a heart; but the saying is true, “The empty vessel makes the greatest sound.” Bardolph and Nym had ten times more valour than this roaring devil i’ the old play, that everyone may pare his nails with a wooden dagger; and they are both hang’d; and so would this be, if he durst steal anything adventurously.

CAPTAIN. Speak boldly to their marshal. Tell him this:
We are no soldiers dressed for festival,
But men acquainted with the dust of roads.
Our cloaks are stained with weather, mud, and toil;
The bright devices that adorned our ranks
Have long since faded under wind and rain.
Yet let him mark this well: though garments fail,
Our spirits keep their edge. Before this sun
Has left the field, my men have sworn to me
They shall wear finer colours than they do now,
Or strip them from the backs of those opposed.
If fortune grants us victory, as I trust,
Then ransom will be gathered easily.
Return no more with offers or demands.
I pledge no payment save these weary limbs,
And if they fall into their enemies' hands,
The gain will hardly justify the trouble.
HERALD. I shall report your answer faithfully.
Farewell, good captain.
Exit Herald.
CAPTAIN. Yet I suspect we have not seen the last of him.
Enter LORD PEMBROKE.
PEMBROKE. My lord, I ask the honour of leading the first assault.
CAPTAIN. The honour is yours.
Now forward, gentlemen.
The day belongs to Heaven.
Exeunt.
SCENE IV. A Battlefield
Alarum. Enter RALPH, a captured Enemy Officer, and THOMAS, a young attendant.
RALPH. Surrender, rogue!
OFFICER. Good sir, have mercy.
RALPH. Mercy? That depends upon your purse.
What are you called?
OFFICER. I am Captain Duval.
RALPH. A captain! Then your value rises.
Young Thomas, ask him whether he can pay.
THOMAS. Sir, my master wishes to know what ransom you offer.
OFFICER. Whatever is reasonable. Only spare my life.
RALPH. Hear him! He bargains already.
Tell him I am a fierce and dreadful warrior,
And not a man to be trifled with.
THOMAS. I shall tell him no such thing.
RALPH. Insolent boy! Do as you are bid.
THOMAS. Very well.
To Officer.
He says you should be frightened.
OFFICER. I am frightened enough.
RALPH. Good. Then mention a number.
OFFICER. Two hundred crowns.
RALPH. Two hundred?
The offer improves.
Ask whether he possesses more.
THOMAS. Do you?
OFFICER. Yes, if my family contributes.
RALPH. Excellent.
Inform him his life is spared.
THOMAS. He says he will release you.
OFFICER. Then I thank him sincerely.
I shall remember his generosity.
RALPH. What does he say?
THOMAS. He calls you generous.
RALPH. A just description.
Come, prisoner, follow me.
Exeunt Ralph and Officer.
THOMAS remains.
THOMAS. I have never witnessed so much noise proceeding from so little courage.
He boasts as though he had conquered kingdoms,
Yet trembles whenever real danger appears.
There are humbler men in the army
Who possess ten times his bravery
And not a fraction of his words.
Exit.

CAPTAIN. Carry this answer back to your commander.
Tell him we are soldiers, not courtiers.
Rain, mud, and hard marching have done their work on us.
The bright feathers are gone, the fine coats ruined,
and there is little splendour left in the army's appearance.
Still, appearances matter less than resolve.
My men believe that before night falls
they will either be dressed more richly than they are now,
or leave the field without need of clothing at all.
Should victory favour us,
ransom will take care of itself.
So save yourself another journey.
The only payment I promise is my own body,
and there is not much profit in that.
HERALD. I will deliver your words.
Exit.
CAPTAIN. Though I think he'll return before this business ends.
Enter LORD PEMBROKE.
PEMBROKE. Grant me the first advance, my lord.
CAPTAIN. Gladly.
Forward now.
Let Heaven determine the outcome.
They exit.

SCENE IV. Amid the Battle
Enter RALPH, dragging a captured officer. THOMAS follows.
RALPH. Yield yourself!
OFFICER. Sir, I surrender.
RALPH. Sensible at last.
Now tell me your name.
OFFICER. Captain Duval.
RALPH. A captain? Then you must be worth keeping alive.
Thomas, ask him what his freedom is worth.
THOMAS. My master wishes to know your ransom.
OFFICER. I will pay what I can. Only spare me.
RALPH. You hear that? The man values life highly.
Tell him he stands before a warrior of fearsome reputation.
THOMAS. I doubt that will be necessary.
RALPH. Do as you're told.
THOMAS. As you wish.
To the Officer.
My master would like you to be impressed.
OFFICER. I am chiefly concerned with surviving.
RALPH. A practical fellow.
Well then, what sum?
OFFICER. Two hundred crowns.
RALPH. Better.
Perhaps there is more where that came from.
THOMAS. Is there?
OFFICER. My family could provide additional funds.
RALPH. Then I grow merciful.
Tell him he may keep his throat uncut.
THOMAS. He accepts your offer and grants your release.
OFFICER. I am deeply grateful.
RALPH. What gratitude does he express?
THOMAS. He believes himself fortunate.
RALPH. A wise judgment.
Come along.
Ralph leads the prisoner away.
THOMAS watches them go.
THOMAS. It is remarkable how loudly some men advertise their courage.
The fellow talks like a conqueror and bargains like a merchant.
I've known quieter soldiers who would face twice the danger with half the fuss.
The world often mistakes noise for bravery.
Exit.

_Assez causé!_ Till we meet again!”  He went out, trembling all over from a sort of wild hysterical sensation, in which there was an element of insufferable rapture. Yet he was gloomy and terribly tired. His face was twisted as after a fit. His fatigue increased rapidly. Any shock, any irritating sensation stimulated and revived his energies at once, but his strength failed as quickly when the stimulus was removed. Zametov, left alone, sat for a long time in the same place, plunged in thought. Raskolnikov had unwittingly worked a revolution in his brain on a certain point and had made up his mind for him conclusively. “Ilya Petrovitch is a blockhead,” he decided. Raskolnikov had hardly opened the door of the restaurant when he stumbled against Razumihin on the steps. They did not see each other till they almost knocked against each other. For a moment they stood looking each other up and down. Razumihin was greatly astounded, then anger, real anger gleamed fiercely in his eyes. “So here you are!” he shouted at the top of his voice--“you ran away from your bed! And here I’ve been looking for you under the sofa! We went up to the garret. I almost beat Nastasya on your account. And here he is after all. Rodya! What is the meaning of it? Tell me the whole truth! Confess! Do you hear?”  “It means that I’m sick to death of you all and I want to be alone,” Raskolnikov answered calmly. “Alone? When you are not able to walk, when your face is as white as a sheet and you are gasping for breath! Idiot!... What have you been doing in the Palais de Cristal? Own up at once!”  “Let me go!” said Raskolnikov and tried to pass him. This was too much for Razumihin; he gripped him firmly by the shoulder. “Let you go? You dare tell me to let you go? Do you know what I’ll do with you directly? I’ll pick you up, tie you up in a bundle, carry you home under my arm and lock you up!”  “Listen, Razumihin,” Raskolnikov began quietly, apparently calm--“can’t you see that I don’t want your benevolence? A strange desire you have to shower benefits on a man who... curses them, who feels them a burden in fact! Why did you seek me out at the beginning of my illness? Maybe I was very glad to die. Didn’t I tell you plainly enough to-day that you were torturing me, that I was... sick of you! You seem to want to torture people! I assure you that all that is seriously hindering my recovery, because it’s continually irritating me. You saw Zossimov went away just now to avoid irritating me. You leave me alone too, for goodness’ sake! What right have you, indeed, to keep me by force? Don’t you see that I am in possession of all my faculties now? How, how can I persuade you not to persecute me with your kindness? I may be ungrateful, I may be mean, only let me be, for God’s sake, let me be! Let me be, let me be!”  He began calmly, gloating beforehand over the venomous phrases he was about to utter, but finished, panting for breath, in a frenzy, as he had been with Luzhin. Razumihin stood a moment, thought and let his hand drop. “Well, go to hell then,” he said gently and thoughtfully. “Stay,” he roared, as Raskolnikov was about to move. “Listen to me. Let me tell you, that you are all a set of babbling, posing idiots! If you’ve any little trouble you brood over it like a hen over an egg. And you are plagiarists even in that! There isn’t a sign of independent life in you! You are made of spermaceti ointment and you’ve lymph in your veins instead of blood. I don’t believe in anyone of you! In any circumstances the first thing for all of you is to be unlike a human being! Stop!” he cried with redoubled fury, noticing that Raskolnikov was again making a movement--“hear me out! You know I’m having a house-warming this evening, I dare say they’ve arrived by now, but I left my uncle there--I just ran in--to receive the guests. And if you weren’t a fool, a common fool, a perfect fool, if you were an original instead of a translation... you see, Rodya, I recognise you’re a clever fellow, but you’re a fool!--and if you weren’t a fool you’d come round to me this evening instead of wearing out your boots in the street!

“Enough of that,” he muttered. “We shall speak again.”
He left abruptly. A strange agitation possessed him—part triumph, part dread. His pulse raced; every nerve seemed strained. For a few moments he felt almost invigorated, as though some hidden spring of energy had been released. Yet the sensation faded almost immediately, leaving behind exhaustion so profound that each step became an effort.
The man he had left behind remained seated long after the conversation had ended. He stared at the table, absorbed in thought. Something in their exchange had unsettled him. A conclusion, vague before, now appeared obvious.
“No,” he said at last to himself. “That cannot be right. Everyone has judged him too hastily.”
Meanwhile, as Victor descended the stairs, he nearly collided with his friend Leon, who was rushing up them.
For several seconds they simply stared at one another.
“You!” Leon burst out. “So this is where you've been!”
His astonishment quickly gave way to anger.
“I've searched half the city. Do you hear me? Half the city! I went back to your room twice. I questioned your landlady. I nearly dragged your servant into the street demanding answers. And here you are strolling about as if nothing has happened.”
Victor looked at him wearily.
“I wanted to be left alone.”
“Alone?” Leon repeated. “You can barely stand. Look at yourself! You're pale as death.”
“Let me pass.”
“No.”
He seized Victor by the arm.
“You disappear without a word, you worry everyone who knows you, and then you tell me to let you pass?”
Victor's expression hardened.
“Why is it so difficult for you to understand? I do not want your concern. I did not ask for it. Every kindness feels like a chain. Every conversation exhausts me. You call it friendship. To me it feels like persecution.”
Leon released his arm, more from surprise than willingness.
Victor continued, speaking faster now.
“You all insist on helping. None of you stop to ask whether your help is wanted. Perhaps I would rather be miserable. Perhaps I would rather fail. Perhaps I simply wish to be left in peace. Why can no one accept that?”
His voice rose despite himself.
“You suffocate me. Every visit, every question, every expression of sympathy. Let me alone. For once, let me alone.”
The last words came out almost as a cry.
Leon stood silent.
Then, unexpectedly, his anger subsided.
“Well,” he said at length, “go where you please.”
Victor turned away.
“Wait.”
Leon pointed a finger at him.
“You people are all alike. Every disappointment becomes a tragedy. Every inconvenience becomes a philosophy. You lock yourselves inside your own heads and stare at your troubles until they grow enormous.”
Victor did not answer.
“No, listen. I mean it. If something hurts you, you polish the wound until it shines. Then you admire it. You call that depth. I call it vanity.”
His face reddened with the force of his irritation.
“And another thing. You're all determined to be original, yet you imitate one another perfectly. The same complaints. The same despair. The same speeches about being misunderstood.”
A reluctant smile almost crossed Victor's lips.
Leon noticed it and grew even louder.
“Don't laugh! I'm serious. This evening I'm hosting a gathering. My uncle is there already receiving guests because I ran out looking for you. If you had a grain of sense, you'd come. Sit among living people for once. Talk nonsense. Eat badly. Stay too late. Anything would be better than wandering the streets arguing with your own thoughts.”

“Enough. Until next time.”
With that he left.
The excitement that had carried him through the conversation still lingered, but only faintly. It was an uncomfortable feeling—something between exhilaration and illness. For a moment he walked quickly, almost energetically. Then the strength drained out of him again. His limbs felt heavy. Even breathing seemed laborious.
The other man remained behind, motionless.
He sat for a long while without touching the untouched glass before him. Their conversation had altered something in his thinking. A suspicion he had entertained for days now seemed less convincing.
“No,” he finally decided. “People have misunderstood him.”
As Victor stepped out into the street, he nearly ran straight into Leon.
The collision was so sudden that both stopped short.
Leon stared.
Then exploded.
“So I've found you at last!”
His voice echoed down the stairwell.
“Do you know where I've been looking? Everywhere! I went to your room. Twice. I questioned everyone who might have seen you. I was beginning to think you'd vanished completely.”
Victor looked exhausted.
“I asked for none of that.”
“Because you wanted to be alone?” Leon said incredulously. “You can hardly stay upright.”
“Move aside.”
Instead, Leon grabbed his shoulder.
“Not a chance.”
Victor's patience snapped.
“You call this friendship, but it feels like punishment. Can't you understand that? I don't want to be watched, advised, rescued, comforted. Every one of you arrives determined to improve my life. Has it occurred to you that I might not want improvement?”
The words came more quickly now.
“You never stop. Questions, concern, advice—endlessly. It wears me down. Leave me alone.”
He ended almost breathless.
Leon released him.
For a moment he seemed genuinely thoughtful.
“All right,” he said. “Go to the devil if that's your wish.”
Victor took a step.
“Not yet.”
Leon folded his arms.
“I've something to say first. The lot of you are impossible. You treat every personal difficulty as though it were a matter of world importance. You brood over your troubles day and night until they become your entire identity.”
Victor remained silent.
“Yes, that's right. Brood over them. Worship them. Feed them. Then congratulate yourselves on being profound.”
His irritation was returning rapidly.
“And don't imagine you're unique. You're all copies. Every one of you thinks the same gloomy thoughts and makes the same speeches about isolation and suffering.”
This time Victor almost smiled.
Leon caught it immediately.
“Don't mock me. I'm making perfect sense.”
He jabbed a finger toward the street.
“I'm having people over tonight. They've probably arrived already. My uncle is entertaining them while I chase you around the city. If you possessed even the smallest amount of wisdom, you'd come with me instead of wearing yourself into the pavement.”
He shook his head.
“Honestly, a few hours among ordinary human beings might do you more good than a month alone with your thoughts.”

Not one of them That yet looks on me, or would know me. Ariel, Fetch me the hat and rapier in my cell. [_Exit Ariel._]  I will discase me, and myself present As I was sometime Milan. Quickly, spirit; Thou shalt ere long be free. Ariel re-enters, singing, and helps to attire Prospero. ARIEL _Where the bee sucks, there suck I: In a cowslip’s bell I lie; There I couch when owls do cry. On the bat’s back I do fly After summer merrily. Merrily, merrily shall I live now Under the blossom that hangs on the bough._  PROSPERO. Why, that’s my dainty Ariel! I shall miss thee; But yet thou shalt have freedom; so, so, so. To the King’s ship, invisible as thou art: There shalt thou find the mariners asleep Under the hatches; the master and the boatswain Being awake, enforce them to this place, And presently, I prithee. ARIEL. I drink the air before me, and return Or ere your pulse twice beat. [_Exit._]  GONZALO. All torment, trouble, wonder and amazement Inhabits here. Some heavenly power guide us Out of this fearful country! PROSPERO. Behold, sir King, The wronged Duke of Milan, Prospero. For more assurance that a living prince Does now speak to thee, I embrace thy body; And to thee and thy company I bid A hearty welcome. ALONSO. Whe’er thou be’st he or no, Or some enchanted trifle to abuse me, As late I have been, I not know: thy pulse Beats, as of flesh and blood; and, since I saw thee, Th’ affliction of my mind amends, with which, I fear, a madness held me: this must crave, An if this be at all, a most strange story. Thy dukedom I resign, and do entreat Thou pardon me my wrongs. But how should Prospero Be living and be here? PROSPERO. First, noble friend, Let me embrace thine age, whose honour cannot Be measur’d or confin’d. GONZALO. Whether this be Or be not, I’ll not swear. PROSPERO. You do yet taste Some subtleties o’ the isle, that will not let you Believe things certain. Welcome, my friends all. [_Aside to Sebastian and Antonio._] But you, my brace of lords, were I so minded, I here could pluck his highness’ frown upon you, And justify you traitors: at this time I will tell no tales. SEBASTIAN. [_Aside._] The devil speaks in him. PROSPERO. No. For you, most wicked sir, whom to call brother Would even infect my mouth, I do forgive Thy rankest fault, all of them; and require My dukedom of thee, which perforce I know Thou must restore. ALONSO. If thou beest Prospero, Give us particulars of thy preservation; How thou hast met us here, whom three hours since Were wrack’d upon this shore; where I have lost,— How sharp the point of this remembrance is!— My dear son Ferdinand. PROSPERO. I am woe for ’t, sir. ALONSO. Irreparable is the loss, and patience Says it is past her cure. PROSPERO. I rather think You have not sought her help, of whose soft grace, For the like loss I have her sovereign aid, And rest myself content. ALONSO. You the like loss! PROSPERO. As great to me, as late; and, supportable To make the dear loss, have I means much weaker Than you may call to comfort you, for I Have lost my daughter. ALONSO. A daughter? O heavens, that they were living both in Naples, The King and Queen there! That they were, I wish Myself were mudded in that oozy bed Where my son lies. When did you lose your daughter? PROSPERO. In this last tempest. I perceive, these lords At this encounter do so much admire That they devour their reason, and scarce think Their eyes do offices of truth, their words Are natural breath; but, howsoe’er you have Been justled from your senses, know for certain That I am Prospero, and that very duke Which was thrust forth of Milan; who most strangely Upon this shore, where you were wrack’d, was landed To be the lord on’t. No more yet of this; For ’tis a chronicle of day by day, Not a relation for a breakfast nor Befitting this first meeting. Welcome, sir. This cell’s my court: here have I few attendants, And subjects none abroad: pray you, look in. My dukedom since you have given me again, I will requite you with as good a thing; At least bring forth a wonder, to content ye As much as me my dukedom.

A rocky shore. SERAPHEL, exiled Queen of Valdris, stands apart. Her spirit LUMEN attends her.
SERAPHEL. They pass me by unknowing — eyes made blind By guilt and sea-salt both. Go, Lumen, fetch My crown and robes from out the cave. [Exit Lumen.] I'll shed this beggar's guise and stand once more As once I stood in Valdris. Swift, good spirit; Thy service nears its end.
Lumen re-enters, singing, and bears the robes.
LUMEN. Where the wind turns, there turn I, Through the hollow reeds I sigh; Where the river meets the sea, There shall I at last run free. Merrily, merrily shall I fly Past the sun and past the sky.
SERAPHEL. My faithful Lumen! Thou shalt have thy freedom; But first, one final office: go unseen Unto the vessel anchored in the bay, And wake the captain from his enchant'd sleep. Bid him attend us here.
LUMEN. I move like thought, and am returned Before the tide can turn a stone. [Exit.]
DUKE ALDRIC. What strangeness holds this coast? Some power bends The air about us. Heaven, be our guide!
SERAPHEL. Look well upon me, brother. I am she Whom you displaced — Seraphel, rightful queen. No phantom this: reach out and take my hand, And feel the warmth of living blood beneath.
ALDRIC. Thou canst not be — thou wert three years at sea, Or drowned — thy pulse — it beats — what sorcery Hath kept thee? Or do I, with fever'd mind, Conjure the face I wronged?
SERAPHEL. No conjuring. The isle hath sheltered me, and taught me patience. That crown which you usurped — I do require it, Though not in malice ask I its return; For I have found, in exile's bitter school, That power without pardon is a prison.
MONTARVO. [Aside.] My conscience wrings me. She was ever just.
SERAPHEL. [Aside to Montarvo.] Thou too wast party to it. I have known These three years past. Yet look, I hold no blade Above thee: thou shalt have thy peace from me If peace is what thou seek'st.
MONTARVO. [Aside.] The devil's mercy — no, it is a saint's.
ALDRIC. If thou art truly she, then tell me how Thou cam'st to live? We watched the tempest break Thy ship to timber. I — I grieve that sight More than thou know'st. My crown sat heavy on me Each morning after.
SERAPHEL. I believe thee, Aldric; And grief is a beginning. I was cast Upon this shore, and found it strange and kind. I shall not now rehearse these years entire — The tale would swallow daylight whole. Know this: I lived, I learned, I lost things dear to me As thou hast too, I warrant. We are both Made lesser by these years, and yet more whole. Take back my hand. This rocky court hath been My throne-room long enough. Let us go home.

A rocky shore. SERAPHEL, exiled Queen of Valdris, stands apart. Her spirit LUMEN attends her.
SERAPHEL. They walk straight past me. Three years gone, and not one eye Knows mine. Go, Lumen — fetch my robes and crown From the cave. [Exit Lumen.] I'll put this beggar's skin Behind me, stand as I was. Be quick now; Thy service is nearly done.
Lumen re-enters bearing the robes, singing.
LUMEN. Where the wind turns, there turn I, Through the hollow reeds I sigh; Where the river meets the sea, There shall I at last run free. Merrily, merrily shall I fly Past the sun and past the sky.
SERAPHEL. Good Lumen. Thou shalt have thy liberty soon enough — But one last errand: go silent to the bay, Find the captain of that vessel sleeping Beneath enchantment still, and wake him. Send him to me here.
LUMEN. I am gone and back before The tide shifts a pebble. [Exit.]
DUKE ALDRIC. What place is this? The very air feels bent. Some power dwells here. God guide us out of it.
SERAPHEL. Look at me properly, Aldric. It is I — Seraphel. The queen you set aside. No spirit, no trick of the light — give me your hand. You feel that? That is blood, and it is warm.
ALDRIC. This cannot — thou wert lost at sea, three years, We saw the ship — thy pulse does beat — I am Not well, I think — some fever holds me still —
SERAPHEL. No fever. The isle kept me. I kept myself. And now I want what's mine returned — not seized By rage, not out of fury. I have had Three years to learn what fury costs, and it costs much. I ask it plainly: give me back my crown.
MONTARVO. [Aside.] She was always just. God, she was always just.
SERAPHEL. [Aside to Montarvo.] I know thy part in it. Have known these three years. Yet I've no blade for thee — only this offer: Come honestly before me and we're done with it.
MONTARVO. [Aside.] This is either mercy or it's witchcraft. And I think it mercy.
ALDRIC. If thou art truly living, tell me how. We watched the storm tear that ship into kindling — I have not — there is not a morning since I have not thought of it. The crown has been No easy thing to wear, I swear to thee.
SERAPHEL. I believe thee. Grief is something, Aldric — It is not nothing. I was thrown ashore here Half-dead, and the isle was strange, and then less strange. I will not tell the whole of it today; The story is three years long and the light Is going. Know only this: I lived. I lost things, Same as thee. We are both worn by it — Perhaps that is sufficient ground to start from. Take my hand again. This shore has served As court to me too long. Let us go home.

Now having a night, a day, and still another night following before me in New Bedford, ere I could embark for my destined port, it became a matter of concernment where I was to eat and sleep meanwhile. It was a very dubious-looking, nay, a very dark and dismal night, bitingly cold and cheerless. I knew no one in the place. With anxious grapnels I had sounded my pocket, and only brought up a few pieces of silver,—So, wherever you go, Ishmael, said I to myself, as I stood in the middle of a dreary street shouldering my bag, and comparing the gloom towards the north with the darkness towards the south—wherever in your wisdom you may conclude to lodge for the night, my dear Ishmael, be sure to inquire the price, and don’t be too particular. With halting steps I paced the streets, and passed the sign of “The Crossed Harpoons”—but it looked too expensive and jolly there. Further on, from the bright red windows of the “Sword-Fish Inn,” there came such fervent rays, that it seemed to have melted the packed snow and ice from before the house, for everywhere else the congealed frost lay ten inches thick in a hard, asphaltic pavement,—rather weary for me, when I struck my foot against the flinty projections, because from hard, remorseless service the soles of my boots were in a most miserable plight. Too expensive and jolly, again thought I, pausing one moment to watch the broad glare in the street, and hear the sounds of the tinkling glasses within. But go on, Ishmael, said I at last; don’t you hear? get away from before the door; your patched boots are stopping the way. So on I went. I now by instinct followed the streets that took me waterward, for there, doubtless, were the cheapest, if not the cheeriest inns. Such dreary streets! blocks of blackness, not houses, on either hand, and here and there a candle, like a candle moving about in a tomb. At this hour of the night, of the last day of the week, that quarter of the town proved all but deserted. But presently I came to a smoky light proceeding from a low, wide building, the door of which stood invitingly open. It had a careless look, as if it were meant for the uses of the public; so, entering, the first thing I did was to stumble over an ash-box in the porch. Ha! thought I, ha, as the flying particles almost choked me, are these ashes from that destroyed city, Gomorrah? But “The Crossed Harpoons,” and “The Sword-Fish?”—this, then must needs be the sign of “The Trap.” However, I picked myself up and hearing a loud voice within, pushed on and opened a second, interior door. It seemed the great Black Parliament sitting in Tophet. A hundred black faces turned round in their rows to peer; and beyond, a black Angel of Doom was beating a book in a pulpit. It was a negro church; and the preacher’s text was about the blackness of darkness, and the weeping and wailing and teeth-gnashing there. Ha, Ishmael, muttered I, backing out, Wretched entertainment at the sign of ‘The Trap!’  Moving on, I at last came to a dim sort of light not far from the docks, and heard a forlorn creaking in the air; and looking up, saw a swinging sign over the door with a white painting upon it, faintly representing a tall straight jet of misty spray, and these words underneath—“The Spouter Inn:—Peter Coffin.”  Coffin?—Spouter?—Rather ominous in that particular connexion, thought I. But it is a common name in Nantucket, they say, and I suppose this Peter here is an emigrant from there. As the light looked so dim, and the place, for the time, looked quiet enough, and the dilapidated little wooden house itself looked as if it might have been carted here from the ruins of some burnt district, and as the swinging sign had a poverty-stricken sort of creak to it, I thought that here was the very spot for cheap lodgings, and the best of pea coffee. It was a queer sort of place—a gable-ended old house, one side palsied as it were, and leaning over sadly. It stood on a sharp bleak corner, where that tempestuous wind Euroclydon kept up a worse howling than ever it did about poor Paul’s tossed craft. Euroclydon, nevertheless, is a mighty pleasant zephyr to any one in-doors, with his feet on the hob quietly toasting for bed.

Now having the better part of two days to pass in Gloucester before the coastal packet would take me further north, the immediate question pressing upon me was where I should find a bed and a meal at a price my circumstances could honestly sustain. The night was raw and wet, the kind that doesn't announce itself with any honest downpour but simply saturates everything by slow degrees, until a man finds his collar soaked without ever having felt a drop fall. I knew nobody in Gloucester. My funds, such as they were, I had already reckoned twice over in my coat pocket as I walked, and the sum did not improve with repetition. Well then, said I to myself, stopping beneath the overhang of a chandler's shop and watching the rain bead on the cobblestones, the matter is simple enough: you want a roof, Nathaniel, and four walls, and something warm in a bowl, and you want them cheap. Pride has no lodgings to offer you tonight.
I made my way along the main street first, as a man will do who knows he cannot afford the main street. The Anchor and Crown had lamplight spilling handsomely from every window and the sound of fiddle-playing within—too handsome entirely, and I moved along. Further down stood the Mariner's Rest, whose sign depicted a sailor in a hammock of improbable comfort, and from whose open door proceeded a smell of roasting meat so persuasive I had to address myself sternly and keep walking. These establishments were for men with warmer pockets than mine. The cobblestones turned uneven underfoot where the frost had buckled them, and my left boot, whose sole had been making silent but unmistakable complaints for a fortnight, now began to admit the wet.
I turned instinctively toward the water, reasoning as I had always reasoned in such ports, that cheapness follows the smell of brine. The streets narrowed. The lamplight thinned to almost nothing. Here were buildings that leaned toward one another overhead as if sharing confidences, and underfoot the ground was uncertain—rope-ends, a broken oar, the dark slick of fish-scales on stone.
