To speak of him with any justice, he is the very map and measure of courtly virtue; in him you will discover the full sum of whatsoever quality a gentleman might covet.
PRINCE. Sir, your account of him loses nothing in the telling, though to anatomise him point by point would exhaust the patience of a scholar and still fall short of his full extent. But in the earnest spirit of praise, I hold him a person of extraordinary composition, and his qualities of so rare and peculiar a stamp that his only true likeness is his own reflection, and any who would follow his example must be content to walk in shadow.
CORVINO. Your highness does him perfect justice.
PRINCE. But what is the substance of it, sir? Why do we dress the gentleman in such laboured ceremony?
CORVINO. My lord?
BENEDETTO. Is it not possible to arrive at a meaning by a shorter road? You will manage it, sir, I am certain.
PRINCE. What is the occasion for naming this gentleman at all?
CORVINO. Of Silvano?
BENEDETTO. His vocabulary is quite spent. Every fine word he had is gone.
PRINCE. Of him, sir.
CORVINO. I trust your highness is not unacquainted with the excellence of Silvano—
PRINCE. I would not dare claim it, lest I be thought to rival him; and besides, to truly know another man requires one to know oneself, which is a longer undertaking.
CORVINO. I speak, my lord, of his skill with the blade specifically; in that particular, by the estimation of those best qualified to judge, he stands without an equal.
PRINCE. What arms does he favour?
CORVINO. Sword and main-gauche.
PRINCE. Those are two arms. Proceed.
CORVINO. The Duke, my lord, has laid against him a wager of four Neapolitan chargers, to which Silvano has opposed four Venetian blades with their full appointments—belts, chapes, and all manner of furnishings, three of which are, I confess, extraordinarily fine, very sympathetic to the hand, and of most ingenious and liberal workmanship.
PRINCE. What do you call the furnishings?
BENEDETTO. I suspected we should need a gloss before it was over.
CORVINO. The furnishings, my lord, are the belt-fittings and mountings.
PRINCE. The word might suit the matter better if we were equipping a siege. Let us say fittings and have done. But continue—four Neapolitan horses against four Venetian blades and their appointments: that is the southern stake against the northern. What is the precise condition of the wager?
CORVINO. The Duke, my lord, has proposed that in a course of ten passes, Silvano shall not surpass your highness by more than two hits. He has set the measure at ten against eight, and it would come to trial directly should your highness consent to take the field.
PRINCE. And if I decline?
CORVINO. My lord, I speak only of your appearance in the contest itself.
PRINCE. Sir, I shall remain here in this gallery. If it please the Duke, this is my accustomed hour for exercise. Let the weapons be brought, let Silvano be agreeable, and let the Duke maintain his intention, and I will do my best on his behalf. If I fall short, I lose nothing but my credit and the margin of hits.
CORVINO. Shall I carry this answer back to the Duke?
PRINCE. To that effect, sir; with whatever ornament your custom requires.
CORVINO. I offer my most devoted service to your highness.
PRINCE. Yours, entirely.
Exit Corvino.
He does well to advertise his own devotion; there is no one else who would do it for him.
BENEDETTO. The man hatched before he was ready and has been running about with the shell on his brow ever since.
PRINCE. He was making courtly speeches before he could properly walk. And there are a dozen more of the same flock whom this age admires beyond reason—fellows who have caught nothing but the pitch and fashion of the time, a frothy assemblage of manners with no substance beneath. Set them to any genuine trial and they dissolve like foam.
Enter a Servant.
SERVANT. My lord, the Duke commends himself to your highness by way of Corvino, who has reported that you await him in the gallery. He desires to know whether your intention to meet Silvano remains fixed, or whether you would have more time appointed.
PRINCE. My purpose does not change. I am at the Duke's disposal.

To speak of him with any justice, he is the very map and measure of courtly virtue—in him you will find the full sum of whatsoever quality a gentleman might covet.
PRINCE. Your account of him loses nothing in the telling, sir, though to anatomise him point by point would exhaust the patience of a scholar and still fall short. But in the earnest spirit of praise, I hold him a person of extraordinary composition, his qualities of so rare a stamp that his only true likeness is his own reflection, and any who would follow his example must be content to walk in shadow.
CORVINO. Your highness does him perfect justice.
PRINCE. But what is the substance of it? Why do we dress the gentleman in so much ceremony?
CORVINO. My lord?
BENEDETTO. Is there no shorter road to a meaning? You will find one, sir, I am quite confident.
PRINCE. What is the occasion for naming this gentleman at all?
CORVINO. Of Silvano?
BENEDETTO. Every fine word he had is spent. The purse is empty.
PRINCE. Of him, sir.
CORVINO. I trust your highness is not unacquainted with the excellence of Silvano—
PRINCE. I would not dare claim it, lest I be thought to rival him. Besides, to truly know another man requires knowing oneself, which is a considerably longer undertaking.

PRINCE. The word might suit the matter better if we were equipping a siege. Let us say fittings. But proceed—four horses against four blades: that is the southern stake against the northern. What exactly are the terms?

PRINCE. And if I should decline?
CORVINO. I speak only of your appearance in the contest itself, my lord.
PRINCE. I shall remain here in the gallery, sir. This is my customary hour for exercise; if it please the Duke, let the weapons be brought, let Silvano be willing, and I will do my best on the Duke's behalf. If I fall short, I lose nothing but my credit and the margin of hits.
CORVINO. Shall I carry this answer back?
PRINCE. To that effect—with whatever flourish your nature requires.
CORVINO. I commend my most devoted service to your highness.
PRINCE. Yours, entirely.
Exit Corvino.
He does well to advertise his own devotion. There is no one else who would think to do it for him.

PRINCE. He was making courtly speeches before he could properly walk. There are a dozen more of the same flock this age admires so extravagantly—fellows who have caught nothing but the pitch and fashion of the time, a frothy collection of surface manners with no substance beneath. Enter a Servant.
SERVANT. My lord, the Duke commends himself to your highness, and desires to know whether your intention to meet Silvano holds, or whether you would have more time appointed.
PRINCE. My purpose does not change. I am at the Duke's disposal.

Methinks your maw, like mine, should be your clock, And strike you home without a messenger. ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. Come, Dromio, come, these jests are out of season, Reserve them till a merrier hour than this. Where is the gold I gave in charge to thee? DROMIO OF EPHESUS. To me, sir? why, you gave no gold to me! ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. Come on, sir knave, have done your foolishness, And tell me how thou hast dispos’d thy charge. DROMIO OF EPHESUS. My charge was but to fetch you from the mart Home to your house, the Phoenix, sir, to dinner. My mistress and her sister stay for you. ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. Now, as I am a Christian, answer me In what safe place you have bestow’d my money, Or I shall break that merry sconce of yours That stands on tricks when I am undispos’d; Where is the thousand marks thou hadst of me? DROMIO OF EPHESUS. I have some marks of yours upon my pate, Some of my mistress’ marks upon my shoulders, But not a thousand marks between you both. If I should pay your worship those again, Perchance you will not bear them patiently. ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. Thy mistress’ marks? what mistress, slave, hast thou? DROMIO OF EPHESUS. Your worship’s wife, my mistress at the Phoenix; She that doth fast till you come home to dinner, And prays that you will hie you home to dinner. ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. What, wilt thou flout me thus unto my face, Being forbid? There, take you that, sir knave. DROMIO OF EPHESUS. What mean you, sir? for God’s sake hold your hands. Nay, an you will not, sir, I’ll take my heels. [_Exit Dromio._]  ANTIPHOLUS OF SYRACUSE. Upon my life, by some device or other The villain is o’er-raught of all my money. They say this town is full of cozenage, As nimble jugglers that deceive the eye, Dark-working sorcerers that change the mind, Soul-killing witches that deform the body, Disguised cheaters, prating mountebanks, And many such-like liberties of sin: If it prove so, I will be gone the sooner. I’ll to the Centaur to go seek this slave. I greatly fear my money is not safe. [_Exit._]    ACT II  SCENE I. A public place  Enter Adriana, wife to Antipholus (of Ephesus) with Luciana her sister. ADRIANA. Neither my husband nor the slave return’d That in such haste I sent to seek his master? Sure, Luciana, it is two o’clock. LUCIANA. Perhaps some merchant hath invited him, And from the mart he’s somewhere gone to dinner. Good sister, let us dine, and never fret; A man is master of his liberty; Time is their master, and when they see time, They’ll go or come. If so, be patient, sister. ADRIANA. Why should their liberty than ours be more? LUCIANA. Because their business still lies out o’ door. ADRIANA. Look when I serve him so, he takes it ill.  LUCIANA. O, know he is the bridle of your will. ADRIANA. There’s none but asses will be bridled so. LUCIANA. Why, headstrong liberty is lash’d with woe. There’s nothing situate under heaven’s eye But hath his bound in earth, in sea, in sky. The beasts, the fishes, and the winged fowls Are their males’ subjects, and at their controls. Man, more divine, the masters of all these, Lord of the wide world and wild wat’ry seas, Indued with intellectual sense and souls, Of more pre-eminence than fish and fowls, Are masters to their females, and their lords: Then let your will attend on their accords. ADRIANA. This servitude makes you to keep unwed. LUCIANA. Not this, but troubles of the marriage-bed. ADRIANA. But, were you wedded, you would bear some sway. LUCIANA. Ere I learn love, I’ll practise to obey. ADRIANA. How if your husband start some other where? LUCIANA. Till he come home again, I would forbear. ADRIANA. Patience unmov’d! No marvel though she pause; They can be meek that have no other cause. A wretched soul bruis’d with adversity, We bid be quiet when we hear it cry; But were we burd’ned with like weight of pain, As much, or more, we should ourselves complain: So thou, that hast no unkind mate to grieve thee, With urging helpless patience would relieve me: But if thou live to see like right bereft, This fool-begg’d patience in thee will be left. LUCIANA. Well, I will marry one day, but to try. Here comes your man, now is your husband nigh.

Methinks your belly, like a clock well-wound, Should toll the hour and draw you home to meat Without a boy to bear you notice of it.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Come, Balthazar, these quips are poorly timed; Keep them for some occasion fit for mirth. Where is the letter that I gave to thee?
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. What letter, sir? You gave no letter me.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Come, come, have done with playing at confusion, And tell me faithfully where you have put it.
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. My charge was only this: to bring you straight From the exchange to supper at the Dolphin. Your cousin and his wife do wait within.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Now, by my faith, I'll have a plain account Of where you've stowed the paper in your keeping, Or I shall find a way to jog that memory That grins and plays the fool when I am vexed. Where is the letter that I trusted you?
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. I have some marks of trust upon my back From errand-running and from being cuffed, But no such letter as you speak of, sir. If I had kept it, I should be kept poorly, For letters bring me nothing but more labour.
LEONATO OF MILAN. What cousin speaks of waiting? Whose relation?
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. Your worship's own, sir, lodging at the Dolphin, Who fasts from supper till your worship come And sends me thrice an hour to fetch you in.
LEONATO OF MILAN. What, dost thou mock me to my face again? Take that for thy impertinence, thou rogue.
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. What would you, sir? For pity, hold your hand! Nay, if you will not, then I'll use my legs.
Exit Balthazar.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Upon my soul, there is some villainy here, Some trick by which the rascal has been gulled Or practised this confusion on himself. They say this city swarms with subtle knaves, With forgers, liars, and dissembling rogues, With cutpurses that work in open day, And whispering cheats that take you by the sleeve. If it be so, I'll not linger long. I'll to the Dolphin, seek this fellow out, And satisfy myself my letter's safe.
Exit.

ACT II, SCENE I. A room in the Dolphin.
Enter Beatrice, wife to Leonato of Naples, and her younger sister Marta.
BEATRICE. Neither my husband nor the boy come back— That boy I sent in haste to find his master? The bells have struck, Marta, past two o'clock.
MARTA. Some merchant of the city may have kept him. The exchange draws men aside at every corner. Good sister, let us eat and think no more of it. A man goes where occasion calls him out, And time itself is all the rein they know; When it suits time, they'll come. Be easy, sister.
BEATRICE. Why should their ease be greater far than ours?
MARTA. Their business ever leads them out of doors.
BEATRICE. When I stay out, he takes it very ill.
MARTA. He is the governor of your own will.
BEATRICE. None but a fool submits to such a bit.
MARTA. But headstrong will must suffer for it yet. There's nothing under heaven's broad canopy But owns some bound in air, on land, or sea. The hawk, the hound, the horse that bears his load, Are subject to the hand that holds the goad. And man, that prides himself the lord of these, The master of the land and of the seas, With reason clothed and with dominion crowned, Is still by nature's ordinance duty-bound To hold his place, and woman hers beside; Then let thy will lie down and cease to chide.
BEATRICE. Such doctrine keeps you from the wedding-pew.
MARTA. Not doctrine, sister—what I've seen men do.
BEATRICE. Were you once married, you'd assert your part.
MARTA. First I would learn submission by my art.
BEATRICE. And if your husband wandered wide abroad?
MARTA. I'd hold my tongue until he crossed the threshold.
BEATRICE. How serene! And easy to be still When you have never suffered against your will. A soul that groans beneath a heavy yoke Is told to hush by those who bear no load; But set the same weight on those patient shoulders And patience thins to nothing, I will warrant. You, with no faithless mate to wound your pride, Bid me be calm while sorrow knocks inside; But live to know such usage as I've known, And this pet virtue will be overthrown.
MARTA. I may be wed one day, and put it to the test. But look—your servant comes, and with him, I think, news.

Methinks your belly, like a clock well-wound, Should toll the hour and bring you home to meat Without a boy dispatched to carry notice.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Come, Balthazar, these quips are poorly timed. Keep them for some occasion fit for mirth. Where is the letter that I gave to thee?
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. What letter, sir? You gave no letter me.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Have done with playing at confusion, come— And tell me plainly what you've done with it.
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. My charge was only this: to bring you straight From the exchange to supper at the Dolphin. Your cousin and his wife are waiting, sir.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Now by my faith, I'll have a clear account Of where you've put the paper in your keeping, Or I shall find a remedy for that grin You wear when I am vexed. The letter—where?
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. I carry marks enough upon my back From fetching and from being knocked about, But no such letter as you speak of, sir. Letters bring me nothing but more labour.
LEONATO OF MILAN. What cousin? Who waits? Whose relation is this?
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. Your worship's own, sir, lodging at the Dolphin— Who will not eat till you come in to supper And sends me out to fetch you every hour.
LEONATO OF MILAN. What, dost thou mock me to my face again? Take that for thy impertinence, thou rogue.
BALTHAZAR OF NAPLES. For pity's sake, sir—hold! Nay, if you will not, I'll trust my legs.
Exit Balthazar.
LEONATO OF MILAN. Upon my soul, there is some villainy here— Some trick by which this rascal has been turned Or plays this game of confusion for himself. They say this city swarms with subtle cheats, With forgers, cutpurses, and whispering rogues That take a man apart from what is his Before he knows which street he's standing in. If so, I'll not stay long. I'll to the Dolphin, Find out this fellow, and be satisfied.
Exit.

ACT II, SCENE I. A room in the Dolphin.
Enter Beatrice, wife to Leonato of Naples, and Marta, her younger sister.
BEATRICE. Neither my husband back, nor yet the boy I sent in haste to find him? Two o'clock, Marta—the bells struck it not a moment past.
MARTA. Some merchant of the city may have kept him; The exchange pulls men aside at every turn. Come, sister, let us eat and think no more on it. A man goes where occasion calls him out, And time itself the only rein he knows. He'll come when it suits time. Be easy, sister.
BEATRICE. Why should their ease be always more than ours?
MARTA. Their business leads them ever out of doors.
BEATRICE. When I stay out, he takes it very ill.
MARTA. He is the governor of your own will.
BEATRICE. None but an ass submits to such a bit.
MARTA. But headstrong will is sorely lashed for it. There's nothing under heaven's open eye But has its bound—on earth, in sea, in sky. The hawk, the hound, the horse beneath the goad Are subject to the hand that rules the road. And man, that calls himself the lord of these, Master of land and of the rolling seas, By nature's own decree is set above, And woman by his side—so cease to chafe, my love.
BEATRICE. Such doctrine's why you've never taken a husband.
MARTA. Not doctrine—what I've watched men do with one.
BEATRICE. Were you once married, you'd assert your share.
MARTA. First I would learn to bow before I dare.
BEATRICE. And if your husband wandered far and wide?
MARTA. I'd hold my tongue until he came inside.
BEATRICE. How very still—and cheap the virtue is When you've no faithless mate to make it his. A soul bent double underneath a weight Is hushed by those who never bore the same; But load those patient shoulders as I'm loaded And patience shrinks to nothing, I'll be bound. You, with no wound to keep you up at night, Bid me be calm—it costs you nothing. Right. But live to know such usage, sister, once, And see how long this cherished peace endures.
MARTA. I may be wed one day and put it to the proof. But look—your servant comes. Your husband's near.

Like desperadoes they tugged and they strained, till the welcome cry was heard—“Stand up, Tashtego!—give it to him!” The harpoon was hurled. “Stern all!” The oarsmen backed water; the same moment something went hot and hissing along every one of their wrists. It was the magical line. An instant before, Stubb had swiftly caught two additional turns with it round the loggerhead, whence, by reason of its increased rapid circlings, a hempen blue smoke now jetted up and mingled with the steady fumes from his pipe. As the line passed round and round the loggerhead; so also, just before reaching that point, it blisteringly passed through and through both of Stubb’s hands, from which the hand-cloths, or squares of quilted canvas sometimes worn at these times, had accidentally dropped. It was like holding an enemy’s sharp two-edged sword by the blade, and that enemy all the time striving to wrest it out of your clutch. “Wet the line! wet the line!” cried Stubb to the tub oarsman (him seated by the tub) who, snatching off his hat, dashed sea-water into it. * More turns were taken, so that the line began holding its place. The boat now flew through the boiling water like a shark all fins. Stubb and Tashtego here changed places—stem for stern—a staggering business truly in that rocking commotion. *Partly to show the indispensableness of this act, it may here be stated, that, in the old Dutch fishery, a mop was used to dash the running line with water; in many other ships, a wooden piggin, or bailer, is set apart for that purpose. Your hat, however, is the most convenient. From the vibrating line extending the entire length of the upper part of the boat, and from its now being more tight than a harpstring, you would have thought the craft had two keels—one cleaving the water, the other the air—as the boat churned on through both opposing elements at once. A continual cascade played at the bows; a ceaseless whirling eddy in her wake; and, at the slightest motion from within, even but of a little finger, the vibrating, cracking craft canted over her spasmodic gunwale into the sea. Thus they rushed; each man with might and main clinging to his seat, to prevent being tossed to the foam; and the tall form of Tashtego at the steering oar crouching almost double, in order to bring down his centre of gravity. Whole Atlantics and Pacifics seemed passed as they shot on their way, till at length the whale somewhat slackened his flight. “Haul in—haul in!” cried Stubb to the bowsman! and, facing round towards the whale, all hands began pulling the boat up to him, while yet the boat was being towed on. Soon ranging up by his flank, Stubb, firmly planting his knee in the clumsy cleat, darted dart after dart into the flying fish; at the word of command, the boat alternately sterning out of the way of the whale’s horrible wallow, and then ranging up for another fling. The red tide now poured from all sides of the monster like brooks down a hill. His tormented body rolled not in brine but in blood, which bubbled and seethed for furlongs behind in their wake. The slanting sun playing upon this crimson pond in the sea, sent back its reflection into every face, so that they all glowed to each other like red men. And all the while, jet after jet of white smoke was agonizingly shot from the spiracle of the whale, and vehement puff after puff from the mouth of the excited headsman; as at every dart, hauling in upon his crooked lance (by the line attached to it), Stubb straightened it again and again, by a few rapid blows against the gunwale, then again and again sent it into the whale. “Pull up—pull up!” he now cried to the bowsman, as the waning whale relaxed in his wrath. “Pull up!—close to!” and the boat ranged along the fish’s flank. When reaching far over the bow, Stubb slowly churned his long sharp lance into the fish, and kept it there, carefully churning and churning, as if cautiously seeking to feel after some gold watch that the whale might have swallowed, and which he was fearful of breaking ere he could hook it out. But that gold watch he sought was the innermost life of the fish. And now it is struck; for, starting from his trance into that unspeakable thing called his “flurry,” the monster horribly wallowed in his blood, overwrapped himself in impenetrable, mad, boiling spray, so that the imperilled craft, instantly dropping astern, had much ado blindly to struggle out from that phrensied twilight into the clear air of the day.

Like men possessed they hauled and heaved, every muscle cracking with the effort, until the cry rang out—"Now, Queequeg!—drive it home!" The lance was thrown. "Back water!" The oars bit hard in reverse; and in that same instant something scorched and singing swept the length of every man's forearms. It was the line, alive and running. A breath before, Flask had whipped three fast turns of it round the loggerhead, and from those turns, by the violence of its speed, a thin grey smoke now twisted up and dissolved into the salt air. Where the line burned round the post it burned also through both of Flask's palms, the wrapped cloth he had bound there having worked loose in the confusion and fallen away. It was the sensation of gripping a wire stretched to breaking—not pain exactly, not at first, but a heat so immediate and absolute it registered as something beyond ordinary feeling.
"Water on the line! Water!" Flask shouted, and the nearest man flung his bailing can across the thwart and sloshed a gout of sea over the smoking hemp.
More wraps were taken. The line steadied. The boat leapt forward with a violence that seemed to lift her clean from the surface, throwing spray in continuous white sheets from her bows while the stern sucked and churned in her wake. Flask and Queequeg shifted positions—a desperate, lunging exchange in that pitching, heaving shell of a boat, each man passing the other in a space scarcely wide enough to stand. From stem to stern the line ran rigid as iron, and the whole craft shuddered along its length at every surge; a man might have believed she had no keel at all but was simply a thing of tension, held to the water's surface by the angle of her strain.
Every man gripped his thwart with both hands and said nothing. The spray came constantly, the bow dipping and throwing and dipping again, and ahead of them the dark shape of the whale moved through the swells like a travelling hill, immense and purposeful and apparently inexhaustible.
Then, gradually, the pull began to slacken.
"Haul in—haul in!" Flask called forward, and the bowsman began working hand over hand, and the others bent to their oars, and foot by foot the boat closed with the whale until they came up along his side, close enough to see the texture of the scarred hide, the barnacles, the slow heave of his breathing. Flask braced his knee against the forward thwart and drove his lance again and again into the rolling flank, the boat backing and ranging and backing again at each command, working always at the angle that kept them clear of that tremendous, lethal tail.
The blood came freely now. It ran in dark streams down the whale's sides and spread behind them on the water in a great stain that the afternoon light turned a deep, terrible copper. The men's faces caught that reflection from the surface; they looked at one another across the thwarts and each face was red, as though they laboured not by daylight but by the light of something burning. The whale's breath came in great, convulsive jets—white vapour shot upward at long intervals, each eruption followed by a silence that felt like suspension—and Flask breathed with it, almost, his own exhalations coming hard and fast as he worked.
At last the great animal began to slow in earnest, to wallow rather than run, and Flask leaned out over the bow and set the long lance with both hands and drove it deep, working it with slow, deliberate turns as a man might work a probe into a wound—searching, careful, unhurried now that the moment had finally come. He sought the life that lay deepest in the animal, the centre of it, the place from which nothing could be recalled.
He found it.
The whale convulsed with a violence that was almost beyond dimension—a rolling, churning detonation of blood and water that obliterated everything within thirty yards of it—and the boat drove back hard through the churned white chaos, every man holding on with closed eyes, until they broke through into the clear air beyond and the sea behind them was a spreading stain, quiet now, the long business finished.

Flask braced his knee against the forward thwart and drove his lance again and again into the rolling flank, the boat backing and ranging at each command, always keeping clear of that tremendous tail.
The blood came freely. It ran in dark streams down the whale's sides and spread across the water behind them in a great stain that the afternoon light turned deep copper. The men's faces caught that reflection from the surface; looking at one another across the thwarts, each face was red, as though they laboured not by daylight but by the light of something burning. At last the animal began to slow in earnest. To wallow rather than run. Flask leaned out over the bow and set the long lance with both hands and drove it deep, working it with slow deliberate turns—searching, careful, unhurried now that the moment had come. He was looking for the life that lay deepest in the animal. The centre of it. The place from which nothing returns.
He found it.
The whale convulsed with a violence almost beyond reckoning—a rolling, churning detonation of blood and water that obliterated everything within thirty yards—and the boat was driven back hard through the white chaos, every man holding on with his eyes shut, until they broke into the clear air beyond. Behind them the sea was a wide, spreading stain. Quiet now. The long business finished.

“What will be his surprise,” thought she, “when he knows who they are! He takes them now for people of fashion.”  The introduction, however, was immediately made; and as she named their relationship to herself, she stole a sly look at him, to see how he bore it; and was not without the expectation of his decamping as fast as he could from such disgraceful companions. That he was _surprised_ by the connection was evident: he sustained it, however, with fortitude: and, so far from going away, turned back with them, and entered into conversation with Mr. Gardiner. Elizabeth could not but be pleased, could not but triumph. It was consoling that he should know she had some relations for whom there was no need to blush. She listened most attentively to all that passed between them, and gloried in every expression, every sentence of her uncle, which marked his intelligence, his taste, or his good manners. The conversation soon turned upon fishing; and she heard Mr. Darcy invite him, with the greatest civility, to fish there as often as he chose, while he continued in the neighbourhood, offering at the same time to supply him with fishing tackle, and pointing out those parts of the stream where there was usually most sport. Mrs. Gardiner, who was walking arm in arm with Elizabeth, gave her a look expressive of her wonder. Elizabeth said nothing, but it gratified her exceedingly; the compliment must be all for herself. Her astonishment, however, was extreme; and continually was she repeating, “Why is he so altered? From what can it proceed? It cannot be for _me_, it cannot be for _my_ sake that his manners are thus softened. My reproofs at Hunsford could not work such a change as this. It is impossible that he should still love me.”  After walking some time in this way, the two ladies in front, the two gentlemen behind, on resuming their places, after descending to the brink of the river for the better inspection of some curious water-plant, there chanced to be a little alteration. It originated in Mrs. Gardiner, who, fatigued by the exercise of the morning, found Elizabeth’s arm inadequate to her support, and consequently preferred her husband’s. Mr. Darcy took her place by her niece, and they walked on together. After a short silence the lady first spoke. She wished him to know that she had been assured of his absence before she came to the place, and accordingly began by observing, that his arrival had been very unexpected--“for your housekeeper,” she added, “informed us that you would certainly not be here till to-morrow; and, indeed, before we left Bakewell, we understood that you were not immediately expected in the country.” He acknowledged the truth of it all; and said that business with his steward had occasioned his coming forward a few hours before the rest of the party with whom he had been travelling. “They will join me early to-morrow,” he continued, “and among them are some who will claim an acquaintance with you,--Mr. Bingley and his sisters.”  Elizabeth answered only by a slight bow. Her thoughts were instantly driven back to the time when Mr. Bingley’s name had been last mentioned between them; and if she might judge from his complexion, _his_ mind was not very differently engaged. “There is also one other person in the party,” he continued after a pause, “who more particularly wishes to be known to you. Will you allow me, or do I ask too much, to introduce my sister to your acquaintance during your stay at Lambton?”  The surprise of such an application was great indeed; it was too great for her to know in what manner she acceded to it. She immediately felt that whatever desire Miss Darcy might have of being acquainted with her, must be the work of her brother, and without looking farther, it was satisfactory; it was gratifying to know that his resentment had not made him think really ill of her. They now walked on in silence; each of them deep in thought. Elizabeth was not comfortable; that was impossible; but she was flattered and pleased. His wish of introducing his sister to her was a compliment of the highest kind. They soon outstripped the others; and when they had reached the carriage, Mr. and Mrs. Gardiner were half a quarter of a mile behind. He then asked her to walk into the house--but she declared herself not tired, and they stood together on the lawn.

Margaret had scarcely recovered from her surprise before another occasion for reflection presented itself. Mr. Hartwell, whose reserve had once appeared almost insurmountable, now conversed with her aunt and uncle with an ease that seemed entirely free of embarrassment. She had expected politeness, perhaps; but this degree of attention exceeded anything she had anticipated.
As her aunt introduced Mr. Benson as her brother, Margaret watched Mr. Hartwell closely. She could not resist observing whether the disclosure altered his manner. For an instant he appeared startled; yet the expression vanished almost as soon as it came. Instead of retreating into silence, he addressed Mr. Benson with renewed civility and soon engaged him in a discussion concerning the surrounding countryside.
Margaret listened with increasing satisfaction. Her uncle acquitted himself admirably. His observations were sensible, his remarks well chosen, and his good humour never descended into familiarity. Every answer he gave seemed to strengthen the favourable impression she hoped he might produce.
The gentlemen soon fell into conversation about improvements to the estate. Mr. Hartwell spoke at length of several recent alterations to the grounds and invited Mr. Benson to inspect them at his leisure. He even offered the use of certain maps and plans prepared by his surveyor, should they be of any interest.
Mrs. Benson exchanged a glance with her niece, expressing surprise without speaking. Margaret returned only a faint smile. She herself could scarcely account for what she witnessed.
Why had he changed so much? His former reserve had softened into something almost approaching openness. She could not persuade herself that such a transformation had occurred on her account. Their previous disagreements hardly seemed sufficient to produce so marked an alteration.
As they continued their walk, a slight rearrangement of the party occurred. Mrs. Benson, finding herself somewhat fatigued, accepted her husband's arm, leaving Margaret beside Mr. Hartwell. For a few moments neither spoke.
At length Margaret remarked that his presence had been wholly unexpected. She had understood, from several sources, that he was not expected at the estate until the following day.
Mr. Hartwell explained that business with his steward had required an earlier arrival than originally intended.
“The remainder of our party will arrive tomorrow,” he said. “Among them are several persons already known to you.”
Margaret inclined her head but offered no immediate reply.
After a brief pause he continued.
“There is one among them, however, whose acquaintance I particularly hope you will make. My sister has heard much of you and would be pleased, I believe, to meet you during your stay here.”
The request took her entirely by surprise. She scarcely knew how to answer. Whatever desire his sister possessed to know her must surely have originated with him, and the thought was not without its comfort.
They walked on for some distance without speaking further. Margaret felt neither ease nor confidence, yet she could not deny a sense of gratification. Such an invitation implied a degree of regard she had not expected.
By the time they reached the carriage, the others had fallen considerably behind. Mr. Hartwell suggested that she might rest for a few minutes within the house, but she declined. The evening was pleasant, and they remained together upon the lawn, each occupied with thoughts not easily expressed.

Margaret had little time to dwell on her first surprise before another followed it. Mr. Hartwell, whose manners had once seemed guarded almost to coldness, was speaking with her aunt and uncle as though no awkwardness existed between them at all. She had expected him to be civil; she had not expected this.
When her aunt mentioned their connection to her, Margaret watched him almost involuntarily. She wondered whether the knowledge would alter his behaviour. If it did, the change was slight. A momentary look of surprise crossed his face, but it passed quickly. Rather than withdrawing, he turned again to her uncle and continued the conversation with evident interest.
The effect on Margaret was immediate. It pleased her more than she cared to admit. There was comfort in seeing that those nearest to her could meet his notice without causing embarrassment to either side.
She listened closely. Her uncle spoke with his usual good sense, easy humour, and quiet confidence. Whenever Mr. Hartwell responded warmly to one of his remarks, she felt a private satisfaction she could not entirely suppress.
The conversation drifted to the estate and the surrounding country. Mr. Hartwell pointed out several recent improvements and urged Mr. Benson to examine them whenever he wished. He offered plans of the grounds, spoke of future projects, and seemed genuinely eager to continue the discussion.
Mrs. Benson caught her niece's eye. The look she gave required no explanation.
Margaret, however, had none to offer. She was still asking herself the same question. What accounted for this difference? His reserve had not disappeared altogether, but it no longer governed every word and action. She could not persuade herself that she was the cause. The idea seemed too improbable.
Their party changed formation a short while later. Mrs. Benson, tired by the walk, took her husband's arm instead, and Margaret found herself beside Mr. Hartwell.
For several moments they walked in silence.
At last she remarked that his arrival had been unexpected. She had been assured he would not return until the next day.
He admitted as much and explained that business with his steward had brought him back sooner than planned.
“The others will arrive tomorrow,” he said. “Some of them are already known to you.”
Margaret acknowledged this with a slight inclination of her head.
After a pause, he added, “There is another person among them whom I hope you will allow me to introduce. My sister has a strong wish to make your acquaintance while you remain here.”
The proposal startled her. For a second she could think of no answer at all.
Whatever interest his sister might have taken in her, it was impossible not to suspect the source. The thought carried a quiet satisfaction. At the very least, it suggested he did not view her with the unfavourable opinion she had once feared.
They continued on without much conversation. Margaret was not entirely at ease, but she was undeniably pleased. The invitation itself felt significant.
Before long they reached the carriage, well ahead of the others. Mr. Hartwell invited her to go inside and rest, but she declined. The air was mild, the evening calm. They remained on the lawn together, saying little, while each seemed occupied by thoughts better left unspoken.

His attention was roused, I saw, for his eyes rained down tears among the ashes, and he drew his breath in suffocating sighs. I stared full at him, and laughed scornfully. The clouded windows of hell flashed a moment towards me; the fiend which usually looked out, however, was so dimmed and drowned that I did not fear to hazard another sound of derision. “‘Get up, and begone out of my sight,’ said the mourner. “I guessed he uttered those words, at least, though his voice was hardly intelligible. “‘I beg your pardon,’ I replied. ‘But I loved Catherine too; and her brother requires attendance, which, for her sake, I shall supply. Now that she’s dead, I see her in Hindley: Hindley has exactly her eyes, if you had not tried to gouge them out, and made them black and red; and her—’  “‘Get up, wretched idiot, before I stamp you to death!’ he cried, making a movement that caused me to make one also. “‘But then,’ I continued, holding myself ready to flee, ‘if poor Catherine had trusted you, and assumed the ridiculous, contemptible, degrading title of Mrs. Heathcliff, she would soon have presented a similar picture! _She_ wouldn’t have borne your abominable behaviour quietly: her detestation and disgust must have found voice.’  “The back of the settle and Earnshaw’s person interposed between me and him; so instead of endeavouring to reach me, he snatched a dinner-knife from the table and flung it at my head. It struck beneath my ear, and stopped the sentence I was uttering; but, pulling it out, I sprang to the door and delivered another; which I hope went a little deeper than his missile. The last glimpse I caught of him was a furious rush on his part, checked by the embrace of his host; and both fell locked together on the hearth. In my flight through the kitchen I bid Joseph speed to his master; I knocked over Hareton, who was hanging a litter of puppies from a chair-back in the doorway; and, blessed as a soul escaped from purgatory, I bounded, leaped, and flew down the steep road; then, quitting its windings, shot direct across the moor, rolling over banks, and wading through marshes: precipitating myself, in fact, towards the beacon-light of the Grange. And far rather would I be condemned to a perpetual dwelling in the infernal regions than, even for one night, abide beneath the roof of Wuthering Heights again.”  Isabella ceased speaking, and took a drink of tea; then she rose, and bidding me put on her bonnet, and a great shawl I had brought, and turning a deaf ear to my entreaties for her to remain another hour, she stepped on to a chair, kissed Edgar’s and Catherine’s portraits, bestowed a similar salute on me, and descended to the carriage, accompanied by Fanny, who yelped wild with joy at recovering her mistress. She was driven away, never to revisit this neighbourhood: but a regular correspondence was established between her and my master when things were more settled. I believe her new abode was in the south, near London; there she had a son born a few months subsequent to her escape. He was christened Linton, and, from the first, she reported him to be an ailing, peevish creature. Mr. Heathcliff, meeting me one day in the village, inquired where she lived. I refused to tell. He remarked that it was not of any moment, only she must beware of coming to her brother: she should not be with him, if he had to keep her himself. Though I would give no information, he discovered, through some of the other servants, both her place of residence and the existence of the child. Still, he didn’t molest her: for which forbearance she might thank his aversion, I suppose. He often asked about the infant, when he saw me; and on hearing its name, smiled grimly, and observed: “They wish me to hate it too, do they?”  “I don’t think they wish you to know anything about it,” I answered. “But I’ll have it,” he said, “when I want it. They may reckon on that!”  Fortunately its mother died before the time arrived; some thirteen years after the decease of Catherine, when Linton was twelve, or a little more. On the day succeeding Isabella’s unexpected visit I had no opportunity of speaking to my master: he shunned conversation, and was fit for discussing nothing. When I could get him to listen, I saw it pleased him that his sister had left her husband; whom he abhorred with an intensity which the mildness of his nature would scarcely seem to allow.

His composure deserted him at last. I saw his hands tremble where they rested upon the arm of the chair, and though he strove to conceal it, grief betrayed itself in every movement. His eyes remained fixed upon the dying fire, yet it seemed to me he saw nothing before him. The room was silent save for the uneven sound of his breathing.
I watched him for some moments and, finding his misery a temptation too strong to resist, spoke with deliberate cruelty.
“You mourn her now,” I said, “yet you were never content while she lived.”
He lifted his head slowly. There was something in his expression that might once have warned me to silence, but suffering had dulled its force.
“Leave this house,” he answered.
The words were scarcely audible.
“I shall leave when I choose,” I replied. “Her memory gives me as much right to speak as it does you. She had kindness enough for all around her. Had she lived longer, she might have judged more clearly the worth of those who claimed to love her.”
He rose so abruptly that I stepped backward.
“Take care,” he said.
“Or what?” I returned. “Will you frighten me into agreement? No. I knew her better than you suppose. She would not have endured this bitterness forever. Whatever attachment bound her to you, it could not have survived unchanged.”
At that he seized a heavy book from the table and hurled it across the room. It struck the wall beside me and fell open at my feet.
The violence accomplished little beyond convincing me to depart.
I reached the doorway and turned once more.
“You are feared by many,” I said, “but fear is not devotion. Remember that.”
Then I fled.
Behind me I heard a crash of overturned furniture and a cry that sounded more wounded than angry. I did not pause to learn the cause. Down the passage, through the yard, and across the fields I ran, urged onward by equal measures of alarm and relief.
When at last the house disappeared behind the rising ground, I slowed my pace. The evening wind was cold, but never had open country seemed more welcoming. I would sooner have wandered the moors until morning than passed another hour beneath that roof.
Here the narrator paused. After a few moments she resumed in a calmer tone.
She left the district shortly afterward and settled many miles away among distant relations. Letters arrived from her occasionally, though they became less frequent as the years passed. News reached us that she had married again and borne a daughter, a delicate child who was said to resemble her mother closely.
As for the man she had fled, he remained unchanged in reputation, if not in spirit. From time to time he questioned those who had known her, asking where she lived and whether she prospered. Few answered willingly.
“Let her remain where she is,” he remarked once when I refused him information. “Distance serves us both.”
Yet he never spoke those words without a bitterness that suggested the opposite.
Years later, when her name was mentioned in his hearing, he would fall silent. Whatever else time had altered, it had not entirely subdued that old wound.

The effort to master himself was beginning to fail. Though he sat motionless by the fire, there was no mistaking the strain in him. His hands shook now and then, and his gaze remained fixed on the embers as if he expected them to answer him. The room had grown very still.
I watched longer than I should have. At last I spoke.
“You grieve for her now,” I said, “but you gave her little peace while she was here.”
He looked up. The expression might once have intimidated me; grief had worn it thin.
“Go,” he said quietly. “Leave me.”
“I will, in time.”
The answer escaped me before prudence could intervene. “But do not pretend you alone have the right to remember her. Others loved her too.”
For a moment he said nothing.
I continued, emboldened by his silence. “You speak as though devotion excuses everything. It does not. She saw more than you believed she did.”
That reached him.
He rose suddenly, and I retreated a step toward the door.
“Enough,” he said.
Still I persisted. “Had she lived, she might have judged you differently in the end.”
A book was the nearest object to his hand. He snatched it up and threw it. It struck the wall beside me with a force that ended the conversation more effectively than any argument.
I was already backing away.
At the threshold I turned once.
“People obey you because they fear you,” I said. “That is not the same thing.”
Then I was gone.
I heard furniture crash behind me, followed by a sound that was neither wholly rage nor wholly sorrow. I had no wish to discover which. Through the yard and across the fields I ran without stopping.
Only when the house had vanished behind the hills did I slow. The air was sharp and cold, but it felt like freedom. I would gladly have spent the night out on the moor rather than return.
The narrator rested for a moment before continuing.
Not long afterward she left the area altogether. She settled far away with relatives, and for a few years letters arrived with some regularity. Later they became rarer. We eventually learned she had remarried and had a daughter of fragile health, though by all accounts a cheerful disposition.
The man she left behind remained much the same to outward appearances. Occasionally he would ask after her. Sometimes he sought news indirectly, pretending little interest in the answer.
“Let her stay where she has chosen to be,” he said to me once.
The words sounded indifferent enough. Yet there was something in the way he spoke them that suggested indifference was the last thing he felt.
As the years passed, her name came up less often. When it did, he usually fell silent. Time had changed many things around him, but not that.

For these my present friends, as they are to me nothing, so in nothing bless them, and to nothing are they welcome. Uncover, dogs, and lap. [_The dishes  are uncovered and prove to be full of lukewarm water._]  SOME SPEAK. What does his lordship mean? SOME OTHER. I know not. TIMON. May you a better feast never behold, You knot of mouth-friends! Smoke and lukewarm water Is your perfection. This is Timon’s last, Who, stuck and spangled with your flatteries, Washes it off and sprinkles in your faces Your reeking villainy. [_Throws water in their faces._]  Live loathed, and long, Most smiling, smooth, detested parasites, Courteous destroyers, affable wolves, meek bears, You fools of fortune, trencher-friends, time’s flies, Cap-and-knee slaves, vapours, and minute-jacks! Of man and beast the infinite malady Crust you quite o’er! [_They stand_.] What, dost thou go? Soft! Take thy physic first; thou too, and thou! Stay, I will lend thee money, borrow none. [_He attacks them and forces them out._]  What, all in motion? Henceforth be no feast Whereat a villain’s not a welcome guest. Burn, house! Sink Athens! Henceforth hated be Of Timon man and all humanity! [_Exit._]  Enter Timon’s Friends, the Senators with other Lords. FIRST FRIEND. How now, my lords? SECOND FRIEND. Know you the quality of Lord Timon’s fury? THIRD FRIEND. Push! Did you see my cap? FOURTH FRIEND. I have lost my gown. FIRST FRIEND. He’s but a mad lord, and nought but humours sways him. He gave me a jewel th’ other day, and now he has beat it out of my hat. Did you see my jewel? THIRD FRIEND. Did you see my cap? SECOND FRIEND. Here ’tis. FOURTH FRIEND. Here lies my gown. FIRST FRIEND. Let’s make no stay. SECOND FRIEND. Lord Timon’s mad. THIRD FRIEND. I feel’t upon my bones. FOURTH FRIEND. One day he gives us diamonds, next day stones. [_Exeunt._]     ACT IV  SCENE I. Without the walls of Athens   Enter Timon. TIMON. Let me look back upon thee. O thou wall That girdles in those wolves, dive in the earth And fence not Athens! Matrons, turn incontinent! Obedience fail in children! Slaves and fools, Pluck the grave wrinkled senate from the bench And minister in their steads! To general filths Convert, o’ th’ instant, green virginity, Do’t in your parents’ eyes! Bankrupts, hold fast; Rather than render back, out with your knives And cut your trusters’ throats! Bound servants, steal! Large-handed robbers your grave masters are, And pill by law. Maid, to thy master’s bed, Thy mistress is o’ th’ brothel. Son of sixteen, Pluck the lined crutch from thy old limping sire, With it beat out his brains! Piety and fear, Religion to the gods, peace, justice, truth, Domestic awe, night-rest and neighbourhood, Instruction, manners, mysteries and trades, Degrees, observances, customs and laws, Decline to your confounding contraries, And let confusion live! Plagues incident to men, Your potent and infectious fevers heap On Athens, ripe for stroke! Thou cold sciatica, Cripple our senators, that their limbs may halt As lamely as their manners! Lust and liberty, Creep in the minds and marrows of our youth, That ’gainst the stream of virtue they may strive And drown themselves in riot! Itches, blains, Sow all th’ Athenian bosoms, and their crop Be general leprosy! Breath infect breath, That their society, as their friendship, may Be merely poison! Nothing I’ll bear from thee But nakedness, thou detestable town! Take thou that too, with multiplying bans! Timon will to the woods, where he shall find Th’ unkindest beast more kinder than mankind. The gods confound—hear me, you good gods all!— Th’ Athenians both within and out that wall, And grant, as Timon grows, his hate may grow To the whole race of mankind, high and low! Amen. [_Exit._]  SCENE II. Athens. A room in Timon’s house  Enter Flavius with two or three Servants. FIRST SERVANT. Hear you, Master Steward, where’s our master? Are we undone, cast off, nothing remaining? FLAVIUS. Alack, my fellows, what should I say to you? Let me be recorded by the righteous gods, I am as poor as you. FIRST SERVANT. Such a house broke? So noble a master fall’n? All gone, and not One friend to take his fortune by the arm And go along with him? SECOND SERVANT. As we do turn our backs From our companion, thrown into his grave, So his familiars to his buried fortunes Slink all away, leave their false vows with him, Like empty purses picked; and his poor self, A dedicated beggar to the air, With his disease of all-shunned poverty, Walks, like contempt, alone.—More of our fellows.

Enter ARISTON alone.
ARISTON.
Now let them banquet where they praised me most,
And count their cups among their reckonings.
Not one remains whose hand would lift my burden,
Though all were eager when my board was spread.
The gold is gone, and with it goes their friendship.
I know them now.
Let every hall that echoed with their laughter
Stand empty as their promises. Let music
Forget the doors it once made glad. The guest
Who smiled upon my fortune and my table
Has vanished with the season of my wealth.
Yet I was blind.
I called these hollow men companions.
I measured hearts by bows and courteous phrases,
And purchased thanks that lasted but an hour.
Enter STEWARD and two SERVANTS.
STEWARD.
My lord, we sought you through the city streets.
ARISTON.
Why seek me?
STEWARD.
Because we fear for you and for ourselves.
The house is left deserted. Creditors
Have sealed the gates. The servants wander idle,
Not knowing where to turn.
FIRST SERVANT.
Good master, speak.
The poorest among us grieves to see this change.
ARISTON.
Grieve not for me.
The world has shown its face at last.
I thank necessity for that instruction.
The flatterer has fled; the borrower forgets;
The friend who swore eternal faith remembers
No syllable of all his former vows.
SECOND SERVANT.
There are some honest men.
ARISTON.
If so, they hide.
I searched for one and found a hundred masks.
The city breeds fair words and barren deeds.
Its marketplaces traffic not in goods alone,
But in affection, honour, gratitude.
STEWARD.
Will you not stay and make your peace with fortune?
ARISTON.
No. Fortune and I are strangers now.
Let those who worship her attend her court.
I shall go where the woods ask nothing of me,
Where no man praises what he means to steal.
FIRST SERVANT.
Then we will follow.
ARISTON.
No.
Keep what remains of your poor livelihoods.
I go alone.
STEWARD.
The gods protect you, sir.
ARISTON.
If they have watched in silence until now,
They may continue so. Farewell to all.
Exit ARISTON.
FIRST SERVANT.
A noble heart brought low.
SECOND SERVANT.
And lower still by those who fed upon it.
STEWARD.
Come. We must gather what the ruin leaves.
When wealth departs, companions vanish first;
Yet honest sorrow lingers after both.
Exeunt.

Enter ARISTON alone.
ARISTON.
Let them feast without me.
The same men who crowded round my table will scarcely spare a thought now that my coffers are empty. While the wine flowed, I was their dearest friend. Now the gold is spent, and so are their affections.
How readily I believed them.
Every hall that once rang with greetings and laughter seems different in memory. I mistook noise for loyalty. A pleasant word, a flattering smile, a show of concern—these passed for friendship in my eyes.
No longer.
Enter STEWARD and two SERVANTS.
STEWARD.
My lord, we have searched everywhere for you.
ARISTON.
And why?
STEWARD.
The house is in disorder. Creditors press their claims. The gates are watched, the servants uncertain what is to become of them. We feared you had left us altogether.
FIRST SERVANT.
It troubles us deeply to see your fortunes changed so suddenly.
ARISTON.
Do not waste pity on me. What has happened has at least taught me something. Prosperity gathered people around me; adversity has shown me who they were.
The borrower forgets. The flatterer disappears. Those who swore they loved me cannot even remember the words they spoke.
SECOND SERVANT.
Surely not everyone is false.
ARISTON.
Perhaps not. Yet I looked for sincerity and found performance. This city values appearances above all things. Gratitude is traded away as easily as coin.
STEWARD.
What will you do now?
ARISTON.
Leave.
Let others chase favour and fortune. I have no appetite for either. The forests are more honest than crowded streets. Trees ask nothing, promise nothing, and deceive no one.
FIRST SERVANT.
Allow us to come with you.
ARISTON.
No. You must think of yourselves. Keep what work you can find and make better use of trust than I did.
STEWARD.
Then may heaven watch over you.
ARISTON.
Heaven has been a distant observer. Still, farewell.
Exit ARISTON.
FIRST SERVANT.
I never thought I would see him brought to this.
SECOND SERVANT.
His wealth failed him; his friends failed him sooner.
STEWARD.
Come. There is still much to set in order. When fortune leaves a house, it takes most of its visitors with it. What remains is seldom comfort, but sometimes it is truth.
Exeunt.

If it be so, this very thing only serves further to demonstrate the malicious craft of these pestilent spirits. For so great is the influence of probity and chastity, that all men, or almost all men, are moved by the praise of these virtues; nor is any man so depraved by vice, but he hath some feeling of honour left in him. So that, unless the devil sometimes transformed himself, as Scripture says, into an angel of light,[110] he could not compass his deceitful purpose. Accordingly, in public, a bold impurity fills the ear of the people with noisy clamour; in private, a feigned chastity speaks in scarce audible whispers to a few: an open stage is provided for shameful things, but on the praiseworthy the curtain falls: grace hides, disgrace flaunts: a wicked deed draws an overflowing house, a virtuous speech finds scarce a hearer, as though purity were to be blushed at, impurity boasted of. Where else can such confusion reign, but in devils' temples? Where, but in the haunts of deceit? For the secret precepts are given as a sop to the virtuous, who are few in number; the wicked examples are exhibited to encourage the vicious, who are countless. Where and when those initiated in the mysteries of Cœlestis received any good instructions, we know not. What we do know is, that before her shrine, in which her image is set, and amidst a vast crowd gathering from all quarters, and standing closely packed together, we were intensely interested spectators of the games which were going on, and saw, as we pleased to turn the eye, on this side a grand display of harlots, on the other the virgin goddess: we saw this virgin worshipped with prayer and with obscene rites. There we saw no shamefaced mimes, no actress overburdened with modesty: all that the obscene rites demanded was fully complied with. We were plainly shown what was pleasing to the virgin deity, and the matron who witnessed the spectacle returned home from the temple a wiser woman. Some, indeed, of the more prudent women turned their faces from the immodest movements of the players, and learned the art of wickedness by a furtive regard. For they were restrained, by the modest demeanour due to men, from looking boldly at the immodest gestures; but much more were they restrained from condemning with chaste heart the sacred rites of her whom they adored. And yet this licentiousness--which, if practised in one's home, could only be done there in secret--was practised as a public lesson in the temple; and if any modesty remained in men, it was occupied in marvelling that wickedness which men could not unrestrainedly commit should be part of the religious teaching of the gods, and that to omit its exhibition should incur the anger of the gods. What spirit can that be, which by a hidden inspiration stirs men's corruption, and goads them to adultery, and feeds on the full-fledged iniquity, unless it be the same that finds pleasure in such religious ceremonies, sets in the temples images of devils, and loves to see in play the images of vices; that whispers in secret some righteous sayings to deceive the few who are good, and scatters in public invitations to profligacy, to gain possession of the millions who are wicked? 27. _That the obscenities of those plays which the Romans consecrated in order to propitiate their gods, contributed largely to the overthrow of public order._  Cicero, a weighty man, and a philosopher in his way, when about to be made edile, wished the citizens to understand[111] that, among the other duties of his magistracy, he must propitiate Flora by the celebration of games. And these games are reckoned devout in proportion to their lewdness. In another place,[112] and when he was now consul, and the state in great peril, he says that games had been celebrated for ten days together, and that nothing had been omitted which could pacify the gods: as if it had not been more satisfactory to irritate the gods by temperance, than to pacify them by debauchery; and to provoke their hate by honest living, than soothe it by such unseemly grossness. For no matter how cruel was the ferocity of those men who were threatening the state, and on whose account the gods were being propitiated: it could not have been more hurtful than the alliance of gods who were won with the foulest vices. To avert the danger which threatened men's bodies, the gods were conciliated in a fashion that drove virtue from their spirits; and the gods did not enrol themselves as defenders of the battlements against the besiegers, until they had first stormed and sacked the morality of the citizens.

For if these things be defended under the name of religion, they furnish the strongest evidence against those who defend them. For men are not wholly deprived of judgment, however much habit and corruption may cloud their minds. Even among those most given to vice there remains some faint admiration for virtue. Therefore the authors of deception, if they wish to ensnare the many, must borrow the appearance of what is good. They praise temperance in words and reward excess in practice; they commend humility with their lips while exalting pride before the eyes of the multitude.
Thus the crowd is taught by spectacle rather than by doctrine. Few listen to sober instruction, but many delight in public display. The result is that honorable precepts are spoken quietly to small assemblies, while disgraceful examples are exhibited openly and celebrated. The lesson most eagerly learned is not the one that is taught, but the one that is applauded.
Consider the festivals that are instituted in honor of divine powers. The people gather in great numbers. Songs are sung, ceremonies are performed, and sacred names are invoked. Yet amid these observances are introduced entertainments whose purpose is not the cultivation of virtue but the encouragement of passion. Those who attend are told that such things are acceptable to heaven. What wonder, then, if they return home with weakened discipline and corrupted desires?
For men are more readily persuaded by what they see than by what they hear. If modesty is praised while shamelessness is displayed, the display triumphs over the praise. If restraint is commended while indulgence is honored before the public eye, indulgence becomes the true object of imitation. The multitude does not inquire what is best; it follows what is celebrated.
What spirit, then, delights in such contradictions? What power urges men toward vice while pretending concern for their improvement? Surely no friend of righteousness would seek to heal conduct by means that inflame corruption. Such practices resemble a physician who commends health while secretly administering poison.
Hence it is not surprising that commonwealths decline when they mistake moral decay for religious devotion. Citizens labor to appease supernatural powers through ceremonies, yet neglect the virtues by which societies are preserved. They fear offending the gods by omitting festivals, but do not fear offending justice through greed, luxury, and dishonesty.
In this way the foundations of public order are gradually weakened. The walls of a city may stand firm, its armies may remain numerous, and its treasuries full; yet if virtue is abandoned, ruin has already entered within the gates. No enemy is more dangerous than the corruption that grows under the protection of sacred approval.

If such customs are defended as acts of religion, they become an argument against themselves. Human beings do not entirely lose their sense of right and wrong, even when vice has become familiar. However corrupt a society may be, people still recognize virtue when they encounter it. Because of this, those who wish to mislead others rarely present evil in its true form. They dress it in respectable language. Temperance is praised, while excess is tolerated. Humility is admired in speeches, but pride receives the applause.
The greater influence comes not from what is taught but from what is displayed. A quiet lesson reaches a few listeners; a public spectacle reaches thousands. When honorable principles are confined to words and dishonorable conduct is turned into entertainment, the example outweighs the instruction. People remember what they see celebrated.
One may observe this in public festivals. Crowds gather, sacred ceremonies are performed, and divine names are invoked with solemnity. Yet alongside these rites appear amusements that excite vanity, appetite, and passion. Those attending are encouraged to regard such things as acceptable, even admirable. It should surprise no one if they leave less disciplined than when they arrived.
Human beings learn powerfully through imitation. If modesty is praised but shamelessness is put on display, the display leaves the deeper impression. If self-restraint is commended while indulgence is rewarded with attention and admiration, indulgence becomes the model that many follow. The public example quietly defeats the spoken rule.
What sort of spirit delights in this arrangement? Certainly not one devoted to goodness. No sincere guide would encourage corruption while claiming to improve character. It is like offering poison under the name of medicine.
Nor should we wonder when societies suffer the consequences. People become diligent in ceremonies yet careless about virtue. They fear neglecting a festival more than neglecting justice. They concern themselves with outward observances while greed, dishonesty, and excess spread unchecked.
A nation may possess strong defenses, wealthy institutions, and every appearance of stability. Yet when moral discipline decays, the danger is already within. External enemies can do great harm, but the corruption that receives public approval is often more destructive than any army at the gates.

She was a little nervous about it just at first, the two creatures got so close to her, one on each side, and opened their eyes and mouths so _very_ wide, but she gained courage as she went on. Her listeners were perfectly quiet till she got to the part about her repeating “_You are old, Father William_,” to the Caterpillar, and the words all coming different, and then the Mock Turtle drew a long breath, and said “That’s very curious.”  “It’s all about as curious as it can be,” said the Gryphon. “It all came different!” the Mock Turtle repeated thoughtfully. “I should like to hear her try and repeat something now. Tell her to begin.” He looked at the Gryphon as if he thought it had some kind of authority over Alice. “Stand up and repeat ‘’_Tis the voice of the sluggard_,’” said the Gryphon. “How the creatures order one about, and make one repeat lessons!” thought Alice; “I might as well be at school at once.” However, she got up, and began to repeat it, but her head was so full of the Lobster Quadrille, that she hardly knew what she was saying, and the words came very queer indeed:—  “’Tis the voice of the Lobster; I heard him declare, “You have baked me too brown, I must sugar my hair.” As a duck with its eyelids, so he with his nose Trims his belt and his buttons, and turns out his toes.”  [later editions continued as follows When the sands are all dry, he is gay as a lark, And will talk in contemptuous tones of the Shark, But, when the tide rises and sharks are around, His voice has a timid and tremulous sound.] “That’s different from what _I_ used to say when I was a child,” said the Gryphon. “Well, I never heard it before,” said the Mock Turtle; “but it sounds uncommon nonsense.”  Alice said nothing; she had sat down with her face in her hands, wondering if anything would _ever_ happen in a natural way again. “I should like to have it explained,” said the Mock Turtle. “She can’t explain it,” said the Gryphon hastily. “Go on with the next verse.”  “But about his toes?” the Mock Turtle persisted. “How _could_ he turn them out with his nose, you know?”  “It’s the first position in dancing.” Alice said; but was dreadfully puzzled by the whole thing, and longed to change the subject. “Go on with the next verse,” the Gryphon repeated impatiently: “it begins ‘_I passed by his garden_.’”  Alice did not dare to disobey, though she felt sure it would all come wrong, and she went on in a trembling voice:—  “I passed by his garden, and marked, with one eye, How the Owl and the Panther were sharing a pie—”  [later editions continued as follows The Panther took pie-crust, and gravy, and meat, While the Owl had the dish as its share of the treat. When the pie was all finished, the Owl, as a boon, Was kindly permitted to pocket the spoon: While the Panther received knife and fork with a growl, And concluded the banquet—]   “What _is_ the use of repeating all that stuff,” the Mock Turtle interrupted, “if you don’t explain it as you go on? It’s by far the most confusing thing _I_ ever heard!”  “Yes, I think you’d better leave off,” said the Gryphon: and Alice was only too glad to do so. “Shall we try another figure of the Lobster Quadrille?” the Gryphon went on. “Or would you like the Mock Turtle to sing you a song?”  “Oh, a song, please, if the Mock Turtle would be so kind,” Alice replied, so eagerly that the Gryphon said, in a rather offended tone, “Hm! No accounting for tastes! Sing her ‘_Turtle Soup_,’ will you, old fellow?”  The Mock Turtle sighed deeply, and began, in a voice sometimes choked with sobs, to sing this:—  “Beautiful Soup, so rich and green, Waiting in a hot tureen! Who for such dainties would not stoop? Soup of the evening, beautiful Soup! Soup of the evening, beautiful Soup! Beau—ootiful Soo—oop! Beau—ootiful Soo—oop! Soo—oop of the e—e—evening, Beautiful, beautiful Soup! “Beautiful Soup! Who cares for fish, Game, or any other dish? Who would not give all else for two p ennyworth only of beautiful Soup? Pennyworth only of beautiful Soup? Beau—ootiful Soo—oop! Beau—ootiful Soo—oop! Soo—oop of the e—e—evening, Beautiful, beauti—FUL SOUP!”   “Chorus again!” cried the Gryphon, and the Mock Turtle had just begun to repeat it, when a cry of “The trial’s beginning!” was heard in the distance.

She hesitated at first, for the two extraordinary creatures had drawn so near that their noses almost touched her sleeves. One blinked solemnly; the other smiled without appearing to know why. Still, finding neither intended to bite, she gathered confidence and continued her account.
Both listeners remained silent until she reached the part concerning the upside-down clock that had insisted upon striking tomorrow before today. Then the larger creature gave a long whistle.
“That is remarkably peculiar,” he observed.
“Peculiar?” cried the smaller one. “It is peculiarity itself!”
“The clock struck tomorrow?” repeated the larger creature thoughtfully. “I should like to hear something else. Let her recite.”
He nodded toward his companion, who immediately assumed an air of authority.
“Stand up and repeat ‘The Busy Bee,’” he commanded.
Alice was not at all pleased.
“They are forever ordering people about,” she thought. “One would imagine lessons were the principal amusement of the world.”
Nevertheless she rose and began. Unfortunately her thoughts were still occupied with the clock, and the verse emerged in a very altered form indeed:
“The curious Bee sat under the sea,
And polished his wings with jam;
He borrowed a hat from a fishing cat,
And dined upon lavender ham.”
The larger creature shook his head.
“That is not how it goes.”
“No,” agreed the smaller one, “though I cannot say how it ought to go either.”
Alice sat down quickly, feeling rather embarrassed.
“What does it mean?” asked the larger creature.
“I do not know,” said Alice.
“Then why say it?”
“I was told to.”
This answer appeared reasonable to her, though neither of the creatures seemed satisfied.
“Try another verse,” said the smaller one.
Alice sighed.
“Beyond the hill where the teacups grow,
An Owl and a Minnow played cards;
The Owl lost twice, but the Minnow paid
In buttons and peppermint shards.”
The larger creature looked alarmed.
“Can minnows carry buttons?”
“Only on formal occasions,” said the smaller one.
Before Alice could protest, both creatures began arguing over the matter. Their disagreement grew louder and more complicated until neither appeared to remember what they were discussing.
At length the larger creature stopped.
“Enough of verses,” he declared. “Let us have a song.”
“A song!” cried the other. “A song at once!”
Without waiting for permission, he began:
“O marvelous Pie, so round and high,
Revolving slowly through the sky!
Who would not trade a crown or two
For one small slice of splendid Pie?
Pie in the morning,
Pie in the rain,
Pie by the windowpane!”
He paused dramatically, then continued in an even louder voice:
“O glorious Pie, of berry and breeze,
Balanced with effortless expertise!
Whether it whistles or whether it sings,
Pie is the finest of edible things!”
“Again!” cried the larger creature.
The singer drew breath to begin once more, but at that moment a distant voice rang across the field:
“The inspection is starting!”
Immediately both creatures sprang to their feet.
“The inspection!” they shouted together, and dashed away, leaving Alice more puzzled than ever.

At first she felt a little uneasy. The two strange creatures had edged so close that she could hear one of them breathing through its nose. The other stared at her with such interest that she wondered whether she had accidentally become part of the entertainment.
Still, they seemed harmless enough, so she went on with her story.
They listened without interruption until she described the clock that insisted on announcing tomorrow before today. That detail produced a strong reaction.
“Well, that is odd,” said the larger creature after a moment.
“Odd?” said the smaller. “It is wonderfully odd.”
“The clock truly did that?”
Alice nodded.
“I should like to hear her recite something,” the larger creature decided.
His companion immediately pointed at her.
“Stand up and say a poem.”
Alice thought this extremely unfair. Everyone she met seemed determined to assign her lessons.
Even so, she stood and tried to comply. The trouble was that her mind was still occupied with impossible clocks, and the poem emerged rather differently than intended:
“The curious Bee beneath the sea
Polished his wings with jam;
He borrowed a hat from a fishing cat
And feasted on lavender ham.”
“That sounds wrong,” remarked the larger creature.
“It certainly sounds unusual,” said the smaller.
Alice sat down again.
“What does it mean?” asked the larger creature.
“I have no idea.”
“Then why recite it?”
“Because you asked me to.”
Neither creature seemed able to argue with that, though both looked disappointed.
“Another verse,” said the smaller one.
With a sigh, Alice continued:
“Beyond the hill where teacups grow,
An Owl and a Minnow played cards;
The Owl lost twice, and the Minnow paid
In buttons and peppermint shards.”
“Can a minnow carry buttons?” the larger creature wondered aloud.
“On special occasions, certainly,” replied the smaller.
This led to a debate so lengthy and so confusing that they soon forgot what had started it. When at last the discussion collapsed under its own weight, the larger creature announced that poetry had been exhausted and that music was required.
“A song!” cried the smaller creature enthusiastically.
Without further ceremony he launched into one:
“O marvelous Pie, so round and high,
Drifting across the sky!
Who would not trade a treasure chest
For one delightful bite of Pie?
Pie in the morning,
Pie in the rain,
Pie by the windowpane!”
He was preparing to repeat the chorus when a distant shout interrupted him.
“The inspection is starting!”
The effect was immediate. Both creatures leapt up.
“The inspection!” they cried.
And before Alice could ask what was being inspected, they had vanished, leaving her alone and no nearer to understanding anything than she had been before.

But to render this acuteness at all successful in the end, the wind and the sea must be the whaleman’s allies; for of what present avail to the becalmed or windbound mariner is the skill that assures him he is exactly ninety-three leagues and a quarter from his port? Inferable from these statements, are many collateral subtile matters touching the chase of whales. The ship tore on; leaving such a furrow in the sea as when a cannon-ball, missent, becomes a plough-share and turns up the level field. “By salt and hemp!” cried Stubb, “but this swift motion of the deck creeps up one’s legs and tingles at the heart. This ship and I are two brave fellows!—Ha, ha! Some one take me up, and launch me, spine-wise, on the sea,—for by live-oaks! my spine’s a keel.
