Ha, ha! we go the gait that leaves no dust behind!”  “There she blows—she blows!—she blows!—right ahead!” was now the mast-head cry. “Aye, aye!” cried Stubb, “I knew it—ye can’t escape—blow on and split your spout, O whale! the mad fiend himself is after ye! blow your trump—blister your lungs!—Ahab will dam off your blood, as a miller shuts his watergate upon the stream!”  And Stubb did but speak out for well nigh all that crew. The frenzies of the chase had by this time worked them bubblingly up, like old wine worked anew. Whatever pale fears and forebodings some of them might have felt before; these were not only now kept out of sight through the growing awe of Ahab, but they were broken up, and on all sides routed, as timid prairie hares that scatter before the bounding bison. The hand of Fate had snatched all their souls; and by the stirring perils of the previous day; the rack of the past night’s suspense; the fixed, unfearing, blind, reckless way in which their wild craft went plunging towards its flying mark; by all these things, their hearts were bowled along. The wind that made great bellies of their sails, and rushed the vessel on by arms invisible as irresistible; this seemed the symbol of that unseen agency which so enslaved them to the race. They were one man, not thirty. For as the one ship that held them all; though it was put together of all contrasting things—oak, and maple, and pine wood; iron, and pitch, and hemp—yet all these ran into each other in the one concrete hull, which shot on its way, both balanced and directed by the long central keel; even so, all the individualities of the crew, this man’s valor, that man’s fear; guilt and guiltiness, all varieties were welded into oneness, and were all directed to that fatal goal which Ahab their one lord and keel did point to. The rigging lived. The mast-heads, like the tops of tall palms, were outspreadingly tufted with arms and legs. Clinging to a spar with one hand, some reached forth the other with impatient wavings; others, shading their eyes from the vivid sunlight, sat far out on the rocking yards; all the spars in full bearing of mortals, ready and ripe for their fate. Ah! how they still strove through that infinite blueness to seek out the thing that might destroy them! “Why sing ye not out for him, if ye see him?” cried Ahab, when, after the lapse of some minutes since the first cry, no more had been heard. “Sway me up, men; ye have been deceived; not Moby Dick casts one odd jet that way, and then disappears.”  It was even so; in their headlong eagerness, the men had mistaken some other thing for the whale-spout, as the event itself soon proved; for hardly had Ahab reached his perch; hardly was the rope belayed to its pin on deck, when he struck the key-note to an orchestra, that made the air vibrate as with the combined discharges of rifles. The triumphant halloo of thirty buckskin lungs was heard, as—much nearer to the ship than the place of the imaginary jet, less than a mile ahead—Moby Dick bodily burst into view! For not by any calm and indolent spoutings; not by the peaceable gush of that mystic fountain in his head, did the White Whale now reveal his vicinity; but by the far more wondrous phenomenon of breaching. Rising with his utmost velocity from the furthest depths, the Sperm Whale thus booms his entire bulk into the pure element of air, and piling up a mountain of dazzling foam, shows his place to the distance of seven miles and more.

The vessel pressed onward through the long heaving swells, cutting the ocean with a steady violence that sent spray flying from her bows. Every spar groaned with the strain, and yet the ship seemed eager for the pursuit, as though some hidden desire possessed her timbers. The men moved about the decks with restless expectation. Each glance cast toward the horizon carried the same question, and each moment of delay sharpened it.
“There again! There again! Off the larboard bow!” came the cry from above.
The words passed through the crew like a spark through dry grass. Heads turned at once. Hands tightened upon ropes and rails. Even those who had spoken little during the morning now stared ahead with shining eyes.
The captain stood unmoving for a moment. Then he stepped forward and fixed his gaze upon the distant sea.
“Keep her course,” he said. “Let no man lose sight of that place.”
The ocean before them appeared empty save for a patch of white disturbance far ahead. Yet all knew the sign. A whale had broken the surface and vanished again beneath the dark water.
Excitement spread among them. Old fears, doubts, and warnings were forgotten. The long chase, the weary watches, the uncertain days behind them—all seemed justified by that fleeting glimpse. Their purpose narrowed to a single point upon the sea.
The ship rushed onward. Wind filled the sails until they swelled like great clouds overhead. The masts bent slightly under the pressure, and the deck trembled beneath the continual drive forward.
Again the lookout shouted.
This time there could be no mistake.
The whale erupted from the depths in a vast arching leap. Water cascaded from its shining body. For an instant it hung against the sky, immense and magnificent, before crashing back into the sea with a report like distant artillery. Foam burst outward in every direction.
A great cry rose from the ship.
The crew surged toward the rails. Some pointed; others laughed aloud. The sight overwhelmed caution. The creature seemed less an animal than a force of nature, appearing suddenly from the hidden world below and declaring its presence to all who watched.
The captain’s eyes never left it.
“There is our quarry,” he said quietly.
The men heard him and answered with renewed energy. Whatever fate awaited them beyond that horizon, they were committed now. Ship, sea, wind, and crew had become joined in a single pursuit, driving after the mighty shape that moved before them through the endless blue.

The ship drove hard across the swells, her bow throwing up sheets of spray as she carved through the water. Every beam and spar complained under the strain, but there was something eager in her motion. She seemed to lean into the chase. Around the deck the crew worked with growing impatience, their attention returning again and again to the horizon.
A cry rang down from the masthead.
“There! Off the larboard bow!”
At once the mood changed. Men stopped what they were doing. A few stepped to the rail. Others shaded their eyes and searched the distance. The call had awakened the same hope in all of them.
The captain looked ahead without speaking. After a brief pause he gave his order.
“Hold the course. Keep your eyes on that water.”
Far off, a patch of white turbulence marked the sea. It was small enough to vanish if one looked away for a moment, yet everyone aboard understood what it meant. Something immense had surfaced and slipped below again.
The sight was enough. Days of uncertainty faded from memory. Fatigue no longer mattered. The chase, which had stretched on through long watches and empty horizons, suddenly felt alive again.
Wind pressed heavily into the sails. The vessel answered, surging forward. The rigging hummed, the masts flexed, and a faint shudder ran beneath every footstep on deck.
Then the lookout shouted once more.
This time nobody doubted.
The whale burst from the sea in a tremendous leap. Water streamed from its body as it rose, suspended for a heartbeat against the bright sky. Then it crashed down. The impact rolled across the water like cannon fire, and a wide circle of foam exploded around it.
A roar went up from the crew.
Men pointed and called to one another. Some laughed from sheer excitement. The creature seemed almost unreal—less a living thing than a sudden manifestation of the ocean itself.
The captain watched in silence.
At last he spoke.
“There is the one we seek.”
His words carried across the deck. No speech was needed after that. Every man knew his part. Drawn together by the same purpose, ship and crew pressed on through the wind and sunlight, following the dark shape moving ahead across the open sea.

Yet, indeed, I am in earnest. I speak nothing but the truth. He still loves me, and we are engaged.”  Jane looked at her doubtingly. “Oh, Lizzy! it cannot be. I know how much you dislike him.”  “You know nothing of the matter. _That_ is all to be forgot. Perhaps I did not always love him so well as I do now; but in such cases as these a good memory is unpardonable. This is the last time I shall ever remember it myself.”  Miss Bennet still looked all amazement. Elizabeth again, and more seriously, assured her of its truth. “Good heaven! can it be really so? Yet now I must believe you,” cried Jane. “My dear, dear Lizzy, I would, I do congratulate you; but are you certain--forgive the question--are you quite certain that you can be happy with him?”  “There can be no doubt of that. It is settled between us already that we are to be the happiest couple in the world. But are you pleased, Jane? Shall you like to have such a brother?”  “Very, very much. Nothing could give either Bingley or myself more delight. But we considered it, we talked of it as impossible. And do you really love him quite well enough? Oh, Lizzy! do anything rather than marry without affection. Are you quite sure that you feel what you ought to do?”  “Oh, yes! You will only think I feel _more_ than I ought to do when I tell you all.”  “What do you mean?”  “Why, I must confess that I love him better than I do Bingley. I am afraid you will be angry.”  “My dearest sister, now be, _be_ serious. I want to talk very seriously. Let me know everything that I am to know without delay. Will you tell me how long you have loved him?”  “It has been coming on so gradually, that I hardly know when it began; but I believe I must date it from my first seeing his beautiful grounds at Pemberley.”  Another entreaty that she would be serious, however, produced the desired effect; and she soon satisfied Jane by her solemn assurances of attachment. When convinced on that article, Miss Bennet had nothing further to wish. “Now I am quite happy,” said she, “for you will be as happy as myself. I always had a value for him. Were it for nothing but his love of you, I must always have esteemed him; but now, as Bingley’s friend and your husband, there can be only Bingley and yourself more dear to me. But, Lizzy, you have been very sly, very reserved with me. How little did you tell me of what passed at Pemberley and Lambton! I owe all that I know of it to another, not to you.”  Elizabeth told her the motives of her secrecy. She had been unwilling to mention Bingley; and the unsettled state of her own feelings had made her equally avoid the name of his friend: but now she would no longer conceal from her his share in Lydia’s marriage. All was acknowledged, and half the night spent in conversation. “Good gracious!” cried Mrs. Bennet, as she stood at a window the next morning, “if that disagreeable Mr. Darcy is not coming here again with our dear Bingley! What can he mean by being so tiresome as to be always coming here? I had no notion but he would go a-shooting, or something or other, and not disturb us with his company. What shall we do with him? Lizzy, you must walk out with him again, that he may not be in Bingley’s way.”  Elizabeth could hardly help laughing at so convenient a proposal; yet was really vexed that her mother should be always giving him such an epithet. As soon as they entered, Bingley looked at her so expressively, and shook hands with such warmth, as left no doubt of his good information; and he soon afterwards said aloud, “Mrs. Bennet, have you no more lanes hereabouts in which Lizzy may lose her way again to-day?”  “I advise Mr. Darcy, and Lizzy, and Kitty,” said Mrs. Bennet, “to walk to Oakham Mount this morning. It is a nice long walk, and Mr. Darcy has never seen the view.”  “It may do very well for the others,” replied Mr. Bingley; “but I am sure it will be too much for Kitty. Won’t it, Kitty?”  Kitty owned that she had rather stay at home.

“I assure you, there is no mistake,” said Marianne. “I have never spoken with greater sincerity. He has declared himself, and I have accepted him.”
Her sister stared in astonishment. “You cannot expect me to believe it immediately. Only a short time ago, you could scarcely mention his name with patience.”
“That belongs entirely to the past,” replied Marianne. “If I judged him harshly before, I was mistaken. I see him differently now. Indeed, I think it would be unjust to dwell upon former opinions when everything has so completely changed.”
Still her sister seemed uncertain.
“You are serious?” she asked at last. “Perfectly serious?”
“Perfectly. I would not trifle with you on such a subject.”
“My dear Marianne!” she cried, taking her hand. “Then I wish you every happiness. Yet tell me honestly—are you certain of your own feelings? Do you esteem him enough? Do you love him as you ought?”
“I do. More than I imagined possible.”
“Then I am content,” said her sister. “Nothing else is necessary. But you have concealed this wonderfully well. Looking back, I can now understand many things that puzzled me, though at the time I suspected nothing.”
“There was little to tell at first,” said Marianne. “Even to myself, the change was gradual. I did not know my own heart until quite recently.”
They continued talking for some time, reviewing conversations and incidents that now appeared in a different light. What had once seemed insignificant acquired fresh meaning. By the end of their discussion, every doubt had disappeared.
The following morning, their mother observed from the drawing-room window that two gentlemen were approaching the house.
“There they are again!” she exclaimed. “I declare, they seem determined to spend every day here. Well, I cannot complain, for one of them is always welcome. But the other surprises me by his persistence.”
Marianne coloured slightly and turned away.
A few moments later the visitors entered. One addressed her sister with unusual warmth, while the other approached Marianne with a composure that concealed, though only imperfectly, his satisfaction.
Their mother, entirely unaware of the true state of affairs, soon proposed an excursion.
“The weather is delightful,” she said. “You should all walk to the hill beyond the meadow. The view is particularly fine this time of year.”
Her younger daughter immediately declined.
“I would rather remain at home,” she said.
“Then the rest of you must go without her,” replied their mother. “There is no reason everyone should lose the pleasure.”
The suggestion was received with more approval than she realized, and several exchanged glances that were not wholly accidental.

“There is nothing uncertain about it,” said Marianne. “I mean every word. He asked me to marry him, and I agreed.”
Her sister looked at her for a long moment.
“Marianne, I can hardly comprehend it. Not very long ago you spoke of him with so little favour.”
Marianne smiled.
“Then you must forget what I said before. I have done so myself. People change their opinions when they learn more of one another. At least, I have.”
The explanation did not entirely remove her sister’s surprise.
“You truly mean it?”
“Entirely.”
“My dear Marianne,” she said, squeezing her hand, “then I am happy for you. But allow me one question. Are you quite certain this attachment is strong enough to make you happy?”
“It is stronger than I ever expected it could be.”
That answer seemed to satisfy her.
“Then I have no further concerns. Still, you have been remarkably secretive. I see now that there were signs, but I never understood them.”
“I scarcely understood them myself,” Marianne admitted. “The change happened so gradually that I cannot point to any exact moment when it began.”
The conversation continued well into the evening. Together they revisited recent events, discovering meanings that had escaped them at the time. By the end, there was little left unexplained.
The next morning their mother happened to glance from the drawing-room window.
“Well!” she cried. “Here come our visitors again.”
She watched them approach the house with evident amusement.
“One might think they had no other destination. Not that I object. Their company is always agreeable enough.”
Marianne lowered her eyes, hoping no one would notice the colour rising in her cheeks.
When the gentlemen entered, greetings were exchanged all around. One spoke with particular warmth to her sister, while the other directed most of his attention toward Marianne, though with studied restraint.
Before long their mother proposed a walk.
“You should all go to the hill beyond the meadow,” she said. “The air is pleasant, and the view is worth seeing.”
Her younger daughter immediately shook her head.
“No, thank you. I would rather stay here.”
“Then stay,” said her mother. “The rest need not be deprived of an outing on your account.”
The proposal appeared perfectly innocent. Yet the quiet looks exchanged across the room suggested that some among the party found it especially welcome.

You must not be left without occupation, and so, work and a definite aim set before you might, I fancy, be very beneficial.”  “Yes, yes; you are perfectly right.... I will make haste and return to the university: and then everything will go smoothly....”  Zossimov, who had begun his sage advice partly to make an effect before the ladies, was certainly somewhat mystified, when, glancing at his patient, he observed unmistakable mockery on his face. This lasted an instant, however. Pulcheria Alexandrovna began at once thanking Zossimov, especially for his visit to their lodging the previous night. “What! he saw you last night?” Raskolnikov asked, as though startled. “Then you have not slept either after your journey.”  “Ach, Rodya, that was only till two o’clock. Dounia and I never go to bed before two at home.”  “I don’t know how to thank him either,” Raskolnikov went on, suddenly frowning and looking down. “Setting aside the question of payment--forgive me for referring to it (he turned to Zossimov)--I really don’t know what I have done to deserve such special attention from you! I simply don’t understand it... and... and... it weighs upon me, indeed, because I don’t understand it. I tell you so candidly.”  “Don’t be irritated.” Zossimov forced himself to laugh. “Assume that you are my first patient--well--we fellows just beginning to practise love our first patients as if they were our children, and some almost fall in love with them. And, of course, I am not rich in patients.”  “I say nothing about him,” added Raskolnikov, pointing to Razumihin, “though he has had nothing from me either but insult and trouble.”  “What nonsense he is talking! Why, you are in a sentimental mood to-day, are you?” shouted Razumihin. If he had had more penetration he would have seen that there was no trace of sentimentality in him, but something indeed quite the opposite. But Avdotya Romanovna noticed it. She was intently and uneasily watching her brother. “As for you, mother, I don’t dare to speak,” he went on, as though repeating a lesson learned by heart. “It is only to-day that I have been able to realise a little how distressed you must have been here yesterday, waiting for me to come back.”  When he had said this, he suddenly held out his hand to his sister, smiling without a word. But in this smile there was a flash of real unfeigned feeling. Dounia caught it at once, and warmly pressed his hand, overjoyed and thankful. It was the first time he had addressed her since their dispute the previous day. The mother’s face lighted up with ecstatic happiness at the sight of this conclusive unspoken reconciliation. “Yes, that is what I love him for,” Razumihin, exaggerating it all, muttered to himself, with a vigorous turn in his chair. “He has these movements.”  “And how well he does it all,” the mother was thinking to herself. “What generous impulses he has, and how simply, how delicately he put an end to all the misunderstanding with his sister--simply by holding out his hand at the right minute and looking at her like that.... And what fine eyes he has, and how fine his whole face is!... He is even better looking than Dounia.... But, good heavens, what a suit--how terribly he’s dressed!... Vasya, the messenger boy in Afanasy Ivanitch’s shop, is better dressed! I could rush at him and hug him... weep over him--but I am afraid.... Oh, dear, he’s so strange! He’s talking kindly, but I’m afraid! Why, what am I afraid of?...”  “Oh, Rodya, you wouldn’t believe,” she began suddenly, in haste to answer his words to her, “how unhappy Dounia and I were yesterday! Now that it’s all over and done with and we are quite happy again--I can tell you. Fancy, we ran here almost straight from the train to embrace you and that woman--ah, here she is! Good morning, Nastasya!... She told us at once that you were lying in a high fever and had just run away from the doctor in delirium, and they were looking for you in the streets. You can’t imagine how we felt! I couldn’t help thinking of the tragic end of Lieutenant Potanchikov, a friend of your father’s--you can’t remember him, Rodya--who ran out in the same way in a high fever and fell into the well in the court-yard and they couldn’t pull him out till next day.

“You must not remain idle,” said the doctor. “A settled routine, some occupation, a purpose that carries you forward—those things would do you more good than any medicine I could prescribe.”
“Yes, perhaps you are right,” replied Nikolai after a pause. “I shall return to my studies soon. Once I am occupied again, everything may become simpler.”
The doctor nodded with satisfaction, though he was secretly disappointed. He had expected gratitude, perhaps even admiration for his advice. Instead, when he looked closely at his patient, he thought he detected a trace of irony in his expression. The impression vanished almost immediately.
His mother hastened to fill the silence.
“We owe you so much already,” she said warmly. “Especially after your kindness in visiting us last night.”
“You saw them last night?” Nikolai asked, turning toward the doctor. “Then you sacrificed your rest as well.”
“It was nothing of consequence,” said the doctor. “I was awake regardless.”
Nikolai lowered his eyes.
“To tell the truth, I do not understand why everyone troubles themselves on my account. You have all shown me more concern than I deserve. I ought to be grateful, yet I find myself embarrassed by it. It is difficult to accept what I cannot explain.”
“You are overthinking the matter,” said the doctor with a laugh. “Young physicians become attached to their early patients. We imagine ourselves responsible for them. Besides, I have not yet the luxury of being overwhelmed with work.”
“And he,” Nikolai added, indicating his friend, “has received nothing in return for his efforts except inconvenience.”
“What absurdity!” his friend exclaimed. “You speak as though we are strangers.”
His sister, however, watched him carefully. There was something in his manner that unsettled her. His words were kind, but they seemed rehearsed, as if he had prepared them beforehand.
He turned toward his mother.
“As for you, I only realized today how much anxiety I caused. You must have suffered greatly yesterday.”
Then, unexpectedly, he extended his hand toward his sister.
The gesture was simple, but the feeling behind it appeared genuine. She grasped his hand immediately. Relief brightened her face. Their disagreement of the previous day seemed suddenly less important.
The mother's eyes filled with tears.
“There,” she thought, “that is the son I know. Whatever troubles him, his heart remains good.”
Her gaze lingered on him. He looked exhausted. His clothes were worn, his appearance neglected. Yet to her he remained dearer than anyone in the world.
Suddenly she began speaking again.
“You cannot imagine how frightened we were yesterday. We arrived full of hope and were told at once that you had left in a feverish state. They were searching the streets for you. I thought of every terrible possibility. I scarcely knew what to do.”
The memory still shook her.
“For hours I could think of nothing else. Every story of illness or misfortune returned to me. I feared we might lose you before we had even seen you properly again.”
The room fell silent. No one doubted the sincerity of her distress.

“You need something to occupy you,” said the doctor. “Work, study, any clear purpose. A man recovers faster when he has somewhere to direct his thoughts.”
Nikolai gave a faint nod.
“Yes. I suppose that is true. I will return to the university before long. Perhaps once I fall back into a routine, things will seem less complicated.”
The doctor appeared pleased by the answer, though only for a moment. When he glanced at his patient, he thought he caught a hint of amusement—or perhaps mockery—in his face. It disappeared so quickly that he could not be certain.
His mother intervened before the silence became awkward.
“We are deeply grateful to you,” she said. “Especially for calling on us last evening.”
Nikolai looked up.
“You visited them last night? Then you have not had much rest yourself.”
“It was no great sacrifice,” the doctor replied lightly.
Nikolai frowned and looked down at his hands.
“What puzzles me is why everyone concerns themselves so much with me. I know I ought to be thankful. Still, I cannot quite understand it. The kindness itself becomes a burden when I feel I have done nothing to earn it.”
The doctor laughed.
“There is no mystery. Young doctors become attached to their first patients. We take an unreasonable interest in them. And, to be honest, I am not so busy that I can afford indifference.”
Nikolai gestured toward his friend.
“And he has endured even more. Trouble, inconvenience, endless demands—nothing else.”
“Nonsense,” his friend said at once. “You speak as though I have been performing a great service.”
His sister remained quiet. She had been watching him all this time. There was warmth in what he said, yet something restrained beneath it, something she could not quite name.
After a moment he turned to his mother.
“I understand now how worried you must have been yesterday. At least a little. I did not think of it before.”
Then he reached out his hand to his sister.
Nothing dramatic accompanied the gesture, but it was enough. She took his hand immediately, her face brightening with relief. The quarrel that had stood between them seemed to dissolve without another word.
Their mother could hardly contain her happiness.
For a few moments she simply looked at him. His clothes were shabby, his face drawn from fatigue, and yet she found herself thinking how dear he was to her. No outward disorder could alter that.
The thought stirred her into speech once more.
“You have no idea what yesterday was like for us. We arrived eager to see you and learned almost at once that you had gone out while ill. They said you were wandering about in a fever. People were searching everywhere.”
She paused, remembering.
“I imagined every possible disaster. The longer you were gone, the worse my fears became. I do not think I have ever passed such anxious hours.”
No one interrupted her. The depth of her distress was evident, and the room grew quiet as she spoke.

My journey had been my own suggestion, and Elizabeth therefore acquiesced, but she was filled with disquiet at the idea of my suffering, away from her, the inroads of misery and grief. It had been her care which provided me a companion in Clerval—and yet a man is blind to a thousand minute circumstances which call forth a woman’s sedulous attention. She longed to bid me hasten my return; a thousand conflicting emotions rendered her mute as she bade me a tearful, silent farewell. I threw myself into the carriage that was to convey me away, hardly knowing whither I was going, and careless of what was passing around. I remembered only, and it was with a bitter anguish that I reflected on it, to order that my chemical instruments should be packed to go with me. Filled with dreary imaginations, I passed through many beautiful and majestic scenes, but my eyes were fixed and unobserving. I could only think of the bourne of my travels and the work which was to occupy me whilst they endured. After some days spent in listless indolence, during which I traversed many leagues, I arrived at Strasburgh, where I waited two days for Clerval. He came. Alas, how great was the contrast between us! He was alive to every new scene, joyful when he saw the beauties of the setting sun, and more happy when he beheld it rise and recommence a new day. He pointed out to me the shifting colours of the landscape and the appearances of the sky. “This is what it is to live,” he cried; “now I enjoy existence! But you, my dear Frankenstein, wherefore are you desponding and sorrowful!” In truth, I was occupied by gloomy thoughts and neither saw the descent of the evening star nor the golden sunrise reflected in the Rhine. And you, my friend, would be far more amused with the journal of Clerval, who observed the scenery with an eye of feeling and delight, than in listening to my reflections. I, a miserable wretch, haunted by a curse that shut up every avenue to enjoyment. We had agreed to descend the Rhine in a boat from Strasburgh to Rotterdam, whence we might take shipping for London. During this voyage we passed many willowy islands and saw several beautiful towns. We stayed a day at Mannheim, and on the fifth from our departure from Strasburgh, arrived at Mainz. The course of the Rhine below Mainz becomes much more picturesque. The river descends rapidly and winds between hills, not high, but steep, and of beautiful forms. We saw many ruined castles standing on the edges of precipices, surrounded by black woods, high and inaccessible. This part of the Rhine, indeed, presents a singularly variegated landscape. In one spot you view rugged hills, ruined castles overlooking tremendous precipices, with the dark Rhine rushing beneath; and on the sudden turn of a promontory, flourishing vineyards with green sloping banks and a meandering river and populous towns occupy the scene. We travelled at the time of the vintage and heard the song of the labourers as we glided down the stream. Even I, depressed in mind, and my spirits continually agitated by gloomy feelings, even I was pleased. I lay at the bottom of the boat, and as I gazed on the cloudless blue sky, I seemed to drink in a tranquillity to which I had long been a stranger. And if these were my sensations, who can describe those of Henry? He felt as if he had been transported to Fairy-land and enjoyed a happiness seldom tasted by man. “I have seen,” he said, “the most beautiful scenes of my own country; I have visited the lakes of Lucerne and Uri, where the snowy mountains descend almost perpendicularly to the water, casting black and impenetrable shades, which would cause a gloomy and mournful appearance were it not for the most verdant islands that relieve the eye by their gay appearance; I have seen this lake agitated by a tempest, when the wind tore up whirlwinds of water and gave you an idea of what the water-spout must be on the great ocean; and the waves dash with fury the base of the mountain, where the priest and his mistress were overwhelmed by an avalanche and where their dying voices are still said to be heard amid the pauses of the nightly wind; I have seen the mountains of La Valais, and the Pays de Vaud; but this country, Victor, pleases me more than all those wonders.

The decision to travel had been mine alone, though Clara consented to it with visible reluctance. She feared less for the hardships of the road than for the solitude into which I willingly cast myself. Her concern had procured for me the companionship of Ashton, whose cheerful temper she believed might draw me from my habitual gloom. Yet no kindness could wholly overcome the burden that oppressed my thoughts.
When at last I departed, she stood silent beside the gate, her composure maintained only by effort. I attempted words of reassurance, but they died before reaching my lips. The carriage bore me away through valleys bright with summer light, across hills clothed in forests, and beside rivers that reflected the clear sky. These scenes passed before me almost unnoticed. My mind remained fixed upon the object of my journey and the unfinished labor that awaited me.
Several days later I arrived at a small city upon the frontier and there remained until Ashton joined me. The contrast between us was striking. Every novelty delighted him. He spoke with enthusiasm of distant towers, crowded marketplaces, and the varied character of the people we encountered. Where I saw only passing forms, he discovered endless occasions for admiration.
“You observe nothing,” he said one evening as we walked beside the river. “The world spreads its treasures before you, yet you move among them as though in a dream.”
I could not deny the justice of his remark. The beauty that animated him seemed unable to penetrate the cloud that surrounded me. Nevertheless, I listened while he described the changing colors of the sunset and the reflections trembling upon the water. His pleasure was sincere and infectious, and at moments I almost believed myself capable of sharing it.
We continued our route by boat, allowing the current to carry us through a succession of fertile districts. Vineyards descended the hillsides in ordered rows. Ancient fortresses crowned rocky heights and overlooked the winding river below. In some places the landscape appeared severe and lonely; in others it opened suddenly into broad fields dotted with villages and church spires.
The season of harvest had begun. Along the banks we heard laborers singing as they worked, their voices carried faintly across the water. Children ran among the orchards, and carts laden with fruit moved slowly along the roads. Such signs of ordinary happiness awakened in me a feeling I had not experienced for many months. Though my anxieties remained, they seemed less absolute than before.
One afternoon, as our vessel drifted beneath a range of wooded cliffs, I reclined against the railing and watched clouds gathering in the western sky. The air grew cooler, and a gentle wind stirred the surface of the river. For a brief interval my thoughts loosened their hold upon me. I ceased to dwell upon the future and surrendered myself to the quiet motion of the voyage.
Ashton, meanwhile, appeared enchanted by everything around him. He spoke of distant countries he hoped to visit and of the many wonders still hidden beyond the horizon. His imagination transformed every scene into the beginning of some greater adventure. Observing his delight, I could not help envying the ease with which he embraced the world.
“Perhaps,” he said as evening descended, “the greatest privilege is not to discover new lands, but to retain the ability to wonder at them.”
The remark lingered with me long after the light had faded and the river had vanished into darkness.

The journey was my own idea, though Clara agreed to it reluctantly. What troubled her was not the travel itself but the thought of my being left alone with my thoughts. She had arranged for Ashton to accompany me, hoping his good spirits would keep me from sinking further into melancholy. It was a thoughtful plan, though at the time I doubted anyone could lighten the mood in which I found myself.
On the morning of my departure she tried to appear calm. A few words passed between us, neither of us saying quite what we meant. Then I was gone. The road led through pleasant country—wooded hills, bright rivers, fields warmed by the sun—but I paid little attention to any of it. My mind was occupied elsewhere, fixed on the purpose that had drawn me from home.
I waited several days at a frontier town before Ashton arrived. From the moment we resumed our travels together, the difference between us became impossible to ignore. Everything interested him. A marketplace, a ruined tower, even a crowded ferry landing could hold his attention for an hour. He had a way of finding something worth admiring wherever he looked.
One evening, while we walked beside the river, he laughed and told me I passed through the world half asleep.
“There is beauty everywhere,” he said. “You look directly at it and never seem to see it.”
The criticism was fair. I had become so accustomed to my own worries that I scarcely noticed what surrounded me. Still, as he pointed out the fading colors in the sky and the reflections scattered across the water, I found myself listening with unexpected interest.
We traveled onward by boat. The river wound through fertile country where vineyards climbed the slopes and old stone fortresses stood watch above the banks. Sometimes the scenery was wild and austere, marked by cliffs and dark forests. A bend in the river would reveal an entirely different prospect—open fields, villages, and church spires rising from among clusters of trees.
Harvest season was underway. Songs drifted from the fields. We saw wagons heavy with fruit and workers gathering crops beneath the late afternoon sun. There was something reassuring in these ordinary scenes. For the first time in many months, I felt a faint easing of the pressure that had weighed upon me.
One afternoon clouds gathered over the western hills as we floated beneath a line of wooded heights. The breeze freshened, rippling the surface of the river. Leaning against the railing, I watched the changing light and, for a little while, forgot to dwell on what lay ahead. The relief was brief, but it was real.
Ashton remained delighted by everything. Every town suggested a story, every distant road another destination. He spoke eagerly of future travels and places he hoped one day to see. Listening to him, I envied the openness with which he met the world.
As darkness settled over the water, he made a remark that stayed with me.
“People spend their lives searching for wonders,” he said. “The rarer gift may be never losing the ability to be surprised by them.”
The river disappeared into the night, but those words remained long after the last light had gone.
